title: D 2020-104
full_text:
D 2020 104
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Order D 2020 068 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain
 
Eligibility for Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 068 temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans 
enrolled in Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, and D 2020 076. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has 
undertaken a broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the
 
Medicaid program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage
 
(FMAP) if they maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after 
March 18, 2020.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, which ensures 
compliance with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending
 
certain statutes to allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those individuals 
enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 2020 and to prevent any changes in eligibility, and 
directs the Executive Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing 

 
Executive Order D 2020 104 
June 14, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                     
 
               
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
(HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s 
Basic Health Plan.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire
 
thirty (30) days from June 14 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other
 
respects, Executive Order D 2020 068 shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
 
fourteenth day of June, 2020
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-105
full_text:
a 
 
 
 
 
 
D 2020 105
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 010, D 2020 040, and D 2020 069 Concerning the 
Suspension of Statute to Extend the Income Tax Payment Deadlines Due to the 
COVID-19 Disaster Emergency
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending
 
Executive Order D 2020 010, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 040 
and extended by Executive Order D 2020 069, ordering the suspension of statute to extend 
certain state income tax payment deadlines for all Colorado taxpayers to quickly provide 
relief from payment and penalties due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 
2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D
 
2020 076. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a
 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President 
approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination 
posed by COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and 
employees in virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action 
to shore up economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community 
recovery. The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) has extended federal tax filing deadlines to 
July 15, 2020, and through Executive Order D 2020 010, as amended and extended, the 
State also extended state tax filing deadlines to July 15, 2020.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 105 
June 15, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
Governor 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 010, as amended and 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 040 and extended by Executive Order D 2020 069, 
for an additional thirty (30) days from June 15, 2020 to align with the IRS extension and 
extend certain state income tax payment deadlines for all Colorado taxpayers.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 010, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 
040 and extended by Executive Order D 2020 069 and this Executive Order, shall expire 
thirty (30) days from June 16, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 010, as amended and extended by Executive Order 
D 2020 040 and extended by Executive Order D 2020 069, shall remain in full force and 
effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
fifteenth day of June, 2020.
 
Jared Polis

title: D 2020-106
full_text:
D 2020 106
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Activating the Colorado National Guard to Assist with
 
Election Cybersecurity Defense Efforts
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular. pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
et seq., I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order 
activating up to six (6) members of the Colorado National Guard Defensive Cyber Operations 
Element to assist with election cybersecurity defense efforts during the 2020 state primary
 
election.
 
I.
 Background and Purpose
 
Like many states, Colorado uses online voter registration systems that allow eligible 
citizens to access and update their voter registration information. These systems are protected 
by various security features that maintain confidentiality and prevent data tampering or 
interference with data.
 
Even with such security features, online voter registration systems could provide an 
avenue for cyber actors to gain unlawful access to voter registration databases. Outside of
 
Colorado, voter registration databases have been compromised by cyber actors hacking into 
various systems. While cyber actors are unable to modify voter records, breaches could result in 
the release of voters’ personally identifiable information. The exposure of voters’ personally 
identifiable information does not threaten the integrity of our State elections, but could 
undermine public confidence in the system and suppress voter registration. The Colorado 
National Guard Defensive Cyber Operations Element has unique cyber capabilities to assist in 
protecting critical election infrastructure.
 
The Colorado Secretary of State has requested that the Colorado National Guard 
Defensive Cyber Operations Element provide election cybersecurity support during the 2020 
state primary election. By activating up to six (6) members of the National Guard Defensive 
Cyber Operations Element, the National Guard will support the Secretary of State with election 
defense efforts up to and including the 2020 state primary election, which is on June 30, 2020.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 106 
June 16, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Directives
 
On June 8, 2020, the Colorado Secretary of State requested support from the Colorado
 
National Guard Defensive Cyber Operations Element for the 2020 state primary election. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, the Governor may employ the National Guard for the defense of 
the State. I hereby activate up to six (6) members of the Colorado National Guard Defensive 
Cyber Operations Element to assist with election cybersecurity defense efforts during the 2020 
state primary election.
 
III.
 Duration
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from June 16, 2020, unless extended
 
further by Executive Order.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
sixteenth day of June, 2020.
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-107
full_text:

EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Order D 2020 071 Concerning the Suspension of Statutes to 
Allow the Operation of Alternate Care Sites in Colorado Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in
 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared
 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending
 
Executive Order D 2020 071 ordering the suspension of certain statutes to allow the 
operation of alternate care sites in Colorado due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19).
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally 
declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado pursuant 
to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has 
undertaken a wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 071 for an additional thirty 
(30) days from June 18, 2020, to ensure health care facilities have sufficient resources and 
to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-19 patients who no longer require inpatient
 
hospitalization but still require some medical care and observation while recovering from
 
COVID-19.

June 18, 2020

. 
II.
 Duration

Jared Polis 
Governor

full_text:

Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 077 temporarily suspending certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health 
workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing and 
treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, and D 2020 076. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has 
undertaken a broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.

title: D 2020-109
full_text:
- 
 
 
 
  
D 2020 109
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the 
Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076 
declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for response activities due 
to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent
 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24
 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from
 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and this
 
Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare 
a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 

Executive Order D 2020 109 
June 20, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date have 
made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize the 
duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives.
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, and D 2020 076 to extend the state of disaster emergency and the deployment of the 
Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State and local authorities 
as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State. Additionally, this Executive Order
 
amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076 to 
increase the amount of funds previously made available to CDPHE by twelve million six
 
hundred thousand dollars ($12,600,000), and increase the amount of funds previously made 
available to the Colorado Department of Public Safety by six million seven hundred thousand 
dollars ($6,700,000), for COVID-19 response activities. The funds made available for response 
activities in this Executive Order were transferred to the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of 
the Disaster Emergency Fund by Executive Order D 2020 070. Executive Order D 2020 070 
transferred forty eight million dollars ($48,000,000) in FY 2019-20 and one hundred and fifty 
seven million dollars ($157,000,000) at the beginning of FY 2020-21 from the CARES Act Fund 
to the Disaster Emergency Fund Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account. 
II. 
Amendment
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 
018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076, is amended and restated as follows:
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and twenty-three million dollars ($23,000,000) 
was transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund. Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that one hundred twenty-four million
 
eight hundred thousand
 
dollars ($124,800,000) from the Disaster Emergency
 
Fund be
 
encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these funds, thirty-six million
 
eight hundred thousand dollars ($36,800,000) shall come from the Coronavirus Relief
 
Fund sub-account of the Disaster
 
Emergency Fund. Of the funds coming from
 
the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, twenty-nine million six hundred thousand dollars 
($29,600,000) shall be encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities 
related to COVID-19. These funds shall remain available for expenses incurred from
 
March 1, 2020 to December 30, 2020, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the 
Disaster Emergency Fund.
 
III. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from June 19, 2020, unless amended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described 

  
Executive Order D 2020 109 
June 20, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
in Section II.C shall remain available for the described purposes and the described time period 
stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, and D 2020 076, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
twentieth day of June, 2020
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-110
full_text:
-
 
D 2020 110
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending, Restating, and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, and D
 
2020 092 Ordering Certain Workers to Wear Non-Medical Face Coverings
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, and D 2020 092 ordering certain workers to wear 
non-medical face covering and providing discretion to certain employers to deny admittance to 
individuals who fail to wear a medical or non-medical face covering due to the presence of 
coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, and D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D
 
2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major
 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’
 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID infections. As we 
take steps to return Coloradans to work, we must continue to take measures to facilitate 
reopening the economy while protecting public health by taking steps to incorporate best
 
practices to protect State and county employees from infection.
 
This Executive Order amends, restates, and extends Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 
2020 067, and D 2020 092 to protect Coloradans by requiring certain employees to wear a non
 
medical mask covering their nose and mouth while at work and while serving the public. This 
Executive Order also protects our State and county employees and the individuals they serve
 

 Executive Order D 2020 110 
June 20, 2020 
Page 2 of 3
 
  
from infection by ensuring that masks are worn within Government Offices and Facilities, and
 
provides discretion to certain employers to deny admittance to individuals who fail to wear a 
medical or non-medical face covering.
 
II.
 Directives
 
A.
 I direct the Executive Director of CDPHE to issue a public health order requiring
 
that employees, contractors, and others providing services for Mass
 
Transportation Operations and Critical Businesses where employees, contractors,
 
or others who interact in close proximity with other employees or with the public
 
must:
 
1.
 Wear medical or non-medical cloth face coverings that cover the nose and mouth
 
while working, except where doing so would inhibit that individual’s health; and
 
2.
 Wear gloves, as appropriate by industry standards, when in physical contact with
 
customers or goods if gloves are provided to workers by their employer.
 
B.
 I direct that to better protect employees of Government Offices and Facilities and
 
the public that they serve and to avoid a public health hazard, Government Offices
 
and Facilities must require and enforce, as a condition of ingress or movement
 
within such locations, that all employees who interact in close proximity with
 
other employees or with the public and all members of the public must wear
 
medical or non-medical face coverings that cover the nose and mouth, except
 
where doing so would inhibit that individual’s health or where law enforcement or
 
physical identification verification functions would be inhibited by wearing a
 
mask.
 
C.
 Nothing in this Executive Order should be construed to prevent employees or
 
persons entering Mass Transportation Operations, Government Offices and
 
Facilities, or Critical Businesses from wearing a surgical-grade mask or other,
 
more protective face covering to cover the nose and mouth if that type of mask or
 
more protective face covering is appropriate under industry standards.
 
D.
 Employers and operators of Places of Public Accommodation, Mass
 
Transportation Operations, and Critical Businesses, and those authorized on their
 
behalf, shall have discretion to deny admittance or service and require the
 
removal of any individual who fails to wear a medical or non-medical face
 
covering.
 
E.
 
These directives shall be applied in a manner consistent with the American with
 
Disabilities Act (42 U.S.C. § 12101 et seq.), Title VII of the Civil Rights Act (42
 
U.S.C. § 2000e et seq.), the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act (C.R.S. § 24-34-
 
401 et seq.), and any other relevant federal or State law.
 

 
 Executive Order D 2020 110 
June 20, 2020 
Page 3 of 3
  
 
 
 
F.
 
Mass Transportation Operations, Government Offices and Facilities, Critical
 
Businesses, and Places of Public Accommodation have the meaning as defined in
 
PHO 20-28, as amended, or any PHO issued pursuant to this Executive Order.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 067 and as
 
amended and extended Executive Order D 2020 092, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 20, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067 and D 2020 092, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
twentieth day of June, 2020.
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-111
full_text:
D 2020 111 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, and D 2020 075 Authorizing the 
Executive Directors of Certain State Agencies to Promulgate and Issue Emergency Rules 
Extending the Expiration Date of Licenses and Other Documents Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, and D 2020 075 authorizing the executive directors of certain 
State agencies to promulgate and issue emergency rules extending the expiration date of licenses 
and other documents for thirty (30) additional days due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, and D 2020 058, and D 2020 076. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 

  
Executive Order D 2020 111 
June 20, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
                  
 
         
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This Executive Order extends the expiration date of Executive Order D 2020 015, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042 and D 2020 075, by an additional thirty 
(30) days. 
II. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, 
D 2020 075, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 20, 2020, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 015, as 
amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 042 and D 2020 75, shall remain in full force 
and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twentieth day of June, 2020. 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-112
full_text:
D 2020 112 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Portions of Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, and D 2020 078 
Temporarily Suspending Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Criminal Justice 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending portions of 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043 and D 
2020 078, which temporarily suspend certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice due 
to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, and D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 
2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection. On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions 

 
Executive Order D 2020 112 
June 20, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
                  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
J
Governor 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order D 2020 
016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive Orders 
D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire. 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days, 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance. 
II. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043 
and D 2020 078 and extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 
20, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043 and D 2020 078, shall 
remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twentieth day of June, 2020. 
 
 
 
 
 
ared Polis

title: D 2020-113
full_text:
D 2020 113
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Rescinding Executive Order D 2020 050 Declaring Insufficient Revenues Available for 
Expenditures and Ordering Suspension or Discontinuation of Portions of Certain State 
Programs and Services to Meet a Revenue Shortfall Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in 
the State of Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order rescinding Executive Order 
D 2020 050 because House Bill 2020-1360 (HB20-1360) replicated Executive Order D 2020 
050’s suspension or discontinuation of two hundred twenty-eight million six hundred eighty-five 
thousand nine hundred fifteen dollars ($228,685,915) in government programs through the end 
of fiscal year (FY) 2019-20.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D
 
2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
COVID-19 presents the largest public health emergency in Colorado, the country, and the 
world in a generation. The health and economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant. The risk 
of contamination posed by COVID-19 has necessitated closure or limited operation of many 
businesses. As a result, unemployment has increased and social distancing policies in Colorado 
and across the country have led to a decline in consumer spending. As the State continues to 
respond to the COVID-19 pandemic, it has become increasingly evident that the prolonged 
period of economic disruption associated with COVID-19 is causing precipitous and significant 
revenue shortfalls for the State.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 113 
June 23, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
  
 
 
On April 30, 2020, I signed Executive Order D 2020 050, which suspended or
 
discontinued two hundred twenty-eight million six hundred eighty-five thousand nine hundred 
fifteen dollars ($228,685,915) in government programs through the end of FY 2019-20. On June 
12, 2020, the General Assembly passed HB20-1360, which included an addendum to the FY 
2019-20 budget that replicated the cuts I ordered in Executive Order D 2020 050. Yesterday, I 
signed HB20-1360 and Executive Order D 2020 050 is no longer necessary and is rescinded.
 
II.
 Directive
 
I rescind Executive Order D 2020 050 because HB20-1360 replicated Executive Order D 
2020 050’s suspension or discontinuation of two hundred twenty-eight million six hundred 
eighty-five thousand nine hundred fifteen dollars ($228,685,915) in government programs 
through the end of FY 2019-2020.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this twenty 
third day of June, 2020.
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-114
full_text:
D 2020 114
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 045 and D 2020 080 Permitting the Limited 
Recommencement of Voluntary or Elective Surgeries and Procedures in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 045 and D 2020 080 for an additional thirty (30) days to allow voluntary or 
elective surgeries and procedures to proceed under certain conditions. 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the 
collateral consequences of the pandemic. 
On March 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 009, which ordered the temporary 
cessation of all voluntary or elective surgeries and procedures and preservation of personal 
protective equipment (PPE) and ventilators in Colorado due to the presence of COVID-19. On 
April 6, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 027 that amended and extended Executive Order 
D 2020 009. On April 26, 2020, Executive Order D 2020 009, as amended and extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 027, expired, and I issued Executive Order D 2020 045 to permit 
voluntary or elective surgeries and procedures under certain conditions that minimize the risk of 
COVID-19 transmission to patients, healthcare workers, community members, and others by 

 
Executive Order D 2020 114 
June 24, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
  
 
 
promoting safety and maximizing protection. On May 26, 2020, I extended Executive Order D 
2020 045 for an additional thirty (30) days in Executive Order D 2020 080.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 045, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2020 080, for an additional thirty (30) days from June 24, 2020 to allow voluntary or 
elective surgeries and procedures under certain conditions.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 045, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 080 and this
 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 24, 2020, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 045, as extended by D 2020 080, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-fourth day of June, 
2020.
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-115
full_text:
D 2020 115
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Designating State’s Prosecutor
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, C.R.S. § 24-31-101(1)(a), and subsequent authority effective with the enactment of 
C.R.S. § 24-31-101(1)(b) (2020) as amended by Senate Bill 20-063, I, Jared Polis, Governor of
 
the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order designating Phil Weiser, Attorney
 
General for the State of Colorado, as the State’s prosecutor to investigate and, if the facts support
 
prosecution, criminally prosecute any individuals whose actions caused the death of Elijah
 
McClain in the City of Aurora, Adams County, Colorado in August 2019.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On August 24, 2019, three Aurora police officers responded to a 911 report of a
 
“suspicious” person and confronted Elijah McClain, a twenty-three year old Black man.
 
In the 
encounter with the officers, Mr. McClain was ultimately placed in a carotid control hold for a
 
period of time.
 
A carotid control hold is designed to subdue an individual by applying pressure 
to the side of the neck to obstruct the carotid arteries and prevent blood flow to the
 
brain.
 
Members of Aurora Fire Rescue later arrived on the scene, and a fire medic injected Mr. 
McClain with ketamine.
 
Mr. McClain was placed into an ambulance where he stopped breathing 
and lost his pulse.
 
On the way to the hospital, Mr. McClain suffered cardiac arrest.
 
On August
 
30, 2019, he was removed from life support.
 
In November 2019, the District Attorney in the 17th Judicial District declined to pursue 
charges against the three officers involved in the incident.
 
Earlier this year, an excessive force 
review board with the Aurora Police Department found that the actions taken by the officers
 
were within policy and consistent with their training.
 
The City of Aurora initiated a separate 
review of the incident, but recently removed the investigator in the case due to charges of bias
 
because of the investigator’s prior employment as a police officer.
 
The Mayor of Aurora 
subsequently issued a statement saying the City Council and Mayor are working together to 
launch a new independent review of the events surrounding Mr. McClain’s death.
 
The City of 
Aurora’s investigation may form an important part of the record to inform prosecutorial 
decisions.
 
This is a tragic and complex case that warrants close attention.
 
Public confidence in the 
outcome of an investigation is extremely important.
 
This is especially true when any Coloradan 
dies during an interaction with law enforcement, and when we must determine whether the facts 
justify criminal charges against members of law enforcement.
 
I am keenly aware that an elected 
district attorney issued a letter explaining his decision not to bring charges in this matter.
 
That 

Executive Order D 2020 115 
June 25, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
  
  
  
 
   
 
 
               
  
  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
letter lays out a detailed review of the facts of this case and an in-depth discussion of the legal 
standard.
 
It does not, however, account for some significant facts that have been widely 
reported, particularly relating to body camera footage and recordings of the incident.
 
The State 
of Colorado just enacted a law requiring use of body cameras by all law enforcement, in part to 
prevent situations like this where the complete circumstances of a deadly encounter cannot be 
fully understood, either because the officers involved did not have body cameras or the body 
cameras at the scene were obstructed.
 
The family affected by this tragedy and the public must
 
have confidence that every aspect of this case has been properly examined.
 
The Governor therefore finds that this case is a matter of statewide importance and it is 
necessary for the Attorney General to act as the State’s prosecutor, investigate, and—if the 
evidence permits—prosecute potential criminal activity by individuals that caused the death of 
Elijah McClain.
 
I respect the solemn duty of the State’s district attorneys to investigate and, at their 
judgment and discretion, bring charges for the commission of crimes.
 
The State rarely steps in 
to investigate, and potentially prosecute, an incident over the individual decisions of district 
attorneys.
 
This, however, is the truly exceptional case where widely reported facts are not 
addressed in any current investigation.
 
These omissions merit a supplemental evaluation of the 
case by an independent prosecutor and thus warrant this Executive Order.
 
II.
 Directives
 
A. 
I appoint Phil Weiser, Attorney General for the State of Colorado, or his duly 
authorized successor, to be the State’s prosecutor and direct him to take all
 
necessary actions to investigate and, if deemed necessary, prosecute, on behalf of
 
the State of Colorado, any potential criminal activity by law enforcement officers
 
or any other individuals that caused the death of Elijah McClain in Aurora, 
Colorado in August 2019.
 
The Attorney General may utilize a State grand jury 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 13-73-101(2) if and as he deems appropriate, and may 
appoint special assistant attorneys general, and utilize other resources to assist in 
the fulfillment of this Executive Order.
 
To the greatest extent possible, the 
Attorney General may work with the City of Aurora on any independent
 
investigation it undertakes into the death of Elijah McClain to ensure 
accountability and learn critical lessons so that we can prevent these tragedies in 
the future.
 
B. 
I direct the Colorado Bureau of Investigation to fully cooperate with and assist the
 
Attorney General and his designees to effectuate this Executive Order, including 
providing any investigation support required by and requested from the Attorney 
General or his designees.
 
C. 
I direct all other State departments and agencies to fully cooperate with the 
Attorney General and his designees to effectuate this Executive Order, including 
the provision of any evidence, documents, records, or other information deemed 
relevant to the investigation by the Attorney General or his designees.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 115 
June 25, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
   
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
D. 
Due to significant funding and staff reductions to the Department of Law 
Criminal Justice line item for the 2020-2021 fiscal year caused by ongoing State 
budget shortfalls, I urge the General Assembly and Joint Budget Committee to 
consider and approve a supplemental appropriations request pursuant to C.R.S. § 
2-3-208 to support the fulfillment of this Executive Order, if such request is 
submitted by the Attorney General to the Joint Budget Committee.
 
III.
 Duration
 
This appointment and this Executive Order shall expire upon completion of the Attorney 
General’s investigation and any prosecution related to the matters herein, unless modified or
 
terminated by further Executive Order of the Governor.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-fifth
 
 
day of June, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-116
full_text:
D 2020
 
116
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 020, D 2020 046, and D 2020 082 Concerning the 
Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes to Expand the Use of Telehealth Services Due to 
the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 020, D 2020 046, and D 2020 082 for thirty (30) days, concerning the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes to expand the use of telehealth services due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 
109.
 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major
 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that
 
request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
COVID-19 is a highly contagious viral disease that has spread throughout many Colorado 
communities, across the country, and across the world. To protect the public health and mitigate 
exposure to and the spread of COVID-19, Colorado health care providers, including 
veterinarians, must be able to use telehealth services whenever possible. These services, 
including low-cost telephone, internet, audio-only, and live video, are widely available and 
accessible to health care providers and patients.
 
On April 1, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 020 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes to extend the use of telehealth services due to the presence of 
COVID-19 in Colorado.
 
On April 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 046, extending 

  
Executive Order D 2020 116 
June 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
  
  
 
 
  
  
  
 
 
  
  
   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 020 for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
On May 29, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 082, extending Executive Orders D 2020 020 and D 2020 046 for an 
additional thirty (30) days.
 
Telehealth services remain essential to ensure that health care providers and 
veterinarians can provide needed services to patients while also minimizing the risk of spread of 
COVID-19.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 020, D 2020 046, and D 
2020 082, for an additional thirty (30) days so that telehealth services can continue throughout 
the State.
 
II. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 020, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 046, D 2020 082, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 27, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order.
 
In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 020, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 046 and D 2020 082, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-seventh day of 
June, 2020.
 
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

full_text:

117

Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain Requirements Preventing Issuance of

(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10,

request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our

and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6)

On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary

licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19. On 

 
Executive Order D 2020 117

-

2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D
 
2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in

On March 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 011 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain regulatory statutes due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado. On April 
6, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 029, which amended and extended Executive Order D 
2020 011 until April 30, 2020, to include a number of additional statutory suspensions. On April
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 118 
June 27, 2020

-
 
30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 052, extending Executive Orders D 2020 011 and D
 
2020 029 for an additional thirty (30) days. On May 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020

On June 5, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 093, which amended Executive Orders D

title: D 2020-119
full_text:
D 2020
 
119
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 053 and D 2020 085 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Petition Gathering for Unaffiliated 
and Independent Candidates for Office Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 053 and D 2020 085, which order the temporary suspension of certain regulatory 
statutes concerning petition collection for unaffiliated and independent candidates due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D
 
2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major
 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. We must take action to minimize the duration of
 
this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread 
primarily by close contact between people and through respiratory droplets when an infected 
person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or
 
maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the 
spread of COVID-19.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 053 and D 2020 085, 
which temporarily suspend the statutory provisions that allow collection of petitions for 
unaffiliated candidates, by suspending an additional statutory provision to ensure designated 
election officials have adequate time to review and provide notification of sufficiency or 
insufficiency to unaffiliated candidates.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 119 
June 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
-
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 053, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 085 and this
 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 27, 2020, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 053, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2020 085, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty
 
seventh day of June, 2020.
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-120
full_text:
D 2020 120
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Directing the Colorado Department of Human Services to Access Federal Funds for Child 
Care Services Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order directing the Colorado 
Department of Human Services to access federal funds for child care services due to the presence 
of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The federal Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020 (CARES Act), 
Public Law 116-136, allocated $3.5 billion to the states through the Child Care and Development 
Block grant. These funds may be used by states for all existing allowable Child Care and 
Development Fund (CCDF) activities as well as a number of purposes related to COVID-19 and 
child care. Executive Order D 2020 007 directed the Colorado Department of Human Services
 
(CDHS) to access additional federal Child Care Development Funds to reimburse counties that
 
pay providers through the Colorado Child Care Assistance Program (CCCAP) for absences or
 
closures as a result of COVID-19 for the next eight (8) weeks, with the possibility of extension.
 
This Executive Order directs CDHS, pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(7)(b), to access
 
remaining available CCDF funds allocated by the CARES Act to continue to support child care
 
services during the COVID-19 pandemic to ensure stability and consistency for child care
 
providers and protect the vital role they play for children and families. Accessing and allocating 

Executive Order D 2020 120 
June 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
these available federal funds now will enable the State to continue to support child care services 
and avoid interrupting support while the economic disruption caused by the pandemic continues.
 
II.
 Directives
 
A.
 I direct CDHS to access additional federal Child Care Development Funds
 
(CCDF) for counties to reimburse open and operating child care programs 
through the Colorado Child Care Assistance Program (CCCAP) for increased
 
child absences as a result of COVID-19. I further direct CDHS to access CCDF to 
support affordable parental fees for subsidized child care as families recover from
 
the impacts of the COVID-related economic recession.
 
B.
 I direct CDHS to access additional federal CCDF to fund additional early 
childhood mental health consultants across the state to support child care 
providers and families as children transition back into early care and learning 
environments. I strongly encourage community mental health organizations and 
early childhood councils to collaborate with early childhood mental health 
consultants to effectively respond to the health and well-being of children and 
providers impacted by COVID-19 by addressing trauma, grief, loss, emotional 
distress, fostering healthy adult-child relationships, and building resilience.
 
C.
 I direct CDHS to access additional federal CCDF to establish and administer 
sustainability grants to assist licensed child care providers to remain open or re-
 
open to enable the State’s workforce to return to work. Sustainability grants will 
be used to support child care providers with costs associated with COVID-19, 
including but not limited to accommodating lower group sizes, purchasing 
additional safety and cleaning supplies, and staff retention strategies.
 
D.
 I direct CDHS, in coordination with the Colorado Department of Education, to 
work with local communities, including school districts and the Charter School 
Institute, to maximize the use of public and private spaces licensed for child care.
 
I strongly encourage school districts to make their facilities available over the 
summer months for child care, including school age care and summer day camps, 
to the greatest extent possible.
 
E.
 
I direct local public health agencies to work with child care providers and 
preschools to determine whether it is safe to continue operations. If there is a 
positive or suspected case of COVID-19 in a student, parent of a student, or staff
 
member at the child care or preschool facility, I direct the facility to work in 
partnership with the appropriate local public health agency to determine whether
 
to close the facility or affected portion of the facility. The facility or affected 
portion shall close for no less than twenty-four (24) hours, and implement 
protocol for responding to COVID-19 pursuant to CDPHE guidance and in 
partnership with the appropriate local public health agency.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 120 
June 27, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III. 
Duration
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from June 27, 2020, unless 
extended further by Executive Order.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
 
twenty-seventh day of June, 
2020.
 
 
 
Jar
Governor
 
ed Polis

title: D 2020-121
full_text:
D 2020 121
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 022, D 2020 055, and D 2020 089 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes Concerning Taxpayer Filing 
Requirements for Certain Taxable Property due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 022, D 2020 055, and D 2020 089 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes concerning taxpayer filing requirements for certain taxable 
property due to the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 022, D 2020 055, and D 2020 
089 for an additional thirty (30) days from June 28, 2020 to continue the temporary suspension 
of calendar year 2020 statutory deadlines concerning taxpayer filing requirements for certain 
taxable property. This executive order also amends Executive Order D 2020 022 by deleting 
paragraph II.A. because the relevant filing date extensions have expired.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 121 
June 28, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
II.
 Amendment
 
1.
 Strike paragraph II.A. in Executive Order D 2020 022.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 022, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 055, D 2020 089,
 
and as amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 
28, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 022, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 055 and D 2020 089, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth 
day of June, 2020. 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

full_text:
