D 2020
 
122
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 056 and D 2020 090 Ordering the Suspension of a 
Relevant Statute to Allow Food Trucks to Operate at Colorado’s Rest Areas to 
Support the Movement of Commercial Vehicle Activities Due to the Presence of
 
COVID-19 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 056 and D 2020 090, which order the suspension of a relevant statute to allow 
food trucks to operate at Colorado’s rest areas to support the movement of commercial vehicle 
activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President 
of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
Commercial truck drivers play a critical role in the supply chain and in the COVID
 
19 emergency response. Truckers transport essential supplies including equipment, 
medicine, food, and personal protective equipment across our State and the country. 
Normal enforcement of restrictions pertaining to commercial activities at highway rest and 
refueling areas stand in the way of accessible, affordable, fresh food options for truck 
drivers operating in Colorado. Other states are allowing food trucks to operate at rest stops
 
during this emergency so that commercial truck drivers have easy and reliable access to
 
nutritious, affordable, and freshly prepared meals, and, as a centrally located state, 
Colorado trucking routes are critical to the national supply chain. We therefore must take 

  
Executive Order D 2020 122 
June 29, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
action to support our hard working truckers and increase the availability of freshly prepared 
food options while providing additional economic opportunities during this COVID-19 
crisis to food truck operators. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 056 and D 2020 090 for an 
additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 056, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 090 and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 29, 2020, unless extended further
 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 056, as extended by
 
Executive Order D 2020 090, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-ninth
 
day of June, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-123
full_text:
D 2020 123
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 091 Safer at Home and in the Vast, 
Great Outdoors
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 091, Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, due to the presence 
of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 
109.
 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major
 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that
 
request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for Vulnerable Individuals as 
ninety percent (90%) of Colorado fatalities are individuals older than sixty (60). Evidence shows 
that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, have 
helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured that Coloradans who needed it had access to 
healthcare resources.
 
While we have seen indications that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, 
increased transmission of the virus, and the resulting strains on our healthcare resources, 

Executive Order D 2020 123 
June 30, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
  
 
  
 
     
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
continue to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. As we take steps to return 
Coloradans to work, we must continue to practice Social Distancing from others at a high level 
and implement other measures, such as limiting our social interactions, extra precautions for 
Vulnerable Individuals, and wearing non-medical masks while in public to facilitate reopening 
the economy while we protect public health. As we move forward, the State continues to monitor 
the spread of COVID-19, increase our testing and tracing capabilities, and consider additional 
precautions as we learn more about the novel coronavirus and its effects on human health.
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 091 by modifying the 
conditions for Bars to open while continuing to allow many Coloradans to return to work and 
recreation in the great outdoors while Coloradans maintain a sustainable level of Social 
Distancing.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 091 is amended as follows:
 
1.
 Replace paragraph II.E.iii. with the following:
 
Get tested for COVID-19 if they are experiencing flu-like symptoms or if they are 
sick. If an individual has tested positive for COVID-19 and/or has developed 
symptoms of COVID-19, including early or mild symptoms (such as cough and 
shortness of breath), they should be in isolation (staying away from others) until 
they have had no fever for at least seventy-two (72) hours (that is three full days 
of no fever without the use of medicine that reduces fevers), other symptoms have
 
improved (such as cough or shortness of breath), and at least ten (10) days have 
passed since symptoms first appeared. Coloradans who are sick and receive 
negative COVID-19 test results should continue to stay home while they are sick 
and should consult with their healthcare provider about the need for additional 
testing and the appropriate time to resume normal activities; and
 
2.
 Add new paragraph II.F.7. as follows:
 
7.
 
Close Bars, as defined in PHO 20-28, as amended, or any other PHO issued 
pursuant to this Executive Order, to ingress, egress, use, and occupancy by 
members of the public unless the establishment ensures access to served food 
from a licensed retail food establishment at tables spaced at least six (6) feet apart 
with set seating for on-premise consumption. In accordance with Executive Order
 
D 2020 029, as amended, Bars are permitted to sell alcoholic beverages to-go for 
takeout or delivery consumption if the alcoholic beverages are sold with food and 
provided in containers approved by rule by the State Licensing Authority to 
customers who are over twenty-one (21) years old. Bars permitted to open 
pursuant to the terms of a county variance granted under paragraph II.J. of this 
Executive Order shall be exempt from any additional restrictions provided in this 
paragraph II.F.7.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 123 
June 30, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
   
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
3.
 Amend paragraph II.I. by inserting the word “Bars,” between the words “Social
 
Distancing Requirements,” and “and.”
 
4.
 Add new paragraph II.N. as follows:
 
N. 
I direct the Executive Director of the Department of Revenue to ensure that the 
Liquor Enforcement Division takes action within twenty-four (24) hours of 
submission of an application for temporary modification of a license for Bars that 
serve food from a licensed retail food establishment in compliance with paragraph 
II.F.7. of this Executive Order.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from June 30, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order.
 
In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 091 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
thirtieth day of June, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-124
full_text:
D 2020 124
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency and Authorizing Employment of the Colorado National 
Guard to Assist with Protecting Municipal and State Buildings in Denver
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of
 
disaster emergency due to civil unrest in Denver.
 
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I hereby authorize employing up to one hundred (100) members of the
 
Colorado National Guard to assist with protecting municipal and state buildings in Denver.
 
I.
 Background and Purpose
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or
 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin . . . .” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-
 
703(3). 
In May 2020, the killing of George Floyd sparked demonstrations in Denver and across 
the country. While most of the protests and demonstrations were peaceful, there were some 
incidents of vandalism and violence in downtown Denver. Denver law enforcement agencies 
requested mutual aid from neighboring cities. Those cities supplied Denver with additional 
support. 
By May 30, 2020, the need to protect property owned by the City and County of Denver 
and the State of Colorado exceeded the capabilities of Denver law enforcement agencies and 
mutual aid provided by neighboring cities. Denver requested that the Governor authorize 
employment of up to one hundred (100) unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard to 
provide additional support to law enforcement to protect eight buildings owned by the City and 
County of Denver, and one building belonging to the State of Colorado. 
At approximately 10:45 AM on May 30, 2020, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
due to civil unrest in Denver and the need to provide additional support for protecting municipal 
and State buildings in Denver. I authorized activating up to one hundred (100) unarmed 

Executive Order D 2020 124 
July 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
  
members of the Colorado National Guard to provide the requested support and security in 
Denver. I also activated the State of Colorado Emergency Operations Plan.
 
II.
 Declarations and Directives
 
A.
 The civil unrest in Denver constitutes a disaster emergency under C.R.S § 24-
 
33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order on May 30, 2020, declaring a disaster
 
emergency and authorizing employment and use of the Colorado National Guard
 
is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall have the full force and
 
effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive Order.
 
B.
 The State Emergency Operations Plan is hereby activated.
 
All State departments
 
and agencies shall take whatever actions may be required and requested by the
 
Director or Acting Director of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to
 
the Emergency Support Function 5 Annex of the State Emergency Operations
 
Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and equipment as necessary.
 
C.
 Pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-
 
104, I have determined that it is in the best interest of the people of Colorado to
 
employee up to one hundred (100) unarmed members of the Colorado National
 
Guard to provide support and security for civil unrest in Denver. To ensure timely
 
reimbursement to the Colorado National Guard, I authorize encumbering two
 
hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) from the Disaster Emergency Fund,
 
established in C.R.S § 24-33.5-706(2)(a), to pay the Colorado National Guard for
 
costs associated with personnel. This amount is currently available in the Disaster
 
Emergency Fund. Seventy-five percent (75%) of costs incurred by the Colorado
 
National Guard for support and security for Denver City and County buildings are
 
to be reimbursed by Denver to the State of Colorado. The remaining twenty-five
 
percent (25%) of costs for support and security will be paid by the State of
 
Colorado.
 
D.
 Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency
 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate funding to the Colorado
 
National Guard and execute awards, purchase orders, or other mechanisms to
 
effect the allocation of the funds. The funds described in Section II(C) above shall
 
remain available for this purpose until six (6) months from the date of this
 
Executive Order, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster
 
Emergency Fund.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 124 
July 6, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III.
 Duration
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from May 30, 2020, unless extended
 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(C) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for six (6) months from the date of this Executive Order.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this sixth
 
day of July, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-125
full_text:
- 
 
 
  
D 2020 125
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due 
to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared
 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and 
D 2020 109 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for response
 
activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent
 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24
 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from
 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109 
and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States 
declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President 
approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 

Executive Order D 2020 125 
July 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
  
  
-
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
-
-
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize
 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives.
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109, to extend the state of disaster emergency and the 
deployment of the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State 
and local authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State. Additionally, this 
Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076 and D 2020 109 to increase the amount of funds previously made available to CDPHE 
by eighty-two million six hundred seventy-five thousand three hundred sixty-four dollars 
($82,675,364);
 
increase the amount of funds previously made available to the Colorado 
Department of Public Safety by fifteen million nine hundred sixty-nine thousand four hundred 
twenty-six dollars ($15,969,426); increase the amount of funds made available to the Colorado 
Governor’s Office of Information and Technology by nine-hundred five thousand four hundred 
fifty dollars ($905,450); and increase the amount of funds made available to the Colorado 
Department of Agriculture by one million six hundred eighty-eight thousand four hundred eight 
dollars
 
($1,688,408) for COVID-19 response activities. The funds made available for response 
activities in this Executive Order were transferred to the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of 
the Disaster Emergency Fund by Executive Order D 2020 070. Executive Order D 2020 070 
transferred forty-eight million dollars ($48,000,000) in FY 2019-20 and one hundred and fifty
 
seven million dollars ($157,000,000) at the beginning of FY 2020-21 from the CARES Act Fund 
to the Disaster Emergency Fund Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account. 
II. 
Amendment
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 
018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109 is amended and restated as follows:
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and twenty-three million dollars ($23,000,000) was 
transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that two hundred twenty-six million thirty-eight 
thousand six hundred forty-eight dollars ($226,038,648) from the Disaster Emergency Fund be 
encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these funds, one hundred thirty
 
eight million thirty-eight thousand six hundred forty-eight dollars ($138,038,648) shall come 
from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds 
coming from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred twelve million two hundred 
seventy-five thousand three hundred sixty-four dollars ($112,275,364) shall be encumbered and 
made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19; twenty-three million one 
hundred sixty-nine thousand four hundred twenty-six dollars ($23,169,426) shall be encumbered 
and made available to the Department of Public Safety for response activities related to COVID
 
19; nine-hundred five thousand four hundred fifty dollars ($905,450) shall be encumbered and 

 
Executive Order D 2020 125 
July 6, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
made available to the Colorado Governor’s Office of Information and Technology for response 
activities related to COVID-19; and one million six hundred eighty-eight thousand four hundred 
eight dollars
 
($1,688,408) shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department 
of Agriculture for response activities related to COVID-19. These funds shall remain available
 
for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 30, 2020, and any unexpended funds 
shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund.
 
III. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109 and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from July 6, 2020, unless amended further by Executive Order, except that the funds 
described in Section II.C shall remain available for the described purposes and the described 
time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended 
by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, and D 2020 109 shall 
remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this sixth
 
day of July, 2020
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-126
full_text:
D 2020 126
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, and D 2020 094 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Juvenile 
Justice, Regional Centers, and Behavioral Health Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060 and D
 
2020 094, ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile 
justice, regional centers, and behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 
2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 

Executive Order D 2020 126 
July 8, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
   
 
  
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
I temporarily suspend the requirement in C.R.S. § 19-2-508(4)(g) that any person 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.
 
On May 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 060, amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 034 to temporarily suspend an additional statute so that the Division of 
Youth Services could take further action to prevent the spread of COVID-19 among its youth

On June 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 094, extending Executive Orders D

This Executive Order extends Sections II.A, II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I of

technical amendment to Section II.I in conformity with SB 19-108, effective July 1, 2020.
 
II.

Section II.I of Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive

who is eighteen years of age or older who is being detained for a delinquent act or

title: D 2020-127
full_text:
D 2020 127
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Protect Our Neighbors
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at risk of severe 
illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive 
Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured that Coloradans 
who needed it had access to healthcare resources.
 
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044 , as amended, and the Safer at 
Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive Order 2020 091, as amended, my 
administration took steps to return Coloradans to work while continuing the efforts to “flatten the 
curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home and Safer at Home and in the Vast, 
Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to continue to practice Social Distancing from
 

Executive Order D 2020 127 
July 9, 2020 
Page 2 of 5 
 
-
  
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
 
     
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
others at a high level and implement other measures, such as limited social interactions, taking 
extra precautions for individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wearing non
 
medical masks while in public to facilitate reopening the economy.
 
As the number of outbreaks decreases in a particular area, we will move into the next 
phase: Protect Our Neighbors. Local communities that meet certain qualifications can apply to 
become a Protect Our Neighbors Community. These communities will have: strong underlying 
public health and health care capacity that can respond to larger outbreaks; strong compliance 
with statewide standards; and the ability to enforce guidelines and public health orders.
 
If a community cannot maintain the required metrics for Protect Our Neighbors, the State
 
will return them to the requirements of Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors. Because 
each community and each part of our State has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows 
communities to create unique and flexible plans within the statewide guidelines. Even as we 
continue to open our economy through Protect Our Neighbors, we must continue to protect and 
support people who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19, including 
individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19 such as older adults and people with 
underlying medical conditions.
 
This Executive Order implements a number of measures that will allow local
 
communities in Colorado that have met certain qualifications and have a mitigation and 
containment plan in place to allow all activities to occur at fifty perfect (50%) capacity with 
Social Distancing with no more than five hundred (500) people in any one setting. Local 
communities may issue more detailed guidelines or public health orders to meet the needs of 
their community as long as the capacity does not exceed the statewide limit. This Executive
 
Order does not apply to Critical Businesses, which have been continuously authorized to operate 
without capacity restrictions throughout the pandemic. Protect Our Neighbors gives local
 
communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still protecting the public’s health.
 
II.
 Directives
 
A.
 All individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19 should Stay at Home or
 
in the vast, great outdoors away from others as much as possible, and continue to 
limit their social interactions, remain at least six (6) feet from others not in their 
household, and wear facial coverings in public. Individuals at risk of severe 
illness from COVID-19 cannot be compelled to perform in-person work for any 
business or government function, including a Critical Business or Critical 
Government Function.
 
B.
 For purposes of this Executive Order, individuals at risk of severe illness from
 
COVID-19 means:
 
1.
 Individuals who are sixty-five (65) years and older;
 
2.
 Individuals with chronic lung disease or moderate to severe asthma;
 

Executive Order D 2020 127 
July 9, 2020 
Page 3 of 5 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
  
 
  
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
3.
 Individuals who have serious heart conditions;
 
4.
 Individuals who are immunocompromised;
 
5.
 Pregnant women; and
 
6.
 Individuals determined to be high risk by a licensed healthcare provider.
 
C.
 I direct the Colorado Civil Rights Division within the Department of Regulatory 
Agencies and CDPHE to provide continued guidance to prevent discrimination in 
the workplace related to COVID-19. Employers must provide reasonable 
accommodation to, and are prohibited from discriminating against, employees 
who are showing symptoms of COVID-19 or who have been in contact with a 
known positive case of COVID-19.
 
D.
 I direct the Executive Director of CDPHE to create a certification process to allow 
local communities to move from Safer at Home in the Vast, Great Outdoors to 
Protect Our Neighbors. To qualify for Protect Our Neighbors, a county or a region 
must:
 
a.
 Certify that they meet qualification metrics established by CDPHE; and
 
b.
 Submit a mitigation and containment plan on what the county or region 
will do if they fall out of compliance with one or more of these metrics, 
and documentation of support for the plan by local officials, law
 
enforcement, and any others identified by CDPHE.
 
E.
 
I direct the Executive Director of CDPHE to issue a new public health order 
(PHO) consistent with the directives in this Executive Order for counties and 
regions that qualify for Protect Our Neighbors. The PHO must:
 
a.
 Strongly encourage all businesses and governmental entities to allow 
workers to telecommute whenever possible. 
b.
 Require all employers to make work accommodations for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19. Employers are also encouraged to 
make accommodations for individuals living in the same household as an
 
individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19 and for individuals 
with childcare responsibilities.
 
c.
 Allow all Non-Critical Office-Based Businesses to operate at fifty percent 
(50%) pre-pandemic capacity, which can be increased over time through 
subsequent public health orders, with at least six (6) feet between non-
 
household members and following the requirements in the PHO 20-28, as 
amended, for any business-specific disease prevention and mitigation 
strategies.
 

Executive Order D 2020 127 
July 9, 2020 
Page 4 of 5 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
  
 
   
 
 
 
. Allow all other businesses and activities to operate at fifty percent (50%) 
capacity, which can be increased over time through subsequent public 
health orders, with at least six feet between non-household members and 
have no more than five hundred (500) people in one setting at a time, 
following the requirements in the PHO 20-28, as amended, for any 
business or activity specific disease prevention or mitigation strategies. 
However, these limits do not apply to Critical Businesses or Critical 
Government Functions, which have been continuously authorized to 
operate without capacity restrictions throughout the pandemic.
 
e.
 Require implementation of containment and mitigation strategies by 
counties or regions that are out of compliance with one or more of the 
qualifying metrics for Protect Our Neighbors.
 
f.
 Authorize counties and regions to develop more detailed public health 
orders or guidelines for different settings as long as the capacity does not
 
exceed the limits established by this Executive Order and any PHO issued 
pursuant to this Executive Order.
 
g.
 Authorize the rescission of Protect Our Neighbors status and a return to 
Safer at Home in the Vast, Great Outdoors for any county or region that
 
falls out of compliance with any of the qualification metrics for Protect 
Our Neighbors.
 
F.
 
Critical Business, Critical Government Function, Non-Critical Office-Based 
Business, Non-Critical Manufacturing, and Social Distancing Requirements shall
 
have the meaning as defined in PHO 20-28, or any PHO issued pursuant to this 
Executive Order.
 
G.
 For the purpose of this Executive Order, local community means a county or a 
group of counties that have agreed to form a region for the purposes of applying 
for Protect Our Neighbors.
 
H.
 Nothing in this Executive Order prevents a county or municipality from adopting 
more protective standards than those contained in this Executive Order as 
necessary.
 
I.
 
Except as modified by this Executive Order, all Executive Orders issued due to 
COVID-19 that are currently in effect shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 127 
July 9, 2020 
Page 5 of 5 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Duration
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from July 9, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this ninth
 
day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-128
full_text:
D 2020 128
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 065 and D 2020 103
 
in Light of the Colorado Supreme Court’s Decision in Ritchie v. Polis  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 065 and D 2020 103 ordering the temporary suspension of certain 
regulatory statutes concerning signature collection for ballot issues and authorizing the Secretary 
of State to create temporary rules to allow for mail and email signature collection due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact 
between people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or
 
sneezes. Public health experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a 
physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of 
COVID-19.
 
On April 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 044, Safer at Home, as 
amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 079. On June 1, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 091, Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, as amended 

Executive Order D 2020 128 
July 10, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
and extended by Executive Order D 2020 123. These Executive Orders limit in-person 
activities, which imposes a significant hurdle for ballot petition circulators, who play an 
essential role in our democratic republic and have significant and determinative barriers 
due to State and local public health orders that prevent them from the normal statutory 
conduct of in-person signature gathering.
 
To preserve our constitutional principle of ballot access, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 065, as extended by D 2020 103. These Executive Orders temporarily suspended
 
statutory provisions requiring registered electors to sign ballot issue petitions in the 
presence of a petition circulator, and authorized the Secretary of State to issue rules that 
allow for signature collection in a manner that protects the public’s health while the 
COVID-19 disaster emergency declaration is in place. My goal with the Executive Orders 
was to protect Coloradans’ constitutional right to shape their government through the 
initiative and referendum processes without risking their health or the health of others.
 
On July 1, 2020, the Colorado Supreme Court held in Ritchie v. Polis, 2020 CO 69, 
that the prior practice under article V, section 1 of the Colorado Constitution requires that
 
ballot initiative petitions be signed in the presence of the petition circulator. The Court 
ruled that this requirement cannot be suspended by Executive Order. This Executive Order 
addresses the Court’s decision by amending Executive Orders D 2020 065 and D 2020 103 
so that each provision in Section II with the exception of Section II.B is deleted. Section 
II.B, which does not pertain to the requirement of in-person presence of petition circulators, 
is extended for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 065, as extended by D 2020 103, is amended as follows:
 
i.
 
Delete Sections II.A, II.C, II.D, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, II.I, II.J, and 
II.K.
 
ii.
 
Section II.B. is extended for thirty (30) days.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 128 
July 10, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 065, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 103 and as 
amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 10, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 065, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 103, shall remain in full force and effect
 
as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this tenth
 
day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-129
full_text:
D 2020 129
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 005, D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D
 
2020 061, and D 2020 095 Concerning Limiting In-Person Contact for the 2020 
Elections and the Secretary of State’s Operations Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in 
Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending 
extending Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, D 2020 061, and D 2020 095, concerning 
changes to the 2020 primary election to limit in-person contact while allowing the elections 
to proceed safely and without interruption to the Secretary of State’s operations due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. We must take action to minimize
 
the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that causes 
COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and through respiratory 
droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts recommend we 
practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from
 
other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 005, as 
amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, 

  
Executive Order D 2020 129 
July 10, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
D 2020 061, and D 2020 095, for thirty (30) days from July 10, 2020. This extension will
 
allow the 2020 primary election to continue safely and without interruption while 
minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19.
 
II. 
Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, D 2020 061, and D 2020 095, is amended as
 
follows:
 
i.
 Sections II.A, II.B, II.C, II.D, and II.E are deleted.
 
ii.
 Sections II.F, II.G, II.H, II.I, and II.J are extended for an additional thirty (30) 
days.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and as
 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, D 2020 061, and amended by this Executive Order, 
shall expire thirty (30) days from July 10, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In 
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, D 2020 061, and D 2020 095, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this tenth 
day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-130
full_text:
D 2020 130
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, and D 2020 096 Ordering the
 
Temporary Suspension of Statute Concerning Bans on Open Burning Due to the 
Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending
 
Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, and D 2020 096 ordering the temporary
 
suspension of statute concerning bans on open burning due to the presence of coronavirus
 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a
 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a
 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The response to COVID-19 is straining State, local, and federal resources. Large
 
wildfires require an inter-agency response that relies on local, State, and federal cooperation and 
coordination. Given the current strain on government resources due to COVID-19, any wildfire
 
response would be diminished, which risks enabling a fire to grow and spread, which in turn 
requires even more resources for firefighting. Given the State’s need to focus on COVID-19 
mitigation efforts, it is imperative that the State take every action to reduce the risk of wildfire.
 
On April 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 037, temporarily suspending statute
 
concerning bans on open burning to give boards of county commissioners broader discretion and 

  
Executive Order D 2020 130 
July 11, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
more flexibility to implement restrictions on open burning that will decrease the risk of fire and
 
enable firefighters and other first responders to participate in the COVID-19 response efforts
 
throughout the State. I extended Executive Order D 2020 037 on May 14, 2020, and again on 
June 12, 2020. This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, and D
 
2020 096 for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 037, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 062, D 2020 
096, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 11, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 037, as extended by
 
Executive Orders D 2020 062 and D 2020 096, shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
 
eleventh day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-131
full_text:
D 2020 131
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, and D 2020 097 Ordering the
 
Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes and Rules to Expand the Healthcare 
Workforce for Hospitals and Other Inpatient Treatment Facilities Due to the 
Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending
 
Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, and D 2020 097, ordering the temporary
 
suspension of certain statutes and rules to expand the healthcare workforce for hospitals
 
and other inpatient treatment facilities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test
 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster
 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado,
 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has
 
undertaken a wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to
 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as extended by
 
Executive Orders D 2020 063 and D 2020 097, for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure
 
that inpatient healthcare facilities have sufficient resources and personnel to treat patients
 
suffering from COVID-19.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 031 
July 11, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 0038, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D
 
2020 097, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 11, 2020, unless
 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 038, as
 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063 and D 2020 097, shall remain in full force and 
effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
 
eleventh day of July, 2020
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-132
full_text:
D 2020 132
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Order D 2020 098 Providing Relief to Public Utility Customers 
affected by COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 098 for an additional thirty (30) days, providing relief to public utility customers to 
mitigate, respond to, and recover from the current economic disruption due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result
 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have
 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D
 
2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by
 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all
 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 098, which temporarily provides 
relief and State support to public utility customers to mitigate, respond to, and recover from the 
current economic disruption due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 132 
July 11, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from July 11, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 098 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
eleventh day of July, 2020
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-133
full_text:
D 2020 133
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Order Ordering Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim
 
Processing to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100 for an additional thirty (30) days, ordering expedited unemployment insurance (UI) 
claim processing due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I. 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result
 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have
 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D
 
2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by
 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all
 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications 
that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten 
Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to reply on unemployment 
insurance to meet their basic needs.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100 for an additional thirty (30)
 
days, which expedites processing of unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will
 
help protect the economic well-being of Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 133 
July 12, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
  
   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from July 12, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 100 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
twelfth day of July, 2020
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-134
full_text:
D 2020 134
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 101 Ordering the Temporary Limiting 
of Certain Evictions to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending
 
Executive Order D 2020 101 ordering state agencies to help prevent evictions of tenants 
economically harmed by coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result
 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have
 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D
 
2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by
 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all
 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
Transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. 
On April 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 044 Safer at Home, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 079 and D 2020 091. To be Safer at Home, Coloradans 
impacted financially by COVID-19 must continue to have a home. We are doing everything we
 
can to get Coloradans back to work, but this process is gradual and must take into account the
 
evolving public health conditions. Many Coloradans continue to experience substantial loss of 

Executive Order D 2020 134 
July 12, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
income as a result of business closures and layoffs, hindering their ability to keep up with their 
rent or mortgage payments through no fault of their own.
 
Through this Executive Order, amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 101, I
 
encourage landlords to take steps to limit evictions for tenants who have made a good faith effort 
to make rental payments or who have made a good faith effort to establish a repayment 
agreement. 
II. 
Amendments
 
Section II.A of Executive Order D 2020 101 is hereby amended to read as follows:
 
I temporarily suspend C.R.S. § 38-12-204(1), 38-12-204.3(2), and 13-40-104(1)(d)
 
requiring landlords to provide tenants ten (10) days’ notice of any default for nonpayment 
of rent that occurred on or after March 10th, 2020, during which time the tenant has the 
opportunity to cure the default. Landlords must provide tenants with thirty (30) days’ 
notice of any default for nonpayment that occurred on or after March 10th, 2020 before
 
initiating or filing action for forcible entry and detainer. Such 30-day notice may extend 
beyond the expiration of this Executive Order. During this thirty (30) day period, tenants 
shall have a one-time opportunity to cure any default for nonpayment that occurred on or
 
after March 10, 2020. 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 101 is hereby amended to include the following: 
E.
 I strongly encourage all municipalities and other local jurisdictions that place limits on 
the number of unrelated persons who can live in a single household or that prohibit group 
or boarding houses to suspend or eliminate those restrictions to enable homeowners to 
rent or give a room or rooms to those in need of housing. 
F.
 I strongly encourage all municipalities and other local jurisdictions that place limits on 
the number of days hotel rooms may be occupied to suspend or eliminate those
 
restrictions.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 134 
July 12, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 101, as amended and extended by this Executive Order shall
 
expire thirty (30) days from July 12, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all
 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 101 shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.
 
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
twelfth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-135
full_text:
D 2020 135
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 064 and D 2020 102 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Signature Collection for
 
Unaffiliated and Independent Candidates and Authorizing the Secretary of State to 
Create Temporary Rules for Registered Electors To Receive and Return Candidate
 
Petitions Over Mail and Email Due to the Presence of COVID19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 064 and D 2020 102 ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory
 
statutes concerning signature collection for unaffiliated and independent candidates who are
 
required to file petition with the Secretary of State under Title 1 and authorizing the Secretary of 
State to create temporary rules to allow for mail and email signature collection due to the
 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a
 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact 
between people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or 
sneezes. Public health experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a
 
physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of 
COVID-19. On April 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 044 Safer at Home, as 

 
Executive Order D 2020 135 
July 12, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 079 and D 2020 091, limiting in 
person activities, which imposes a significant hurdle for unaffiliated and independent 
candidates and duly designated circulators, who play an essential role in our democratic
 
republic and have significant and determinative barriers due to State and local public health 
orders that prevent them from the normal statutory conduct of in-person signature
 
gathering.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 064, as extended by D 2020 
102, which preserves our constitutional principle of ballot access, for an additional thirty
 
(30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 064, as extended by D 2020 102 and this Executive Order, 
shall expire thirty (30) days from July 12, 2020, unless extended further by Executive
 
Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 064, as extended by Executive Order 
D 2020 102, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
twelfth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-136
full_text:
D 2020 136
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068 and D 2020 104 Temporarily Suspending
 
Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan
 
Enrollees
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 068 and D 2020 104, temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain
 
eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test
 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster
 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado,
 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has
 
undertaken a broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to
 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the
 
Medicaid program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage 
(FMAP) if they maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after
 
March 18, 2020.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 104, which ensures compliance with the Families First 
Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending certain statutes to allow for
 
continued eligibility or benefits status for those individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 136 
July 12, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
March 18, 2020 and to prevent any changes in eligibility, and directs the Executive
 
Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to delay the
 
collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic Health Plan.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104 and this
 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 12, 2020, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by
 
Executive Order D 2020 104, shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
twelfth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-137
full_text:
D 2020 137
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 071 and D 2020 107 Concerning 
the Suspension of Statutes to Allow the Operation of Alternate Care Sites in Colorado 
Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. ("Act"), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order
 
amending and extending Executive Orders D 2020 071 and D 2020 107 ordering the 
suspension of certain statutes to allow the operation of alternate care sites in Colorado due 
to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally 
declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I
 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken 
a wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the 
collateral consequences of the pandemic. It is imperative that we ensure health care facilities 
have sufficient resources and to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-
 
19 patients who no longer require inpatient hospitalization but still require some medical 
care and observation while recovering from COVID-19. 
This Executive Order amends and extends the suspension of certain statutes and 
authorizes the Division of Homeland Security and Emergency Management (DHSEM) in the 
Department of Public Safety to enter into contracts to allow alternative care sites (ACSs) to 
operate at the Colorado Convention Center, The Ranch Larimer County Fairgrounds and Events 
Complex (The Ranch), St. Anthony’s facility in Thornton (St. Anthony’s), St. Mary-Corwin 
Medical Center in Pueblo (St. Mary-Corwin Medical Center), and the Western Slope Memory 

Executive Order D 2020 137 
July 14, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
Center in Grand Junction (Western Slope Memory Center). Denver Health Hospital Authority 
(DHHA) will provide services to operate the Colorado Convention Center ACS, UCHealth will 
provide services to operate The Ranch ACS, and Veritas Management Group, LLC will provide 
services to operate St Anthony’s, St. Mary-Corwin Medical Center, and the Western Slope 
Memory Center. All ACSs will be operated under the oversight of DHSEM. 
II.
 Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 071, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 107, is amended 
as follows: 
1.
 Strike Section II.A. and replace with the following:
 
I direct DHSEM to create ACSs at the Colorado Convention Center, The Ranch, St
 
Anthony’s, St. Mary-Corwin Medical Center, and the Western Slope Memory
 
Center to provide healthcare services to patients recovering from COVID-19.
 
2.
 Insert the phrase “Western Slope Memory Center, St Anthony’s, St. Mary-Corwin
 
Medical Center,” between the words “the” and “Colorado” in Section II.B.
 
3.
 Insert the phrase “Western Slope Memory Center, St Anthony’s, St. Mary-Corwin
 
Medical Center,” between the words “the” and “Colorado” in Section II.E.
 
4.
 Insert the phrase “Western Slope Memory Center, St Anthony’s, St. Mary-Corwin
 
Medical Center,” between the words “the” and “Colorado” in Section II.F.
 
5.
 Add a new Section II.G.:
 
G.
 Pursuant to the authority established in relevant provisions of the Act,
 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I direct DHSEM and Veritas Management
 
Group, LLC to enter into a contract to provide healthcare services consistent
 
with the provisions of this Executive Order at St. Anthony’s, St. Mary-
 
Corwin Medical Center, and the Western Slope Memory Center ACS
 
locations. While acting in good faith to comply completely with this
 
Executive Order, and the resulting contract, to operate the St. Anthony’s, St.
 
Mary-Corwin Medical Center, and the Western Slope Memory Center ACS
 
locations, Veritas Management Group, LLC and its personnel shall be
 
immune from civil or criminal liability for any action taken to comply with
 
this Executive Order related to their activities at the St Anthony’s, St. Mary-
 
Corwin Medical Center, and the Western Slope Memory Center ACS
 
locations pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-711.5(2).
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 137 
July 14, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 071, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 107 and as
 
amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 14, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Orders D 
2020 071 and D 2020 107 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
fourteenth day of July, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-138
full_text:
D 2020 138
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
