Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, and D
 
2020 110 Ordering Individuals in Colorado to Wear Non-Medical Face Coverings
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, and D 2020 110 ordering 
individuals in Colorado to wear a medical or non-medical face covering due to the presence of 
coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. We have seen over 37,000 infections and lost over 1,700 
Coloradans. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 
2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders
 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’
 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID infections. As we 
take steps to return Coloradans to work, we must continue to take measures to facilitate 
reopening the economy while protecting public health by taking steps to incorporate best
 
practices to protect individuals from infection.
 
We have learned that widespread mask use is a low cost and highly effective way to 
reduce the spread of COVID-19 infections by as much as 65%. Broad adoption of mask wearing 
in Colorado may have significant economic benefits by allowing the State to prevent re-closures 
of businesses and schools and, ultimately, return to our normal lives more quickly. A recent 
study from Goldman Sachs concluded that a federal mask mandate could save the U.S. economy 

Executive Order D 2020 138 
July 16, 2020 
Page 2 of 4 
 
  
 
  
 
  
 
     
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
   
 
 
 
   
 
 
 
 
from taking a 5% hit to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). At this time, thirty-nine Colorado 
counties and municipalities already have mandatory mask orders in place.
 
Under Executive Order D 2020 110, mask wearing is mandatory for employees, 
contractors, and others providing services for Mass Transportation Operations and Critical 
Businesses as well as for State and county employees and the individuals they serve at 
Government Offices and Facilities. These requirements remain intact with this Executive Order.
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 
2020 092, and D 2020 110 to protect Coloradans by requiring individuals in Colorado to wear a 
non-medical face covering over their nose and mouth, subject to several exceptions.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 110 is amended as follows:
 
1.
 Strike the words “have discretion to” in Paragraph II.D.
 
2.
 After Paragraph F, add the following new Paragraphs G through R:
 
G.
 Except as permitted by Paragraphs L, M, and N, below, all individuals over ten 
(10) years old must wear a face covering over their nose and mouth when entering 
or moving within any Public Indoor Space, as such term is defined in Paragraph R 
of this Executive Order, or while using or waiting to use the services of any taxi, 
bus, light rail, train, car service, ride-sharing or similar service, or Mass 
Transportation Operations.
 
H.
 Any individual who endangers the health of others by knowingly entering or 
remaining in a Public Indoor Space, as such term is defined in Paragraph R of this 
Executive Order, in violation of the terms of this Executive Order may be subject 
to civil or criminal penalties, including but not limited to prosecution for 
trespass.
 
I.
 
Except as permitted by Paragraphs L, M, and N, below, to protect workers,
 
customers, and the community, no owner, operator, or manager of a Public Indoor 
Space, as such term is defined in Paragraph R of this Executive Order, may 
provide service to individuals or allow an individual to enter or move within that 
Public Indoor Space, unless the individual is wearing a face covering as required 
by this Executive Order.
 
J.
 
Owners, operators, or managers of Public Indoor Spaces, as such term is defined 
in Paragraph R of this Executive Order, must post signs at entrance(s) instructing 
individuals of their legal obligation under this Executive Order to wear a face 
covering when entering or moving within a Public Indoor Space.
 

Executive Order D 2020 138 
July 16, 2020 
Page 3 of 4 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
K. 
A State or local department or agency that learns that a business licensee is in 
violation of this Executive Order will consider whether the public health, safety or
 
welfare requires summary, temporary suspension of the business’s license to 
operate (including but not limited to a liquor license).
 
L. 
The following individuals are exempt from the requirements of this Executive
 
Order:
 
Individuals ten (10) years old and younger; or
 
Individuals who cannot medically tolerate a face covering.
 
M. 
Individuals performing the following activities are exempt from the requirements
 
of this Executive Order while the activity is being performed:
 
1.
 Individuals who are hearing impaired or otherwise disabled or who are 
communicating with someone who is hearing impaired or otherwise disabled 
and where the ability to see the mouth is essential to communication;
 
2.
 Individuals who are seated at a food service establishment;
 
3.
 Individuals who are exercising alone or with others from the individual’s 
household and a face covering would interfere with the activity;
 
4.
 Individuals who are receiving a personal service where the temporary removal 
of the face covering is necessary to perform the service;
 
5.
 Individuals who enter a business or receive services and are asked to 
temporarily remove a face covering for identification purposes;
 
6.
 Individuals who are actively engaged in a public safety role such as law 
enforcement, firefighters, or emergency medical personnel;
 
7.
 Individuals who are officiating at a religious service; or
 
8.
 Individuals who are giving a speech for broadcast or an audience.
 
N. 
Counties that complete certification for Protect Our Neighbors may choose to be
 
exempt from Paragraphs G through N, and R of this Executive Order.
 
O.
 Nothing in this Executive Order should be construed to prevent individuals from
 
wearing a surgical-grade mask or other, more protective face covering to cover
 
the nose and mouth if that type of mask or more protective face covering is 
appropriate under industry standards.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 138 
July 16, 2020 
Page 4 of 4 
 
  
  
 
 
   
 
 
 
 
   
 
          
  
  
   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
P.
 
Nothing in this Executive Order prevents a county or municipality from adopting 
more protective standards than those contained in this Executive Order.
 
Q. 
Except as modified by this Executive Order, all Executive Orders or Public 
Health Orders, including Public Health Order 20-31, issued due to COVID-19 and 
that are currently in effect shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
R.
 For the purposes of this Executive Order, Public Indoor Space means any 
enclosed indoor area that is publicly or privately owned, managed, or operated to 
which individuals have access by right or by invitation, expressed or implied, and 
that is accessible to the public, serves as a place of employment, or is an entity 
providing services. Public Indoor Space does not mean a person’s residence, 
including a room in a motel or hotel or a residential room for students at an 
educational facility.
 
III. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 067, and as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 092, D 2020 110, and
 
this Executive Order, 
shall expire thirty (30) days from July 16, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order.
 
In 
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 067, D 2020 092, and D 2020 110, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
sixteenth day of July, 2020
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-139
full_text:
D 2020 139
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 077 and D 2020 108 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Statutes to Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce and 
Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for Medicaid Enrollees
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 077 and D 2020 108 temporarily suspending certain statutes to increase the 
Medicaid home health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has 
undertaken a broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, 
prevent further spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to 
address the collateral consequences of the pandemic.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 077 and D 2020 108, which 
suspend certain statutes to preserve the State’s Medicaid home health workforce and 
protect Medicaid enrollees from COVID-19 by reducing the need for in person visits. I also 
temporarily suspend statutory requirements for cost sharing for COVID-19 testing and
 
treatment for Medicaid enrollees.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 139 
July 19, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 108 and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 19, 2020, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2020 108, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
nineteenth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-140
full_text:
D 2020 140
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, D 2020 078, and D 2020 112
 
Temporarily Suspending Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Criminal Justice
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 016, as amended by D 2020 043, D2020 078, and D 2020 112, concerning the 
suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D
 
2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to
 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between
 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health
 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State
 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection. On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 140 
July 19, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued
 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order D 2020 
016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive Orders 
D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire. On June 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 112,
 
which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days,
 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of
 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with
 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043 
D 2020 078, D 2020 112 and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 19, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, D 2020 078, and D 2020 112, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
nineteenth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-141
full_text:
D 2020 141
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, and D 2020 111 
Authorizing the Executive Directors of Certain State Agencies to Promulgate and 
Issue Emergency Rules Extending the Expiration Date of Licenses and Other 
Documents Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, and D 2020 111 authorizing the executive 
directors of certain State agencies to promulgate and issue emergency rules extending the 
expiration date of licenses and other documents for thirty (30) additional days due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further
 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 141 
July 19, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This Executive Order extends the expiration date of Executive Order D 2020 015, as 
amended by Executive Order D 2020 075 and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042 and D 
2020 111, for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 075 and extended 
by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 111, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from July 19, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 075 and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 042 and D 2020 111, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
nineteenth day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-142
full_text:
D 2020 142
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 091 and D 2020 123 Safer at Home
 
and in the Vast, Great Outdoors
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 091 and D 2020 123, Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved
 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for Vulnerable Individuals as 
ninety percent (90%) of Colorado fatalities are individuals older than sixty (60). Evidence shows 
that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, have 
helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured that Coloradans who needed it had access to 
healthcare resources.
 
While we have seen indications that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, 
increased transmission of the virus, and the resulting strains on our healthcare resources, 

  
Executive Order D 2020 142 
July 21, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
continue to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. As we take steps to return 
Coloradans to work, we must continue to practice Social Distancing from others at a high level 
and implement other measures, such as limiting our social interactions, extra precautions for 
Vulnerable Individuals, and wearing non-medical masks while in public to facilitate reopening 
the economy while we protect public health. As we move forward, the State continues to monitor 
the spread of COVID-19, increase our testing and tracing capabilities, and consider additional 
precautions as we learn more about the novel coronavirus and its effects on human health.
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 091 and D 2020 123 
by requiring that all licensees contained in Articles 3, 4, and 5 of Title 44 of the Colorado 
Revised Statutes must cease alcohol beverage sales to end consumers at 10:00 P.M. each day.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and extended by Executive Order
 
D 2020 123, is amended as follows:
 
1.
 Add new paragraph II.O. that states the following:
 
O.
 Effective 9:00 A.M. MDT on Thursday July 23, 2020, Pursuant to Colorado
 
Revised Statute § 24-33.5-704(7)(h), C.R.S., all licensees contained in Articles 3,
 
4, and 5 of Title 44 of the Colorado Revised Statutes must cease alcohol beverage
 
sales to end consumers at 10:00 P.M. MDT each day.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 123
 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 21, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order.
 
In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 123, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and the
 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-first day 
of July, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-143
full_text:
y 
  
 
  
 
 
D 2020 143
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Elephant Butte Fire in Jefferson County
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due the Elephant Butte Fire in Jefferson County.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damages, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.” C.R.S § 24-33.5-703(3).
 
The Elephant Butte Fire started on July 13, 2020, in Jefferson County. As of noon on Jul
 
14, 2020, the fire spread was estimated at forty-two acres, with zero percent containment, on 
both state and private land.
 
At approximately 6:05 P.M. on July 13, 2020, the Division of Fire 
Prevention and Control determined that the Elephant Butte Fire had exceeded County capacity 
per the Emergency Fire Fund criteria.
 
At approximately 6:40 P.M. on July 13, 2020, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
for the Elephant Butte Fire in Jefferson County and activated the State of Colorado Emergency 
Operations Plan.
 
Approximately seven hundred primary residences were evacuated because of the fire, 
and it had potential to spread further and impact additional values. The Elephant Butte Fire 
burned fifty-two acres and was declared 100% contained on July 18, 2020, at 9:00pm.
 
II.
 Declarations and Directives
 
A.
 The Elephant Butte Fire and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency
 
under C.R.S § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of July 13, 2020, declaring a
 
disaster emergency is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall
 
have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive
 
Order.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 143 
July 22, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
-
 
 
 
B.
 The State Emergency Operations Plan is hereby activated. All State departments
 
and agencies shall take whatever actions may be required and requested by the 
Director or Acting Director of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to 
the Emergency Support Function 5 Annex of the State Emergency Operations 
Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and equipment as necessary.
 
C.
 Due to the insufficiency of funds in the Emergency Fire Fund to pay for the 
State’s share of fire suppression and recovery efforts related to the Elephant Butte 
Fire, I authorize encumbering eight hundred fifty thousand dollars ($850,000) 
from the Disaster Emergency Fund, established in C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(2)(a), for 
costs associated with the State fire suppression and response efforts related to the 
Elephant Butte Fire since July 13, 2020. This amount is currently available in the
 
Disaster Emergency Fund.
 
D.
 Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the 
appropriate government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute 
awards, purchase orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of the
 
funds.
 
The funds described in section II(C) above shall remain available for this 
purpose until thirty-six (36) months from the date of this Executive Order, and 
any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund.
 
E.
 
The Division of Fire Prevention and Control, via its fire billing practices, is 
authorized to coordinate application for any funds available related to this disaster 
emergency. Specifically, the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control is 
directed to seek reimbursement and make payments as needed to agencies 
providing assistance on this incident.
 
III.
 Duration
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from July 13, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(C) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for thirty-six (36) months from the date of this Executive 
Order.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty
 
second day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-144
full_text:
D 2020 144
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 091, D 2020 123, and D 2020 142 Safer 
at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant

Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending

The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and

through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for Vulnerable Individuals as 
ninety percent (90%) of Colorado fatalities are individuals older than sixty (60). Evidence shows 
that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, have

While we have seen indications that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working,

Executive Order D 2020 144 
July 23, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
  
 
 
 
continue to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. As we take steps to return

This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 091, D 2020 123, 
and D 2020 142 by requiring that all on-premises licensees licensed under Articles 3, 4, or 5 of 
Title 44 of the Colorado Revised Statutes must cease alcohol beverage sales to end consumers at 
10:00 P.M. each day.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and extended by Executive
 
Orders D 2020 123 and D 2020 142, is amended as follows:
 
1.
 Replace paragraph II.O. with the following:
 
Effective 10:00 P.M. MDT on Thursday July 23, 2020, pursuant to Colorado 
Revised Statute § 24-33.5-704(7)(h), all on-premises licensees licensed under 
Articles 3, 4, or 5 of Title 44 of the Colorado Revised Statutes, including 
approved sales rooms licensed under §§ 44-3-402(2), 44-3-402(7), 44-3-
 
403(2)(e), or 44-3-407(1)(b), must cease alcohol beverage sales to end consumers

MDT but takeout alcohol beverage sales are not permitted after 10:00 P.M. 
MDT. 

 
Executive Order D 2020 144 
July 23, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
   
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 091, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 123 
and D 2020 142 and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 23, 2020, 
unless extended further by Executive Order.
 
In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 091, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 123 and D 2020 142, shall remain in full

GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State

Jared Polis

title: D 2020-145
full_text:
D 2020 145
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, and D 2020 114 Permitting the 
Limited Recommencement of Voluntary or Elective Surgeries and Procedures in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, and D 2020 114 to allow voluntary or elective surgeries and 
procedures to proceed under certain conditions.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and there is community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further
 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic.
 
On March 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 009, which ordered the temporary 
cessation of all voluntary or elective surgeries and procedures and preservation of personal 
protective equipment (PPE) and ventilators in Colorado due to the presence of COVID-19. On 
April 6, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 027 that amended and extended Executive Order 
D 2020 009. On April 26, 2020, Executive Order D 2020 009, as amended and extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 027, expired, and I issued Executive Order D 2020 045 to permit
 
voluntary or elective surgeries and procedures under certain conditions that minimize the risk of 
COVID-19 transmission to patients, healthcare workers, community members, and others by 
promoting safety and maximizing protection. On May 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 080 extending Executive Order D 2020 045 for an additional thirty (30) days. On June 24, 

 
Executive Order D 2020 145 
July 23, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
  
  
 
 
 
 
2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 114, extending Executive Orders D 2020 045 and D 2020

title: D 2020-146
full_text:
D 2020 146
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, and D 2020
 
117 Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain Requirements Preventing Issuance 
of Marriage Licenses Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as amended by D 2020 028, D2020 048, D 2020 083, and D 2020 117, concerning 
concerning the temporary suspension of certain statutes preventing the issuance of marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed, due to the presence of coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result
 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10,
 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our
 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people
 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health
 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6)
 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.
 
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary
 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage
 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19. On 

  
Executive Order D 2020 146 
July 26, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
April 6, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 028, extending Executive Order D 2020 014
 
until April 30, 2020. On April 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 048, extending
 
Executive Orders D 2020 014 and D 2020 028 for an additional thirty (30) days. On May 29,
 
2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 083, extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020
 
028, and D 2020 048 for an additional thirty (30) days. On June 27, 2020 I issued Executive 
Order D 2020 117, extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, and D 
2020 083 for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D
 
2020 083, and D 2020 117 for an additional thirty (30) days. This extension will enable 
Coloradans to obtain marriage licenses while observing social distancing guidelines and 
minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 26, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, and D 2020 117, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-sixth
 
day of July, 2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-147
full_text:
D 2020 147
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 
084, D 2020 093, and D 2020 118 Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain 
Regulatory Statutes Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, and D 2020 
118, concerning the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D
 
2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 011 for an additional 
thirty (30) days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D
 
2020 084, D 2020 093, and D 2020 118, so that the following provisions expire: Section II.A, 
concerning statutory license limitations for retailers licensed for on-premises alcohol 

  
Executive Order D 2020 147 
July 26, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
consumption and for breweries, wineries, and distilleries operating an approved sales room; and 
Section II.L, concerning in-person requirements for certain offender registration.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029,
 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, and D 2020 118, is amended as follows:
 
i.
 Sections II.A and II.L are deleted.
 
ii.
 Sections II.B, II.C, II.D, II.E, II.F, II.H, II.K, II.M, II.N, II.O, II.P, and II.Q are

days from July 26, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects,
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D

EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 053, D 2020 085, and D 2020 119 Ordering the 
Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Petition Gathering for 
Unaffiliated and Independent Candidates for Office Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 053, D 2020 085, and D 2020 119, which order the temporary suspension of 
certain regulatory statutes concerning petition collection for unaffiliated and independent 
candidates due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D
 
2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. We must take action to minimize the duration of
 
this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread 
primarily by close contact between people and through respiratory droplets when an infected 
person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or
 
maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the 
spread of COVID-19.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 053, D 2020 085 and D 2020 
119, which temporarily suspend the statutory provisions that allow collection of petitions for 
unaffiliated candidates and ensure designated election officials have adequate time to review and 
provide notification of sufficiency or insufficiency to unaffiliated candidates.

2020 085 and D 2020 119, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.

title: D 2020-149
full_text:

c 
 
D 2020 149
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 120 Directing the Colorado Department
 
of Human Services to Access Federal Funds for Child Care Services Due to the Presence of
 
COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending
 
Executive Order D 2020 120, directing the Colorado Department of Human Services to access
 
federal funds for child care services due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID
 
19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a
 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The federal Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020 (CARES Act), 
Public Law 116-136, allocated $3.5 billion to the states through the Child Care and Development
 
Block grant. These funds may be used by states for all existing allowable Child Care and 
Development Fund (CCDF) activities as well as a number of purposes related to COVID-19 and 
child care. Executive Order D 2020 007 directed the Colorado Department of Human Services
 
(CDHS) to access additional federal Child Care Development Funds to reimburse counties that
 
pay providers through the Colorado Child Care Assistance Program (CCCAP) for absences or
 
closures as a result of COVID-19 for the next eight (8) weeks, with the possibility of extension.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 149 
July 26, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 120 for an additional thirty (30)
 
days, which directs CDHS, pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(7)(b), to access remaining available
 
CCDF funds allocated by the CARES Act to continue to support child care services during the
 
COVID-19 pandemic to ensure stability and consistency for child care providers and protect the
 
vital role they play for children and families. Accessing and allocating these available federal
 
funds now will enable the State to continue to support child care services and avoid interrupting
 
support while the economic disruption caused by the pandemic continues.
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Order D 2020 120 by deleting Section II.E,
 
which is no longer necessary in light of forthcoming guidance from CDPHE.
 
II.
 Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 120 is amended as follows:
 
i.
 
Sections II.A, II.B, II.C, and II.D are extended for an additional thirty (30) days. 
ii.
 
Section II.E is deleted
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 120, as amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall
 
expire thirty (30) days from July 26, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all
 
other respects Executive Order D 2020 120 shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.
  
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
twenty-sixth day of July, 
2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10,

My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide

title: D 2020-151
full_text:
-
 
 
D 2020 151
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 056, D 2020 090, and D 2020 122 Ordering the 
Suspension of a Relevant Statute to Allow Food Trucks to Operate at Colorado’s Rest
 
Areas to Support the Movement of Commercial Vehicle Activities Due to the Presence 
of COVID-19 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 056, D 2020 090, and D 2020 122, which orders the suspension of a relevant 
statute to allow food trucks to operate at Colorado’s rest areas to support the movement of 
commercial vehicle activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125. On March 25, 2020, I requested that
 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
Commercial truck drivers play a critical role in the supply chain and in the COVID
 
19 emergency response. Truckers transport essential supplies including equipment, 
medicine, food, and personal protective equipment across our State and the country. 
Normal enforcement of restrictions pertaining to commercial activities at highway rest and 
refueling areas stand in the way of accessible, affordable, fresh food options for truck 
drivers operating in Colorado. Other states are allowing food trucks to operate at rest stops
 
during this emergency so that commercial truck drivers have easy and reliable access to
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 151 
July 28, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
nutritious, affordable, and freshly prepared meals, and, as a centrally located state, 
Colorado trucking routes are critical to the national supply chain. We therefore must take 
action to support our hard working truckers and increase the availability of freshly prepared 
food options while providing additional economic opportunities during this COVID-19 
crisis to food truck operators. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 056, D 2020 090, and D
 
2020 122 for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 056, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 090, D 2020
 
122, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from July 28, 2020, unless
 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 056, as
 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 090 and D 2020 122, shall remain in full force and 
effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand 
and the Executive Seal of 
the State of Colorado, this
 
twenty-eighth day of July, 
2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-152
full_text:
- 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
D 2020 152 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125 Declaring a Disaster 
Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, and D 2020 125 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds 
for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 

Executive Order D 2020 152 
August 4, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D2020 125 to extend the state of disaster emergency 
and the deployment of the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to 
State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State. 
Additionally, this Executive Order increases the amount of funds previously made available to: 
CDPHE by forty-nine million dollars ($49,000,000); the Colorado Governor’s Office of 
Information and Technology by two hundred fifty-four thousand four hundred forty dollars 
($254,440); and the Colorado Department of Corrections by twelve million eight hundred eight 
thousand six hundred eighty-five dollars ($12,808,685), for COVID-19 response activities. The 
funds made available for response activities in this Executive Order were transferred to the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund by Executive Order D 
2020 070. Executive Order D 2020 070 transferred forty-eight million dollars ($48,000,000) in 
FY 2019-20 and one hundred fifty-seven million dollars ($157,000,000) at the beginning of FY 
2020-21 from the CARES Act Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund Coronavirus Relief Fund 
sub-account. 
II. 
Amendment 
1. Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 
018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, and D 2020 125 is amended and 
restated as follows: 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed 
insufficient pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b) and twenty-three million 
dollars ($23,000,000) was transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust 
Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund. Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I 
order that two hundred eighty-eight million one hundred one thousand seven 
hundred seventy-three dollars ($288,101,773) from the Disaster Emergency 
Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these 
funds, two hundred million one hundred one thousand seven hundred seventy- 
three dollars ($200,101,773) shall come from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub- 
account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds coming from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred sixty-one million two 
hundred 
seventy-five 
thousand 
three 
hundred 
sixty-four 
dollars 
($161,275,364) shall be encumbered and made available to CDPHE for 
response activities related to COVID-19; twenty-three million one hundred 
sixty-nine thousand four hundred twenty-six dollars ($23,169,426) shall be 
encumbered and made available to the Department of Public Safety for 
response activities related to COVID-19; one million one-hundred fifty-nine 
thousand eight hundred ninety dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and 

  
Executive Order D 2020 152 
August 4, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
            
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
made available to the Colorado Governor’s Office of Information and 
Technology for response activities related to COVID-19; one million six 
hundred eighty-eight thousand four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408) shall 
be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Agriculture 
for response activities related to COVID-19; and twelve million eight hundred 
eight thousand six hundred eighty-five dollars ($12,808,685) shall be 
encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Corrections 
for response activities related to COVID-19. These funds shall remain 
available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 30, 2020, 
and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
III. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from August 4, 2020, unless amended further by Executive Order, except 
that the funds described in Section II.C shall remain available for the described purposes and the 
described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 
109, and D 2020 125, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fourth day of August, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order
 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, and D 2020 126, ordering the
 
temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile justice, regional centers,
 
and behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have
 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster
 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive
 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D
 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the
 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the
 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to
 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between
 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health
 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6)
 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to
 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals
 
will return.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 153 
August 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
-
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain
 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including
 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable
 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of
 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.
 
On May 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 060, amending and extending
 
Executive Order D 2020 034 to temporarily suspend an additional statute so that the Division of
 
Youth Services could take further action to prevent the spread of COVID-19 among its youth 
population. 
On June 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 094, extending Executive Orders D
 
2020 034 and D 2020 060 for an additional thirty (30) days and amending those Executive
 
Orders so that two provisions expired concerning the juvenile parole board and juvenile
 
commitment.
 
On July 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 126, extending Sections II.A, II.C, 
II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I of Executive Orders D 2020 034 and D 2020 060, for an additional
 
thirty (30) days, and making a technical amendment to Section II.I in conformity with SB 19
 
108, effective July 1, 2020.
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A, II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I for an 
additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from August 6, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, and D 2020 126, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this sixth
 
day of August, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-154
full_text:
D 2020 154
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 127 Protect Our Neighbors 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 127 due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I. 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result
 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, and D 2020 152. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide
 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way
 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at risk of severe
 
illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive
 
Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured that Coloradans 
who needed it had access to healthcare resources. 
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044, as amended, and the Safer at 
Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive Order 2020 091, as amended, my
 
administration took steps to return Coloradans to work while continuing the efforts to “flatten the
 
curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home and Safer at Home and in the Vast, 
Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to continue to practice Social Distancing from 
others at a high level and implement other measures, such as limited social interactions, taking
 

Executive Order D 2020 154 
August 7, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
-
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
    
 
 
  
 
-
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
extra precautions for individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wearing non
 
medical masks while in public to facilitate reopening the economy. 
As the number of outbreaks decreases in a particular area, we will move into the next 
phase: Protect Our Neighbors. Local communities that meet certain qualifications can apply to
 
become a Protect Our Neighbors Community. These communities will have: strong underlying
 
public health and health care capacity that can respond to larger outbreaks; strong compliance
 
with statewide standards; and the ability to enforce guidelines and public health orders. 
If a community cannot maintain the required metrics for Protect Our Neighbors, the State
 
will return them to the requirements of Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors. Because
 
each community and each part of our State has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows 
communities to create unique and flexible plans within the statewide guidelines. Even as we
 
continue to open our economy through Protect Our Neighbors, we must continue to protect and 
support people who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19, including
 
individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19 such as older adults and people with 
underlying medical conditions.
 
On July 9, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 127, implementing a number of 
measures that will allow local communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still
 
protecting the public’s health. 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 127 for an additional 
thirty (30) days. 
II. 
Directives
 
Section II.B of Executive Order 2020 127 is amended to read as follows:
 
B.
 For purposes of this Executive Order, individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID
 
19 means:
 
1.
 Individuals who are sixty-five (65) years and older;
 
2.
 Individuals who have cancer;
 
3.
 Individuals who have chronic kidney disease;
 
4.
 Individuals who have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease;
 
5.
 Individuals who are immunocompromised;
 
6.
 Individual who have a body mass index of 30 or higher; 
7.
 Individuals who have serious heart conditions, such as heart failure, coronary
 
artery disease, or cardiomyopathies;
 
8.
 Individuals who have Sickle cell disease; 

 
Executive Order D 2020 154 
August 7, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
9.
 Individuals who have Type 2 diabetes mellitus; and
 
10.
 
Individuals determined to be high risk by a licensed healthcare provider. 
III. 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 127, as amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall
 
expire thirty (30) days from August 7, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all
 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 127 shall remain in full force and effect as originally
 
promulgated.  
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
seventh day of August, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-155
full_text:
D 2020 155
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 005, D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 
2020 061, D 2020 095, and D 2020 129 Concerning Limiting In-Person Contact for the 
2020 Elections and the Secretary of State’s Operations Due to the Presence of
 
COVID-19 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending
 
extending Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 008 and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129 
concerning changes to 2020 election activity to limit in-person contact while allowing the 
elections to proceed safely and without interruption to the Secretary of State’s operations 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D
 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152. On March 25, 2020, I
 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 
2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a
 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. We must continue to take action to 
minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that 
causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and through 
respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6)
 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.
 

Executive Order D 2020 155 
August 8, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 005, as 
amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 
095, and D 2020 129, for thirty (30) days from July 10, 2020. This extension will allow the 
2020 election activity to continue safely and without interruption while minimizing the risk 
of spreading COVID-19. 
II. 
Amendments
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, and D 2020 129, is amended to include 
the following:
 
K.
 I hereby direct that the major parties make any changes necessary to their rules to comply
 
with restrictions that may arise due to the disaster emergency declared in Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152, including but not
 
limited to, changes to proxy or quorum requirements, and that any such changes to party
 
rules can be done outside the existing political party rules process.
 
L.
 I temporarily suspend the requirements of C.R.S. 1-3-101 et seq., 1-4-1001 et seq. and 1-
 
12-201 et seq. for political parties to conduct in-person meetings or transactions to allow 
the parties to conduct party business by remote participation.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 155 
August 8, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, 
D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129 and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty
 
(30) days from August 8, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, D 
2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, and D 2020 129, shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this eighth 
day of August, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-156
full_text:
D 2020 156
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, and D 2020 130 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Statute Concerning Bans on Open Burning
 
Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, and D 2020 130 ordering the temporary
 
suspension of statute concerning bans on open burning due to the presence of coronavirus
 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, and D 2020 152.
 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The
 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a
 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The response to COVID-19 is straining State, local, and federal resources. Large
 
wildfires require an inter-agency response that relies on local, State, and federal cooperation and 
coordination. Given the current strain on government resources due to COVID-19, any wildfire
 
response would be diminished, which risks enabling a fire to grow and spread, which in turn
