Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 056, D 2020 090, D 2020 122, and D 2020 151, which orders the suspension of a 
relevant statute to allow food trucks to operate at Colorado’s rest areas to support the movement 
of commercial vehicle activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 
2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
Commercial truck drivers play a critical role in the supply chain and in the COVID-
19 emergency response. Truckers transport essential supplies including equipment, 
medicine, food, and personal protective equipment across our State and the country. 
Normal enforcement of restrictions pertaining to commercial activities at highway rest and 
refueling areas stands in the way of accessible, affordable, fresh food options for truck 
drivers operating in Colorado. Other states are allowing food trucks to operate at rest stops 

Executive Order D 2020 174 
August 26, 2020 
Page 2 of 2  
during this emergency so that commercial truck drivers have easy and reliable access to 
nutritious, affordable, and freshly prepared meals, and, as a centrally located state, 
Colorado trucking routes are critical to the national supply chain. We therefore must take 
action to support our hard working truckers and increase the availability of freshly prepared 
food options while providing additional economic opportunities during this COVID-19 
crisis to food truck operators.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 056, D 2020 090, D 2020 
122, and D 2020 151 for an additional thirty (30) days. 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 056, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 090, D 2020
122, D 2020 151, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from August 26, 
2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 056, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 090, D 2020 122, and D 2020 151, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-sixth day of August, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-175
full_text:
Jared Polis Governor, Seal of the State of Colorado 1876, 136 State Capitol Denver, Colorado, 80203, Tel 303-866-2471 Fax 303-866 2003.
D 2020 175
EXECUTIVE ORDER
Directing the Department of Personnel and Administration to Lead State Action on  
Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion for the State of Colorado
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order directing the Department 
of Personnel and Administration to lead State action on equity, diversity, and inclusion for the 
State of Colorado.
I. 
Background and Purpose
The State of Colorado believes that an equitable, diverse, and inclusive workplace is one 
where all employees and community partners, whatever their gender, race, ethnicity, religion, 
national origin, age, sexual orientation, gender identity, citizenship status, education, disability, 
socio-economic status, or any other identity, feel valued and respected. We are committed to 
nondiscriminatory practices and providing equitable opportunities for employment and 
advancement in all of our departments, programs, services, and worksites. We respect and value 
diverse life experiences and work to ensure that all voices are heard so that a person’s future 
success is not determined by their identity.
We will only reach our potential as a state when all Coloradans can live, work, learn, 
play, and thrive in healthy, inclusive, and equitable environments. We do not build walls of 
exclusion in Colorado – we build ladders of opportunity. To achieve our goal of recognizing, 
embracing, and celebrating all identities and abilities, Colorado is committed to working together 
and leading the nation in equity, diversity and inclusion (EDI) efforts. Therefore, EDI are core 
values and top priorities to realize our vision of a Colorado For All.
II. 
Declarations and Directives
Recognizing the importance of creating an equitable, diverse, and inclusive environment 
that allows our workforce to thrive and the State to be an employer of choice, I hereby direct the 
Department of Personnel & Administration (DPA) to take the following immediate actions:
A.
Develop an EDI Universal Policy to guide and direct State agencies in creating  
long-term strategic plans with the goal of inclusive, anti-discriminatory workplace 
cultures, and implementing equitable hiring, compensation, and retention  
practices.

Executive Order D 2020 175
August 27, 2020
Page 2 of 3
B.
Develop and deliver required training for all employees on equity, diversity, and 
inclusion, including specific EDI training for State supervisors and executive  
leaders.
C.
To ensure accountability, create a proposal for an operational plan to support,  
coordinate, and oversee statewide EDI initiatives, and develop a reporting  
template and procedure for agencies to publicly report on progress on, and build 
trust  
in, their EDI initiatives.
D.
In partnership with the Colorado Equity Alliance and other relevant stakeholder  
groups, create statewide standards of accessibility to guide agencies in ensuring  
State buildings, systems, vital documents, community meetings, and other 
communications and resources, including websites, are accessible to all  
Coloradans, regardless of ability or language. Where applicable, these standards  
shall be at or above the standards required in the Americans with Disabilities Act  
and the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act.
E.
Using the findings of the procurement disparity study commissioned by Senate  
Bill 19-135, guide agencies in addressing systemic inequities posed by  
contracting barriers.
In order to achieve our vision of a Colorado For All and equity for all, all State agencies  
must play a role in incorporating an equity mindset in their work. State agencies are directed to  
take a coordinated approach, led by DPA, to operationalize equity in systems, policies, and  
practices, at minimum to include the following:
F.
State agency executive leadership will ensure employee participation, including  
their executive leadership teams, in DPA’s required training and support internal 
educational training regarding implicit bias, historical injustices and trauma,  
community engagement practices, assessment tools, and other appropriate equity- 
related topics as they relate to their work.
G.
All agencies will have representation on the Colorado Equity Alliance to align  
and implement consistent EDI tools and support cross-agency EDI goals. All  
agencies will create and continuously update a long-term plan to identify and  
address barriers as well as metrics to evaluate progress such as Governor’s Office 
dashboard measures, agency measures, and/or Employee Engagement survey  
measures. As part of their annual performance plans pursuant to SMART Act  
(C.R.S. § 2-7-204), each Executive Director will provide an annual report to the 
Governor’s Office and DPA. Each agency, in addition to presenting to the  
General Assembly on topics pursuant to C.R.S. § 2-7-203, shall also present any  
specific efforts and progress in addressing EDI within their agency. Additionally, 
Executive Directors may consider designating staff to lead and coordinate these 
activities.

Executive Order D 2020 175
August 27, 2020
Page 3 of 3
H.
Agencies shall respect and value life experiences from community residents to  
ensure that State agency staff hear diverse perspectives and actively build  
relationships with a goal of full participation. This includes involving community 
partners in decision-making from the beginning to end of projects, as well as  
measuring diversity and inclusion efforts on State boards and commissions  
appointed by the Governor’s Office.
I.
Agencies shall review, acknowledge, and dismantle any inequities within agency 
policies, systems, programs, and services, and continually update and report  
agency progress. Agencies shall respond to any systemic procurement equity  
barriers as identified by DPA’s statewide procurement study.
J.
Executive Directors shall engage in Cabinet-level discussion about systemic  
inequities, how they impact the State’s work and outcomes for all Coloradans, and 
actively facilitate change to create a culture of anti-discriminatory behaviors  
through engagement of agency leadership teams.
K.
Agencies shall adopt and maintain DPA’s standards of accessibility for all State 
buildings, systems, vital documents, community meetings, and other resources  
available to Coloradans.
L.
Agencies shall begin to implement these directives within existing budgets and 
authorities. If necessary, agencies shall engage with OSPB to identify resource 
requirements and incorporate those requirements into the annual budget  
development process for any actions to be implemented in future fiscal years.
III. 
Duration
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor.
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-seventh 
day of August, 2020.
Jared Polis  
Governor

title: D 2020-176
full_text:
D 2020 176 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018,  
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152 Declaring a 
Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 
125, and D 2020 152 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for 
response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  

Executive Order D 2020 176 
September 2, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152 to extend the state of disaster 
emergency and the deployment of the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning 
resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State.  
 
II.  
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and this Executive 
Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from September 2, 2020, unless amended further by 
Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C. shall remain available for the 
described purposes and the described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, and D 2020 152, shall remain in full force and effect 
as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
second day of September, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-177
full_text:
D 2020 177 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, and D 2020 
153 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning 
Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, and Behavioral Health Due to the Presence of COVID-19  
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, and D 2020 
153, ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile justice, 
regional centers, and behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-
19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152 and D 2020 176. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  
 

Executive Order D 2020 177 
September 4, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.  
 
On May 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 060, amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 034 to temporarily suspend an additional statute so that the Division of 
Youth Services could take further action to prevent the spread of COVID-19 among its youth 
population.  
 
On June 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 094, extending Executive Orders D 
2020 034 and D 2020 060 for an additional thirty (30) days and amending those Executive 
Orders so that two provisions expired concerning the juvenile parole board and juvenile 
commitment.  
 
On July 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 126, extending Sections II.A, II.C, 
II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I of Executive Orders D 2020 034 and D 2020 060, for an additional 
thirty (30) days, and making a technical amendment to Section II.I in conformity with SB 19-
108, effective July 1, 2020. 
 
On August 6, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 153, extending Sections II.A, II.C, 
II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I of Executive Orders D 2020 034 and D 2020 060, for an additional 
thirty (30) days, to enable CDHS and the Division of Youth Services to continue to respond to 
the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of COVID-19 in their 
facilities.  
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A, II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I for an 
additional thirty (30) days. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 177 
September 4, 2020 
Page 3 of 3  
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from September 4, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, D 
2020 094, D 2020 126, and D 2020 153 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fourth 
day of September, 2020 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-178
full_text:
D 2020 178 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 127 and D 2020 154 Protect Our Neighbors  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 127 and D 2020 154 due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-
19) in Colorado. 
 
I.       Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice Social 
Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at risk of severe 
illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at Home Executive 
Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured that Coloradans 
who needed it had access to healthcare resources.  
 
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044, as amended, and the Safer at 
Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive Order 2020 091, as amended, my 
administration took steps to return Coloradans to work while continuing the efforts to “flatten the 

Executive Order D 2020 178 
September 5, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home and Safer at Home and in the Vast, 
Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to continue to practice Social Distancing from 
others at a high level and implement other measures, such as limited social interactions, taking 
extra precautions for individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wearing non-
medical masks while in public to facilitate reopening the economy.  
 
As the number of outbreaks decreases in a particular area, we will move into the next 
phase: Protect Our Neighbors. Local communities that meet certain qualifications can apply to 
become a Protect Our Neighbors Community. These communities will have: strong underlying 
public health and health care capacity that can respond to larger outbreaks; strong compliance 
with statewide standards; and the ability to enforce guidelines and public health orders.  
 
If a community cannot maintain the required metrics for Protect Our Neighbors, the State 
will return them to the requirements of Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors. Because 
each community and each part of our State has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows 
communities to create unique and flexible plans within the statewide guidelines. Even as we 
continue to open our economy through Protect Our Neighbors, we must continue to protect and 
support people who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19, including 
individuals at risk of severe illness from COVID-19 such as older adults and people with 
underlying medical conditions. 
 
On July 9, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 127, implementing a number of 
measures that will allow local communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still 
protecting the public’s health.  On August 7, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 154, 
amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 127.  
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 127, as amended and 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 154, for an additional thirty (30) days.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 178 
September 5, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
II.   
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 127, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 154, 
and as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from September 5, 2020, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
127, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 154, shall remain in full force and 
effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fifth 
day of September, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-179
full_text:
D 2020 179 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 005, D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 
2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, and D 2020 155 Concerning Limiting In-Person 
Contact for the 2020 Elections and the Secretary of State’s Operations Due to the 
Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, and D 2020 155 concerning 
changes to 2020 election activity to limit in-person contact while allowing the elections to 
proceed safely and without interruption to the Secretary of State’s operations due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On March 
25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the 
State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. We must continue to take action to 
minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that 
causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and through 
respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 
 

Executive Order D 2020 179 
September 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 005 for an 
additional thirty (30) days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, D 
2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, and D 2020 155.  This amendment and 
extension will allow 2020 election activity to continue safely and without interruption 
while minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19.  
 
II.  
Amendments 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, and D 2020 155, is 
amended to include the following: 
 
 
M. 
I temporarily suspend the requirements of C.R.S. §§ 1-12-203 and 1-12-206 
for any notarization or handwritten signature in any rules, regulations, or 
forms regarding the certification or acceptance of nomination, selection, or 
appointment. 
 
III. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from September 6, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended 
and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 
2020 129, and D 2020 155, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this sixth 
day of September, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-180
full_text:
D 2020 180
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, and D 2020 
156 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Statute Concerning Bans on Open Burning
 
Due to the Presence of COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, and D 2020 156, ordering the 
temporary suspension of statute concerning bans on open burning due to the presence of
 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of
 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect
 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The response to COVID-19 is straining State, local, and federal resources. Large
 
wildfires require an inter-agency response that relies on local, State, and federal cooperation and 
coordination. Given the current strain on government resources due to COVID-19, any wildfire
 
response would be diminished, which risks enabling a fire to grow and spread, which in turn 
requires even more resources for firefighting. Given the State’s need to focus on COVID-19 
mitigation efforts, it is imperative that the State take every action to reduce the risk of wildfire.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 180 
September 7, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
  
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
   
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
On April 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 037, temporarily suspending statute
 
concerning bans on open burning to give boards of county commissioners broader discretion and 
more flexibility to implement restrictions on open burning that will decrease the risk of fire and
 
enable firefighters and other first responders to participate in the COVID-19 response efforts
 
throughout the State. I extended Executive Order D 2020 037 on May 14, 2020, June 12, 2020, 
July 11 2020, and August 9, 2020. 
On August 19, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 168, declaring that conditions of
 
extreme fire hazard exist and ordering temporary fire restrictions in the State of Colorado.
 
That
 
Executive Order does not supersede more comprehensive or inclusive fire or open burning
 
restrictions that have been or may be established by Colorado counties, municipalities, or other
 
political subdivisions of the State.
 
Given the continued fire risk in the State and the continued strain on government
 
resources due to COVID-19, this Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 
2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, and D 2020 156, for an additional thirty (30) days.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 037, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 062, D 2020 
096, D 2020 130, D 2020 156, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from
 
September 7, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive
 
Order D 2020 037, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, and 
D 2020 156, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this
 
seventh day of September, 
2020
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-181

D 2020 181
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 098, D 2020 132, and D 2020 157 Providing Relief to Public 
Utility Customers affected by COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant portions of the 
Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of
 
Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by
 
Executive Orders D 2020 132
 
and D 2020 157, for an additional thirty (30) days, providing relief to 
public utility customers to mitigate, respond to, and recover from the current economic disruption due to
 
the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s (CDPHE) 
public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since
 
then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have evidence of community spread 
throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 
2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 
018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State 
of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide array of
 
actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect against overwhelming
 
our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the economic 
stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 
necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the 
economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, employment, 
community cohesion, and community recovery.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 132 and D 2020 157, which temporarily provides relief and State support to public utility customers 
to mitigate, respond to, and recover from the current economic disruption due to the presence of COVID
 
19 in Colorado.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 181 
September 7, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
     
 
         
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from September 7, 2020, unless extended further by
 
Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Order D
 
2020 132 and D 2020 157, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
seventh day of September, 
2020.
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-183
full_text:
D 2020 183
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, and D 2020 
160 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for Medicaid and 
Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, and D 2020 160, temporarily suspending certain 
statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in Medicaid and the Children’s Basic
 
Health Plan.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have
 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the
 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the
 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a
 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further
 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral
 
consequences of the pandemic.
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid
 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they
 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive
 
Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, and D 2020 160, which ensures compliance with the Families
 
First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending certain statutes to allow for
 
continued eligibility or benefits status for those individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 

  
Executive Order D 2020 183 
September 8, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
            
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, and directs the Executive Director of the
 
Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees
 
for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic Health Plan.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 2020 136, 
D 2020 160 and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from September 8, 2020, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, 
as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 2020 136, and D 2020 160, shall remain in full
 
force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this eighth
 
 
day of September, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

full_text:

D 2020 184
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, and D 2020 161 Ordering Expedited 
Unemployment Insurance Claim Processing to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant portions of the 
Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of
 
Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by
 
Executive Orders D 2020 133 and D 2020 161, for an additional thirty (30) days, ordering expedited
 
unemployment insurance (UI) claim processing due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID
 
19)
 
in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s (CDPHE)
 
public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since 
then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have evidence of community spread 
throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 
2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 
018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, and D 2020 176. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State 
of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide array of
 
actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect against overwhelming 
our health care resources.
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the economic 
stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 
necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the 
economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, employment,
 
community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications that our efforts to “flatten 
the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and
 
livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely on unemployment insurance to meet their basic needs.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D
 
2020 133 and D 2020 161, for an additional thirty (30) days, which expedites processing of
 
unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will help protect the economic well-being of
 
Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19.
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 184 
September 8, 2020

. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
II.
 
Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133 and D 2020 161, and

Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Order D 
2020 133 and D 2020 161, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
 
GIVEN under my hand and
