title: D 2020-267
full_text:
. 
D 2020 267
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 115 and D 2020 246 to Designate State’s Prosecutor
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 115 and D 2020 246 to further define the breadth and scope of the Attorney 
General’s authority to investigate and prosecute offenses arising from the August 24, 2019 
encounter with Elijah McClain in the City of Aurora, Adams County, Colorado and/or his 
subsequent death.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On June 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 115, finding that the death of Elijah 
McClain is a matter of statewide importance and that it is necessary for the Attorney General to 
act as the State’s prosecutor, investigate, and—if the evidence permits—prosecute potential 
criminal activity by individuals that caused the death of Elijah McClain.
 
On November 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 246, which amended Executive 
Order D 2020 115 to further define the breadth and scope of the Attorney General’s authority to 
investigate and prosecute offenses arising from this matter.
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 115 and D 2020 246 to clarify 
the amendment in Executive Order D 2020 246.
 
II. 
Amendment
 
Section II.A of Executive Order D 2020 115, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 
246, is amended as follows:
 
1.
 Replace Section II.A. with the following:
 
I appoint Phil Weiser, Attorney General for the State of Colorado, or his duly 
authorized successor, to be the State’s prosecutor and direct him to take all
 
necessary actions to investigate, on behalf of the State of Colorado, offenses, 
including criminal offenses, arising from the August 24, 2019 encounter with 
Elijah McClain and/or his subsequent death, and, if deemed necessary, prosecute 
any persons, including law enforcement or any other individual, for such offenses
 

 
Executive Order D 2020 267 
December 2, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
The Attorney General may utilize a State grand jury pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 13-73-101(2) if and as he deems appropriate, and may appoint special
 
assistant attorneys general, and utilize other resources to assist in the fulfillment
 
of this Executive Order. To the greatest extent possible, the Attorney General may
 
work with the City of Aurora on any independent investigation it undertakes into
 
the death of Elijah McClain to ensure accountability and learn critical lessons so
 
that we can prevent these tragedies in the future.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 115, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 246 and this 
Executive Order, shall expire upon completion of the Attorney General’s investigation and any 
prosecution related to the matters herein, unless modified or terminated by further Executive 
Order of the Governor. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 115, as amended by 
Executive Order D 2020 246, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this 
second day of December, 
2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-268
full_text:
D 2020 268
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, and
 
D 2020 264 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease
 
2019 in Colorado
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant
 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive
 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, and D 2020 264 
declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for response activities due
 
to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I. 
Background and Purpose
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent
 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any
 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. 
§ 24-33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread 
from person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is
 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as
 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the
 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date
 

Executive Order D 2020 268 
 
 
December 3, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
-
 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize
 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives.
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, and D 2020 264 to increase the amount of funds previously made 
available to CDPHE by eighty-four million three hundred thousand dollars ($84,300,000); and to 
transfer eighty-four million three hundred thousand dollars ($84,300,000) to the Disaster
 
Emergency Fund from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund.
 
II.
 Amendment
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive
 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, and D 2020 264 is amended to 
read as follows:
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient
 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred sixty seven million eight
 
hundred thousand dollars ($167,800,000) was transferred from the Controlled 
Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 related 
purposes. Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that four hundred thirty seven 
million eight hundred thousand dollars ($437,800,000) from the Disaster Emergency
 
Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these funds, two 
hundred five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the Coronavirus Relief
 
Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds coming from the
 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred sixty one million two hundred 
seventy five thousand and three hundred sixty four dollars ($161,275,364) shall be
 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19;
 
twenty three million one hundred sixty nine thousand and four hundred twenty six dollars
 
($23,169,426) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Public
 
Safety for response activities related to COVID-19;
 
twelve million eight hundred eight
 
thousand and six hundred eighty five dollars ($12,808,685) shall be encumbered and 
made available to the Colorado Department of Corrections for response activities related
 
to COVID-19; four million eight hundred ninety eight thousand and two hundred twenty 
seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado 
Department of Labor and Employment for response activities related to COVID; one
 
million one hundred fifty nine thousand and eight hundred ninety dollars ($1,159,890)
 
shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Governor’s Office of
 
Information and Technology for response activities related to COVID-19; and one
 
million six hundred eighty eight thousand and four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408)
 
shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Agriculture for
 
response activities related to COVID-19. Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub
 
account of the Disaster Emergency Fund shall remain available for expenses incurred
 
from March 1, 2020 to December 30, 2020, and any unexpended funds shall remain in 

  
Executive Order D 2020 268 
 
 
December 3, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
  
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
the Disaster Emergency Fund. All other funds from the Disaster Emergency Fund shall
 
be available until expended.
 
III.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and this Executive Order, shall expire on 
December 27, 2020, unless amended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described
 
in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available
 
for the described purposes and the described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other
 
respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders
 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, 
D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, and D 2020 264 shall remain in full force
 
and effect as originally promulgated.
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this third
 
day of December, 2020.
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-269
full_text:
D 2020 269
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, 
D 2020 156, D 2020 180, D 2020 210, and D 2020 238 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Statute Concerning Bans on Open Burning Due to the Presence of
 
COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 037, D 2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, D 2020 156, D 2020 180, 
D 2020 210, and D 2020 238 ordering the temporary suspension of statute concerning bans on 
open burning due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.
 
The response to COVID-19 is straining State, local, and federal resources. Large 
wildfires require an inter-agency response that relies on local, State, and federal cooperation and 
coordination. Given the current strain on government resources due to COVID-19, any wildfire 
response would be diminished, which risks enabling a fire to grow and spread, which in turn 
requires even more resources for firefighting. Given the State’s need to focus on COVID-19 
mitigation efforts, it is imperative that the State take every action to reduce the risk of wildfire.
 

  
Executive Order D 2020 269 
 
 
December 3, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
   
 
  
 
 
 
   
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
On April 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 037, temporarily suspending statut
 
concerning bans on open burning to give boards of county commissioners broader discretion and 
more flexibility to implement restrictions on open burning that will decrease the risk of fire and 
enable firefighters and other first responders to participate in the COVID-19 response efforts 
throughout the State. I extended Executive Order D 2020 037 on May 14, 2020, June 12, 2020, 
July 11, 2020, August 9, 2020, September 7, 2020, October 6, 2020, and November 4, 2020.
 
On August 19, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 168, declaring that conditions of
 
extreme fire hazard exist and ordering temporary fire restrictions in the State of Colorado. On 
September 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 196, extending D 2020 168 until 
September 30, 2020.
 
Those Executive Orders did not supersede more comprehensive or 
inclusive fire or open burning restrictions that have been or may be established by Colorado 
counties, municipalities, or other political subdivisions of the State.
 
Given the continued fire risk in the State and the continued strain on government 
resources due to COVID-19, this Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 037, D 
2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, D 2020 156, D 2020 180, D 2020 210, and D 2020 238 for 
an additional thirty (30) days.
 
e 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 037, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 062, D 2020 
096, D 2020 130, D 2020 156, D 2020 180, D 2020 210, D 2020 238, and this Executive Order, 
shall expire thirty (30) days from December 3, 2020, unless extended further by Executive 
Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 037, as extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 062, D 2020 096, D 2020 130, D 2020 156, D 2020 180, D 2020 210, and D 2020 238 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thir
day of December, 2020. 
d 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-270
full_text:
D 2020 270
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 098, D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, and D 2020
 
211, and D 2020 239 Providing Relief to Public Utility Customers affected by COVID-19
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, D 2020 
211, and D 2020 239 for an additional thirty (30) days, providing relief to public utility 
customers to mitigate, respond to, and recover from the current economic disruption due to the
 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
 
I.
 
Background and Purpose
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. The economic impacts of COVID-19 are 
significant, and threaten to undermine the economic stability of many Coloradans and local 
businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple 
businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. 
We must take action to shore up economic security, employment, community cohesion, and 
community recovery.
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, D 2020 211, and D 2020 239 which temporarily 

  
Executive Order D 2020 270 
December 3, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
 
 
 
 
provides relief and State support to public utility customers to mitigate, respond to, and recover 
from the current economic disruption due to the presence of COVID19 in Colorado.
 
II.
 Duration
 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157,
 
D 2020 181, D 2020 211, D 2020 239, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from December 3, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 
181, D 2020 211, and D 2020 239 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the
 
State of Colorado, this third 
day of December, 2020.
 
Jared Polis
 
Governor

title: D 2020-271
full_text:
D 2020 271 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 
131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, and D 2020 240 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Statutes and Rules to Expand the Healthcare Workforce for 
Hospitals and Other Inpatient Treatment Facilities Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 
2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, and D 2020 240, ordering the 
temporary suspension of certain statutes and rules to expand the healthcare workforce for 
hospitals and other inpatient treatment facilities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
058, D 2020 064, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.    
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, 
D 2020 212, and D 2020 240, for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure that inpatient healthcare 
facilities have sufficient resources and personnel to treat patients suffering from COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2020 271 
 
 
December 3, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
II.     Amendments 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, 
D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, and D 2020 240, is amended as 
follows:  
1. Replace Section III.E with the following:  
I direct the Executive Director of DORA, through the Director of DPO, to promulgate 
and issue temporary emergency rules providing for the temporary licensure of nurses who 
hold valid nursing licenses in good standing from other states, regardless of whether the 
issuing state is a participant with Colorado in a licensing compact, and the temporary 
certification of nurse aides without a written examination or skills-based examination. 
III.     Duration 
         Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, 
D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, and this Executive 
Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 3, 2020, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 2020 038, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097,  D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, 
D 2020 240, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this third 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-272
full_text:
D 2020 272 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, 
D 2020 214, and D 2020 241 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for 
Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, and D 2020 
241 temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in 
Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, and D 2020 264. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States 
declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President 
approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, and D 2020 241 which 
ensures compliance with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily 

Executive Order D 2020 272 
 
 
December 4, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
suspending certain statutes to allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those 
individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, 
and directs the Executive Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing 
(HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic 
Health Plan. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 2020 136, 
D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from December 4, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other 
respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 2020 136, 
D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, and D 2020 241, shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fourth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-273
full_text:
D 2020 273 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, and D 2020 
215, and D 2020 242 Ordering Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim Processing to 
Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 
215, and D 2020 242 for an additional thirty (30) days, ordering expedited unemployment 
insurance (UI) claim processing due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID19) in 
Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 
076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
and D 2020 264. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications 
that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten 

Executive Order D 2020 273 
 
 
December 4, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely on unemployment 
insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, and D 2020 242 for an additional 
thirty (30) days, which expedites processing of unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans 
and will help protect the economic well-being of Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-
19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, 
D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from December 4, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, and D 2020 242 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fourth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-274
full_text:
D 2020 274 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, and D 2020 243 Temporarily 
Suspending Statute to Facilitate Learning Pods Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in the 
State of Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, and D 2020 243 temporarily suspending statute to facilitate 
learning pods due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
In light of COVID-19, many school districts across the State are utilizing remote 
instruction in place of, or in conjunction with, in-person learning. In response, some families are 
forming groups to facilitate child supervision and remote learning during the school day 
(“learning pods”).  
State child care licensing law generally requires licensing for anyone regularly caring for 
or supervising more than four (4) unrelated children. To allow for more school-age children to be 
served in licensed child care, I encourage the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS) 
to grant waivers of sections 7.707, 7.702, and 7.712 under 12 CCR 2509-8 to allow licensed 

Executive Order D 2020 274 
 
 
December 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
family child care homes to care for one (1) additional school-age child, and to allow child care 
centers and school-age child care centers to care for two (2) additional school-age children. I also 
encourage CDHS to grant waivers of section 7.707.22 to allow licensed family child care homes 
of all license types to exclude their own school-age children from counting toward the maximum 
number of children allowed by their license type. I encourage CDHS to promote the availability 
of these waivers to licensed child care programs throughout the State. 
On September 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 188, temporarily suspending 
statute to facilitate learning pods due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado and to balance 
health and safety with needed flexibility for supervision and care of children who are learning 
remotely during this pandemic.  I extended Executive Order D 2020 188 on October 9, 2020 and 
November 7, 2020. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, and 
D 2020 243 for an additional thirty (30) days. 
II. 
Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 188, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 217, D 2020 243, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 6, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 188, as extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 217 and D 2020 243, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this sixth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-275
full_text:
D 2020 275 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 071, D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, 
D 2020 218, and D 2020 244 Concerning the Suspension of Statutes to Allow the Operation 
of Alternate Care Sites in Colorado Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 071, D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, and 
D 2020 244 concerning the suspension of statutes to allow the operation of alternate care sites in 
Colorado due to the presence of COVID-19.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. It is imperative that we ensure health care facilities have 
sufficient resources and to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-19 patients who no longer 
require inpatient hospitalization but still require some medical care and observation while 
recovering from COVID-19.  
 
Two of five alternative care sites, The Ranch Complex in Loveland and the Western 
Memory Care Center in Grand Junction, are being decommissioned. Decommissioning these two 
sites will save the State money while ensuring that we still have the necessary capacity to 
respond to a surge of COVID-19 patients.  

Executive Order D 2020 275 
 
 
December 6, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended or extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, and 
D 2020 244 for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure health care facilities have sufficient 
resources and to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-19 patients who no longer require 
inpatient hospitalization but still require some medical care and observation while recovering 
from COVID-19. 
 
II.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 107, 
D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, D 2020 244, and this Executive Order shall 
expire thirty (30) days from December 6, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In 
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended or extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, and D 2020 244, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this sixth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-276
full_text:
D 2020 276 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, 
D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, and D 2020 245 Ordering Individuals in 
Colorado to Wear Non-Medical Face Coverings 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, 
D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, and D 2020 245 ordering individuals in Colorado to wear 
a medical or non-medical face covering due to the presence of coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, and D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID infections. As we 
take steps to return Coloradans to work, we must continue to take measures to facilitate 
reopening the economy while protecting public health by taking steps to incorporate best 
practices to protect individuals from infection. 
 
We have learned that widespread mask use is a low cost and highly effective way to 
reduce the spread of COVID-19 infections by as much as 65%. Broad adoption of mask wearing 

Executive Order D 2020 276 
December 8, 2020 
Page 2 of 2  
in Colorado may have significant economic benefits by allowing the State to prevent re-closures 
of businesses and schools and, ultimately, return to our normal lives more quickly. A recent 
study from Goldman Sachs concluded that a federal mask mandate could save the U.S. economy 
from taking a 5% hit to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). When I first implemented this 
Executive Order, thirty-nine Colorado counties and municipalities already had mandatory mask 
orders in place. 
Under Executive Order D 2020 110, mask wearing is mandatory for employees, 
contractors, and others providing services for Mass Transportation Operations and Critical 
Businesses as well as for State and county employees and the individuals they serve at 
Government Offices and Facilities. These requirements remain intact with this Executive Order. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, 
D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, and D 2020 245 to 
protect Coloradans by requiring individuals in Colorado to wear a non-medical face covering 
over their nose and mouth, subject to several exceptions 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 8, 2020, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, 
D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, and D 2020 245 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this eighth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-277
full_text:
D 2020 277 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, 
D 2020 220, and D 2020 247 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes to 
Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce and Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19

247 temporarily suspending certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health workforce and 
eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing and treatment for 
Medicaid enrollees. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have

The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a

This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive

suspend certain statutes to preserve the State’s Medicaid home health workforce and protect 
Medicaid enrollees from COVID-19 by reducing the need for in person visits. This Executive 

Executive Order D 2020 277 
 
 
December 11, 2020

Order also extends the temporary suspension of statutory requirements for cost sharing for

title: D 2020-278
full_text:
D 2020 278 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112,  
D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, and D 2020 248 Temporarily Suspending 
Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Criminal Justice  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 016, as amended and extended by D 2020 043, D2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, 
D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, and D 2020 248 concerning the suspension of certain 
regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2020 278 
 
 
December 11, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection.  On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order D 2020 
016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive Orders 
D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire. On June 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 112, 
which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  On July 19, 2020, I 
issued Executive Order D 2020 140, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional 
thirty (30) days. On August 17, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 166, which extended 
Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  On September 15, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 192, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) 
days. On October 14, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 221, which extended Sections II.A 
and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On November 12, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 248, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. 
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days, 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance.  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, 
D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 11, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, 
D 2020 192, D 2020 221, and D 2020 248 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eleventh day of December, 
2020.  
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-280
full_text:
D 2020 280 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, 
D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, and D 2020 251 Authorizing the Executive Directors of 
Certain State Agencies to Promulgate and Issue Emergency Rules Extending the 
Expiration Date of Licenses and Other Documents Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 
195, D 2020 224, and D 2020 251 authorizing the executive directors of certain State agencies to 
promulgate and issue emergency rules extending the expiration date of licenses and other 
documents for an additional thirty (30) days due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I.         Background and Purpose  
  
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 

Executive Order D 2020 280 
 
 
December 13, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, 
D 2020 224, and D 2020 251 for an additional thirty (30) days. 
II.   
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders, D 2020 
042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 13, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, 
D 2020 195, D 2020 224, and D 2020 251 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
thirteenth day of December, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, and D 2020 276, is amended as follows:  
 
1. Strike the word “moving,” in Section II.G.  
 
2. Add a new Paragraph 9 to Section II.M. that states the following: 
 
 
Individuals who are alone in a room.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 281 
December 14, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
 
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, and this Executive Order, shall expire on January 6, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, 
D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, and D 2020 276 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
Jared Polis 
Governor 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fourteenth day of 
December, 2020.

title: D 2020-282
full_text:
D 2020 282 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, and D 2020 256 Concerning the 
Temporary Suspension of Certain Requirements Preventing Issuance of Marriage Licenses 
Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as extended by D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, 
D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, and D 2020 256, concerning the temporary suspension of 
certain statutes preventing the issuance of marriage licenses when county clerk and recorder 
offices are closed, due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and 
D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2020 282 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19.  I 
have extended this Executive Order multiple times due to the continuing impact of COVID-19 in 
Colorado.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, and D 2020 256 
for an additional thirty (30) days. This extension will enable Coloradans to obtain marriage 
licenses while observing social distancing guidelines and minimizing the risk of spreading 
COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
          Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 18, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, 
D 2020 200, D 2020 228, and D 2020 256 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eighteenth day of December, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-283
full_text:
D 2020 283 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, 
D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, and 
D 2020 257 Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Due  
to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, 
D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, and D 2020 257, concerning the temporary 
suspension of certain regulatory statutes due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend the practice of “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet 
or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2020 283 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 011 for an additional 
thirty (30) days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, 
D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, and 
D 2020 257.  This amendment includes an additional statutory suspension to enable gaming and 
sports betting applicants to obtain temporary or conditional licenses without submitting 
fingerprints during the COVID-19 pandemic. 
 
II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, 
D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, and D 2020 257, is amended to include the following:  
U. 
I temporarily suspend the portions of C.R.S. §§ 44-30-510(3) and 
44-30-1504(7)(a) that require gaming and sports betting applicants to submit 
fingerprints with the submission of an application. I authorize the Director of the 
Colorado Division of Gaming to accept applications and allow fingerprints to be 
submitted up to and prior to the issuance of a permanent license or approval. 
V.  
I authorize the Colorado Limited Gaming Control Commission to promulgate and 
issue emergency rules as necessary, if such authority does not already exist, to 
establish procedures to achieve the objectives set forth in Section II.U of this 
Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 283 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III. 
Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 
2020 229, D 2020 257, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 
18, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, 
D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, and D 2020 257 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eighteenth day of December, 
2020.

title: D 2020-284
full_text:
D 2020 284 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, and D 2020 268 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of 
Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 
125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 
268 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for response activities 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268 and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 

Executive Order D 2020 284 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 2 of 4 
 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268 to transfer $15,720,896 from the Controlled 
Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund; transfer $5,000,000 from the 
Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund; transfer 
$24,552,249 from the CWCB Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency fund; increase the 
amount of funds made available to the Department of Public Health and Environment by 
$71,800,000 from multiple sources, including a transfer from the Department of Public Safety of 
$23,169,426 from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund, 
$4,417,614 from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund, and $44,212,960 from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund; increase the amount of funds made available to the Department of 
Agriculture by $312,000 from the Disaster Emergency Fund; increase the amount of funds made 
available to the Department of Human Services by $505,538 from the Disaster Emergency Fund; 
and increase the amount of funds made available to the Department of Personnel and 
Administration by $242,647 from the Disaster Emergency Fund.   
II. 
Amendment 
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 
152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268 is 
amended to read as follows:  
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred eighty three million five 
hundred twenty thousand eight hundred ninety six dollars ($183,520,896) was transferred 
from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for 
COVID-19 related purposes; five million dollars ($5,000,000) was transferred from the 
Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for 
COVID-19 related purposes; twenty four million five hundred fifty two thousand two 
hundred forty nine dollars ($24,552,249) was transferred from the Colorado Water 
Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency fund for COVID-19 
related purposes.  
 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that four hundred eighty three million 
seventy three thousand one hundred forty five dollars ($483,073,145) from the Disaster 
Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these 
funds, two hundred and five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds 
coming from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred eighty four million 
four hundred forty four thousand seven hundred ninety dollars ($184,444,790) shall be 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19; 

Executive Order D 2020 284 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 3 of 4 
 
zero dollars ($0) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Public 
Safety for response activities related to COVID-19;  twelve million eight hundred eight 
thousand six hundred eighty five dollars ($12,808,685) shall be encumbered and made 
available to the Colorado Department of Corrections for response activities related to 
COVID-19; four million eight hundred and ninety eight thousand two hundred and 
twenty-seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Colorado Department of Labor and Employment for response activities related to 
COVID; one million one hundred and fifty nine thousand eight hundred and ninety 
dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Governor’s 
Office of Information and Technology for response activities related to COVID-19; and 
one million six hundred eighty eight thousand four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408) 
shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Agriculture for 
response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
In addition, I order that four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred 
fourteen dollars ($4,417,614) from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund be 
encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Public Health and 
Environment for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund 
shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 30, 2020, 
and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. Funds from 
interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available for expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 30, 2020. All other funds from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 284 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 4 of 4 
  
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 
2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire on December 27, 2020, unless amended further by Executive Order, except that the funds 
described in Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall 
remain available for the purposes and the time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 
2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 
205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, and D 2020 268, shall remain in full force and effect 
as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eighteenth day of December, 
2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-285
full_text:
D 2020 285 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 223, D 2020 227, and D 2020 255 
Limiting Certain Evictions to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 223, D 2020 227, and D 2020 255 providing assistance to residential 
and commercial tenants at risk for eviction who were economically harmed by coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose  
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice social 
distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. Keeping people housed can help limit the spread of COVID-19 
during a public health disaster.  
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 

Executive Order D 2020 285 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 2 of 4 
 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hit the hardest. We must take action to shore up economic 
security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
 
Transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. 
On April 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 044 Safer at Home, as amended and 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 079. On July 1, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 091 
Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 091, D 2020 123, D 2020 142, D 2020 144, D 2020 170, and D 2020 199. To be Safer at 
Home, Coloradans impacted financially by COVID-19 must continue to have a home.  
 
On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 235 Establishing Directives for 
the COVID-19 Dial Framework. We are doing everything we can to get Coloradans back to 
work but this process is gradual and must take into account the evolving public health conditions. 
Many Coloradans continue to experience substantial loss of income as a result of business 
closures and layoffs, hindering their ability to keep up with their residential and nonresidential 
rent payments through no fault of their own.  
 
I have already taken steps to keep Coloradans housed. On June 13, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 101, which provided tenants with thirty (30) days to make rental 
payments before facing eviction and instructed the Department of Local Affairs (DOLA) to 
create a model rental repayment agreement to assist individuals who were facing financial 
hardship due to COVID-19. On July 12, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 134, which 
extended the provisions of Executive Order D 2020 101 and encouraged municipalities to 
eliminate housing-related restrictions to provide more access to housing. On August 10, 2020, I 
issued Executive Order D 2020 162, which extended the provisions of Executive Orders D 2020 
101 and D 2020 134. On September 8, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 185, which 
extended the provisions of Executive Orders D 2020 101, D 2020 134, and D 2020 162. On 
September 22, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 202, which extended the provisions of 
Executive Orders D 2020 101, D 2020 134, D 2020 162, and D 2020 185, and required landlords 
to provide notice of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)’s Temporary Halt in 
Residential Evictions To Prevent the Further Spread of COVID-19.  
 
Additionally, on August 26, 2020, I signed Executive Order B 2020 006 creating the 
Special Eviction Prevention Task Force (Task Force). This temporary Task Force was directed to 
consider causes of and propose solutions to housing instability arising from the impact of 
COVID-19. The Task Force created recommendations on how to address housing instability due
