to COVID-19 in Colorado and presented these recommendations to me on October 9, 2020.  
 
On October 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 223 which built on the actions my 
administration has already taken and implemented recommendations from the Task Force 
ensuring Coloradans have stable housing during this public health emergency.  
 

Executive Order D 2020 285 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 3 of 4 
 
 
On October 21, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 227 amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 223, to protect residential and commercial tenants at risk for eviction 
who were economically harmed by COVID-19. On November 19, 2020 I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 255 extending Executive Order D 2020 223, as amended and extended by Executive 
Order D 2020 227, for an additional 30 days.  
 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 223, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 227 and D 2020 255. We must continue to take steps to 
provide relief to Colorado renters and small businesses.  
 
II.  
Amendments  
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 223, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 227 and D 2020 255, is amended as follows:  
 
1. Replace Section II.H. with the following:  
 
For the purposes of this Executive Order, “financial hardship due to COVID-19” means 
an individual who, at any time after March 10, 2020, meets all of the following criteria: 
 
1. Is using best efforts to obtain government assistance for rent or housing; 
 
2. Either (1) expects to earn no more than $99,000 in annual income for Calendar 
Year 2020 (or no more than $198,000 if filing a joint tax return); (2) was not 
required to report any income in 2019 to the U.S. Internal Revenue Service; or (3) 
received an Economic Impact Payment (stimulus check) pursuant to Section 2201 
of the federal CARES Act; 
 
3. Is unable to pay the full rent or make a full housing payment due to substantial 
loss of household income, loss of compensable hours of work or wages, a lay-off, 
or extraordinary out-of-pocket medical expenses; 
 
4. Is using best efforts to make timely partial payments that are as close to the full 
payment as the individual’s circumstances may permit, taking into account other 
nondiscretionary expenses; and 
 
5. Would likely be rendered homeless or forced to move into and live in close 
quarters in a new congregate or shared living setting because the individual has no 
other available housing options if evicted. 
 
2. Replace Section II.I. with the following:  
 
I direct the Executive Director of DOLA to create a declaration form that uses the 
requirements outlined in Section II.H. above. A tenant can demonstrate financial hardship 
due to COVID-19 by accurately completing and signing the form under penalty of 

Executive Order D 2020 285 
 
 
December 18, 2020 
Page 4 of 4 
  
perjury and providing it to their landlord. A court may verify the accuracy of a 
declaration on this DOLA form if the form is submitted to a landlord after December 18, 
2020. A tenant may provide underlying documentation to their landlord to support the 
declaration form. Additionally, the tenant may provide the CDC’s declaration form in 
lieu of DOLA’s form to demonstrate financial hardship due to COVID-19.  
 
3. Add Section II.Q. as follows:  
 
Nothing in this Executive Order shall apply to any individual who is an unauthorized 
person as defined by C.R.S. § 13-40.1-101(b).  
III.     Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 223, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 227, 
D 2020 255, and this Executive Order, shall expire on December 31, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 223, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 227 and D 2020 255, shall remain in full force and effect 
as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this eighteenth 
day of October, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-286
full_text:
D 2020 286 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 260 Authorizing the Colorado 
Department of Public Health and Environment to Order Hospitals and Freestanding 
Emergency Departments to Transfer or Cease the Admission of Patients to Respond to the 
Current COVID-19 Disaster Emergency in Colorado
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., including 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II), I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue
this Executive Order amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 260 authorizing the
Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) to order hospitals and
freestanding emergency departments to transfer or cease the admission of patients to respond to
the current disaster emergency due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, CDPHE public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive
positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. 
I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, 
D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID-19 infections.   
Unfortunately, given the increase in infections, the number of persons seeking medical 
treatment at hospitals may far exceed the capacity of any given hospital. Hospitals who have 
reached capacity or are reasonably anticipated to reach capacity during the duration of this 
Executive Order may need to cease admitting patients or may need to transfer patients to a 

Executive Order D 2020 286 
 
 
December 22, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
separate facility without first obtaining the individual’s written or informed consent for such 
transfer. The transfer of patients from hospitals that have reached capacity to other specified care 
facilities will help to ensure that Coloradans have adequate care as we combat the current public 
health emergency due to COVID-19. 
 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II) specifically empowers the Governor, in the event of an 
emergency epidemic that has been declared a disaster emergency, to act by Executive Order to 
order hospitals “to transfer or cease admission of patients or perform medical examinations of 
persons.”  
 
 
The federal Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA), 
42 U.S.C. § 1395dd, requires hospitals receiving Medicare funds to provide appropriate medical 
screening to determine whether a patient has an emergency medical condition. EMTALA 
prohibits a hospital from transferring a patient with an emergency medical condition unless and 
until that condition has been stabilized. One of the purposes of EMTALA is to prevent hospital 
emergency departments from “dumping” patients who are unable to pay. EMTALA also states: 
“The provisions of this section do not preempt any State or local law requirement, except to the 
extent that the requirement directly conflicts with a requirement of this section.” 42 U.S.C. 
§ 1395dd(f). The Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Centers for Medicare and 
Medicaid Services (CMS) have waived 42 U.S.C. § 1395dd(a) so that hospitals can screen 
patients at a location offsite from the hospital’s campus to prevent the spread of COVID-19. This 
Executive Order is not intended to conflict with EMTALA, and hospitals must still comply with 
all non-waived sections of EMTALA. 
 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 260 authorizing 
CDPHE to order hospitals and freestanding emergency departments to transfer or cease the 
admission of patients to respond to the current disaster emergency due to COVID-19 in Colorado 
within the requirements of federal law. 
 
 
II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Section II.A of Executive Order D 2020 260 is amended to read as follows: 
 
A. 
A hospital or freestanding emergency department that has reached capacity, or is 
reasonably anticipated to reach capacity, to examine and treat patients, shall 
notify the State ESF 8 Lead, Office of Emergency Preparedness and Response, 
within CDPHE by contacting the 24/7 Emergency and Incident Reporting Line at 
1-877-518-5608. The State ESF 8 Lead will then contact and inform the Health 
Facilities and Emergency Medical Services Division that the hospital or 
freestanding emergency department has reached capacity, or is reasonably 
anticipated to reach capacity, to examine and treat patients. Upon notification to 
CDPHE and after CDPHE has determined that a hospital or freestanding 
emergency department is at, or reasonably anticipated to reach, capacity, CDPHE: 
 

Executive Order D 2020 286 
 
 
December 22, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
 
Section II.E of Executive Order D 2020 260 is amended to read as follows: 
 
E. 
A person receiving care in a hospital or freestanding emergency department who 
is transferred to another hospital or facility pursuant to this Executive Order is 
deemed to have an emergency medical condition, and all services for the transfer 
and treatment at the receiving facility are considered to be emergency services for 
purposes of C.R.S. § 10-16-704(5.5), including the in network cost sharing 
benefits and consumer protections against balance billing, and the prohibition 
against prior authorization. 
 
III.      Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 260, as amended and extended by this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from December 22, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In 
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 260 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-second 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-287
full_text:
D 2020 287 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 230 and D 2020 262 Directing the 
Colorado Department of Labor and Employment to Make One-Time Direct Stimulus 
Payments to Qualifying Individuals Experiencing Economic Hardships Caused by COVID-
19 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, the relevant portions of 
the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., and C.R.S. § 24-33.5-1106, 
I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending 
and extending Executive Orders D 2020 230 and D 2020 262 directing the Colorado Department 
of Labor and Employment (CDLE) to make one-time direct stimulus payments to qualifying 
individuals experiencing economic hardships caused by coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) 
in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community transmission throughout the State. I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. While we have seen indications that our efforts to 
“flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ way 
of life and livelihoods. 

Executive Order D 2020 287 
 
 
December 25, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
Since March 15, 2020, hundreds of thousands of Coloradans have filed for and received 
unemployment insurance benefits to help pay for groceries, rent, and utilities to weather the 
hardships caused by COVID-19. As of August 2020, employment rates among workers making 
less than $27,000 decreased by 15% compared to January 2020, while workers making more 
than $60,000 have almost returned to their January 2020 employment rates. Many Coloradans 
continue to rely on unemployment insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
On March 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 012, which mobilized State 
resources to improve and expedite services available to individuals applying for unemployment 
insurance benefits to promote the economic stability of the State. Subsequently, I issued 
Executive Orders D 2020 031, D 2020 051, and D 2020 088, which extended the availability of 
the resources issued in Executive Order D 2020 012. On June 13, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 100, which further extended these resources to improve and expedite unemployment 
insurance benefits. Executive Order D 2020 100 was extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, 
D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, and D 2020 273.  
 
On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order Executive Order D 2020 230 to allow 
CDLE to make one-time direct stimulus payments through the unemployment insurance system 
to qualifying individuals experiencing economic hardships caused by COVID-19 in Colorado. 
On November 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 262, extending Executive Order D 
2020 230 for thirty (30) days. This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 
2020 230 and D 2020 262.  
 
II. 
Amendment 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 230, as extended by Executive Order, D 2020 262, 
is amended as follows:  
 
1. Section II.F. is amended to read as follows:  
 
I further encumber one hundred sixty-eight million dollars ($168,000,000) from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund, established in C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(2)(a), made available to 
CDLE, for one-time direct stimulus payments transferred from the Disaster Emergency 
Fund to all qualifying individuals experiencing economic hardships caused by COVID-19 
in Colorado. These funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. These funds shall 
remain available for this purpose until February 28, 2021.  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 230, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 262 and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 25, 2020, unless amended further 
by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.F. of Executive Order D 2020 
230, as extended, shall remain available for the described purposes until February 28, 2010. In 

Executive Order D 2020 287 
 
 
December 25, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 230, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 262, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-fifth day of 
December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-288
full_text:
D 2020 288 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 005, D 2020 008, D 2020 036,  
D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, D 2020 231, and 
D 2020 263 Concerning Limiting In-Person Contact for the 2020 Elections and the 
Secretary of State’s Operations Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, 
D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, 
D 2020 231, and D 2020 263, concerning changes to 2020 election activity to limit in-person 
contact while allowing the elections to proceed safely and without interruption to the Secretary 
of State’s operations due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 
2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. We must continue to take action to 
minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that 
causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and through 
respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2020 288 
 
 
December 26, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 005 for an 
additional thirty (30) days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, 
D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, 
D 2020 231, and D 2020 263.  I am extending Sections II.K and II.L to enable political 
parties to continue utilizing remote operations to minimize the risk of spreading COVID-
19, and allowing all other provisions to expire.  
 
II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders  D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, 
D 2020 179, D 2020 209, D 2020 231, and D 2020 263, is amended as follows: 
 
 
i. 
Sections II.F, II.G, II.H, II.I, II.J, and II.M are deleted. 
 
 
ii. 
Sections II.K and II.L are extended for an additional thirty (30) days. 
 
 
III. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, 
D 2020 209, D 2020 231, D 2020 263, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from December 26, 2020, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other 
respects, Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, 
D 2020 209, D 2020 231, D 2020 263, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-sixth day 
of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-289
full_text:
D 2020 289 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 235 and D 2020 265 Establishing Directives for the  
COVID-19 Dial Framework 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 265, establishing directives 
for the Dial Framework due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, and D 2020 284. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes, speaks, or sings. It can 
also be spread through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we 
practice Social Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other 
people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at 
Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured 
that Coloradans who needed it had access to healthcare resources.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 

Executive Order D 2020 289 
 
 
December 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended 
and extended by Executive Order D 2020 024, taking a wide array of actions to mitigate the 
effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect against overwhelming our health care 
resources. Stay at Home required Coloradans to stay at home, subject to certain limited 
exceptions, and ordered the Executive Director of the CDPHE to issue a public health order 
(PHO) defining critical emergency personnel, infrastructure, government functions, and other 
activities exempt from the Stay at Home directives. Under Stay at Home and social distancing, 
the increase in cases and rate of infection slowed.  
 
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044, as amended and extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 079, and the Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive 
Order 2020 091, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 123, D 2020 142, D 2020 
144, D 2020 170, D 2020 199, and D 2020 213, I took steps to return Coloradans to work while 
continuing the efforts to “flatten the curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home 
and Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to practice 
Social Distancing from others, limit social interactions, take extra precautions for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wear non-medical masks while in public to facilitate 
reopening the economy.  
 
On July 9, 2020, I issued Protect Our Neighbors Executive Order D 2020 127, as 
amended or extended by D 2020 154, D 2020 178, D 2020 207, which implemented a number of 
measures giving local communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still protecting 
the public’s health. Under Protect Our Neighbors, local communities that have met certain 
qualifications and have an approved mitigation and containment plan may allow all activities to 
occur at fifty perfect (50%) capacity with Social Distancing with no more than five hundred 
(500) people in any one setting. Qualifying local communities are permitted to issue more 
detailed guidelines or public health orders to meet the needs of their community as long as the 
capacity did not exceed the Statewide limit. Because each community and each part of our State 
has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows communities to create unique and flexible 
plans within the statewide guidelines as we continue to open our economy and support people 
who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19.  
 
On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 235, superseding and replacing 
Executive Orders Protect Our Neighbors and Safer at Home in the Vast, Great Outdoors and 
establishing directives for the COVID-19 Dial Framework. On November 28, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 265, amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 235 to include 
Level Purple. The Dial Framework recognizes unique local circumstances and uses an intuitive 
dial to visualize a community’s success in containing the spread of COVID-19.  
 
Each county or region is at one of the six levels represented by colors on the Dial, which 
range from least restrictive to most restrictive. The goal of each level is to strike the important 
balance between enabling economic and social activity while ensuring that our testing, contact 
tracing, and health care systems are able to contain the virus. Each level is defined by objective 
scientific metrics such as the number of new cases, percent positivity of COVID-19 tests, and 
hospitalizations. Based on these metrics, the dial assigns certain limitations and restrictions to 

Executive Order D 2020 289 
 
 
December 27, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
contain the virus, including capacity limitations. A community may move between dial levels 
based on the metrics and in consultation with State and local public health officials to ensure 
unique local conditions are appropriately considered. If a county is moved “back to normal” off 
the dial entirely because of a major reduction in transmission or risk, it will no longer be subject 
to this Executive Order and the corresponding PHO.   
 
 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 235 and D 2020 265 for an 
additional thirty (30) days due to the continued presence of COVID-19 in Colorado. 
 
II. 
Duration  
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 265, 
and as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 27, 2020, 
unless amended further by Executive Order.  In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 235, 
as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 265, shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-seventh 
day of December, 2020.  
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-290
full_text:
D 2020 290 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence 
of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado  
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 
125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, 
and D 2020 284 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for 
response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
 
I.  
Background and Purpose 
  
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 
2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 
268, D 2020 284, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 

Executive Order D 2020 290 
 
 
December 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, and D 2020 284 to extend the state of disaster 
emergency and the deployment of the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning 
resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the 
State.  
II.  
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 
2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and this Executive 
Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 27, 2020, unless extended further by 
Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended and extended, shall remain available for the described purposes and the described 
time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended 
and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 
109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, and D 2020 284 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-seventh day of 
December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-291
full_text:
D 2020 291 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, and D 2020 266 Ordering the Temporary Suspension 
of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, and 
Behavioral Health Due to the Presence of COVID-19  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 
2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, and D 2020 266, ordering the temporary suspension of 
certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile justice, regional centers, and behavioral health 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and D 2020 290. On March 25, 2020, I requested 
that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2020 291 
 
 
December 29, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.  I amended and extended that Executive Order in Executive 
Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 
236, and D 2020 266.   
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A, II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I for an 
additional thirty (30) days, to enable CDHS and the Division of Youth Services to continue to 
respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of COVID-19 in 
their facilities. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from December 29, 2020, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, 
D 2020 206, D 2020 236, and D 2020 266 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-ninth 
day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-292
full_text:
D 2020 292 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Statute to Allow the Use of Telehealth 
Services for Veterinarians Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order ordering the 
temporary suspension of statute to allow the use of telehealth services for veterinarians due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284 and D 2020 290. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has 
undertaken a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, and preserve our health care resources. COVID-19 is a highly contagious viral disease 
that has spread throughout many Colorado communities, across the country, and across the 
world. To protect the public health and mitigate exposure to and the spread of COVID-19, 
Colorado veterinarians, must be able to use telehealth services whenever possible. These 
services, including low-cost telephone, internet, audio-only, and live video.  
This Executive Order orders the temporary suspension of statute to allow the use of 
telehealth services for veterinarians due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado.  

Executive Order D 2020 292 
 
 
December 29, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
II. 
Directives  
 
A. 
I temporarily suspend the requirement in C.R.S. § 12-315-104(19)(b) that a 
veterinarian-client patient relationship is established when the veterinarian has 
recently seen and is personally acquainted with the keeping and care of the animal 
by virtue of an examination of the animal or by medically appropriate and timely 
visits to the premises where the animal is kept. 
 
III. 
Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from December 29, 2020, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-ninth day 
of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-293
full_text:
D 2020 293 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Authorizing the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment to Reallocate 
Existing Funds from Contracts with Local Public Health Agencies Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order authorizing the Colorado 
Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) to reallocate existing funds from 
contracts with local public health agencies due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in the State.  
 
I.  
 Background and Purpose 
  
On March 5, 2020, CDPHE’s public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive 
positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally 
declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 
2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 
205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and D 2020 290. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. On May 16, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 066, which reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies during 
State Fiscal Year 2019-2020.  
This Executive Order authorizes CDPHE to reallocate existing funds from contracts with 
local public health agencies to COVID-19 response activities. Reprioritizing the use of existing 
funds will provide flexibility to local public health agencies as they respond to COVID-19 during 
State Fiscal Year 2020-2021. 
 

Executive Order D 2020 293 
 
 
December 29, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
II.     Directives  
A. 
I authorize CDPHE to redirect any remaining State fund balance on contracts 
between CDPHE and a county or district public health agency or the Denver 
Health and Hospital Authority (collectively, “local public health agency”), related 
to the Communities that Care (CTC) Program appropriated from the Marijuana 
Tax Cash Fund created by C.R.S. § 39-28.8-501(1), to COVID-19 response 
activities.  CDPHE may permit a local public health agency who is a CTC 
contractor to utilize any remaining State contract funds for State Fiscal Year 
2020-2021 for this purpose. 
III.   
Duration 
  
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from December 29, 2020, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-ninth day 
of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-294
full_text:
D 2020 294 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Deadlines to Allow the Continued 
Use of Federal Funds from the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 
2020  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order temporarily 
suspending certain deadlines to allow the continued use of federal funds from Coronavirus 
Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020, Public Law 116-136 (CARES Act) due to 
the presence of due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, we have evidence of 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 
10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 284, and D 2020 290. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President 
of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
On March 27, 2020, the federal CARES Act was enacted to provide emergency 
assistance in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CARES Act established the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund, which provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments 
navigating the impact of COVID-19. On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 
070, as amended and extended, to direct the expenditure of these funds. In Executive Order 
D 2020 070, I allocated seventy million dollars to the General Fund for eligible 
expenditures pursuant to the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund. The CARES Act 
originally required these funds only cover expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 until 
December 30, 2020. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, which was signed into 
law on December 28, 2020, extends the time period during which state can incur expenses 
covered by the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from December 30, 2020 to 
December 31, 2021. A number of Colorado state statutes and enacted legislation 

Executive Order D 2020 294 
 
 
December 30, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
appropriating CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars conformed to the previous 
federal deadline. These deadlines were imposed by the General Assembly to comply with 
the original CARES Act deadline. This deadline was extended by the Consolidated 
Appropriations Act, 2021.  
 
This Executive Order temporarily suspends certain deadlines to allow the continued 
use of Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars from the CARES Act.  
II.  
Directives  
A. 
I temporarily suspend the December 30, 2020 deadline where it appears in the 
following legislation:  
1. HB20-1360;  
2. HB20-1197;  
3. HB20-1410;  
4. HB20-1411;  
5. HB20-1412;  
6. HB20-1422;  
7. HB20-1417; 
8. SB20-212; and  
9. SB20-222 
 
III.  
Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from December 30, 2020 unless 
extended further by Executive Order. 
  
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this thirtieth day 
of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-295
full_text:
D 2020 295 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 070, D 2020 081, and D 2020 216 Directing the 
Expenditure of Federal Funds Pursuant to the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic 
Security Act of 2020 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 070, D 2020 081, and 2020 216 directing the expenditure of federal funds 
pursuant to the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020 (CARES Act), 
Public Law 116-136. 
I.
Background and Purpose
The CARES Act provides emergency assistance in response to the coronavirus disease
2019 (COVID-19) pandemic. The CARES Act established the Coronavirus Relief Fund, which 
provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments navigating the impact of COVID-19. 
Colorado’s State government received approximately $1.674 billion from the Coronavirus Relief 
Fund pursuant to the CARES Act. Under the CARES Act, a state must use the funds from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund to cover expenses that (1) are necessary expenditures incurred due to 
the COVID-19 public health emergency (COVID-19 emergency); (2) were not accounted for in 
the budget most recently approved as of March 27, 2020 (the date of enactment of the CARES 
Act) for the State; and (3) were incurred between March 1, 2020 and December 30, 2020. The 
Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, which was signed into law on December 28, 2020, 
extended the deadline for expenditure of the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from 
December 30, 2020 until December 31, 2021. As Governor, I have the authority to direct the 
expenditure of these federal funds.  
Pursuant to the CARES Act, on April 15, 2020 I certified to the United States 
Department of the Treasury that the State’s proposed use of the funds from the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund would comply with the specified purposes in the CARES Act. The State of Colorado 
has created the CARES Act Fund to receive Coronavirus Relief Fund moneys prior to allocation 
for eligible expenditures.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. The funds received by the State from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund are essential for mitigating the effects of the pandemic.   

Executive Order D 2020 295 
 
 
December 30, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 070 disbursing those funds for 
various purposes. On May 28, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 081, amending Executive 
Order D 2020 070, to change the fund source for the accounting of certain CARES Act Fund 
dollars for education and to clarify the permissible uses of CARES Act Fund dollars for local 
governments. On October 9, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 216, amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 070 and D 2020 081, to reallocate certain CARES Act Fund dollars to support 
recovery for small businesses and individuals across the State from the impacts of COVID-19, 
and to meet changing needs identified by agencies.  
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 070, D 2020 081, and D 2020 
216 to reflect that the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, allows CARES funds to be 
accessible past December 30, 2020 until December 31, 2021 and to indicate that Coronavirus 
Relief Fund dollars may be reallocated to ensure appropriate support for ongoing Federal 
reporting and monitoring.  
II.  
Amendment  
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended by Executive Order D 2020 081 
and D 2020 216, is amended as follows:  
 
1. Add Section II.D as follows:  
 
If Coronavirus Relief Fund recipients and/or subrecipients choose to continue to expend 
CARES Act Fund dollars beyond the original December 30, 2020 deadline, a small 
portion of the funds that remain unexpended beyond December 30, 2020 may be 
reallocated to ensure appropriate support for ongoing Federal reporting and monitoring 
beyond the Act’s original expiration date. The Governor’s Office will work with 
recipients and/or subrecipients to effectuate this reallocation.  
 
III. 
Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended 
by Executive Orders D 2020 081 and D 2020 216, shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this thirtieth day of 
December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-296
full_text:
D 2020 296 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and D 2020 290 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to 
the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 
125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, 
D 2020 284, and D 2020 290 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional 
funds for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
 
I.  
Background and Purpose 
  
 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, 
I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 

Executive Order D 2020 296 
 
 
December 30, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date   
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and D 2020 290 to reflect that the 
Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021 allows CARES funds to be accessible past December 30, 
2020 until December 31, 2021.  
 
II.  
Amendment  
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 
152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, 
and D 2020 290 is amended as follows:  
 
1. Replace the last paragraph of Section II. C. beginning with “Funds from the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund” with the following:  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency 
Fund shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to 
December 31, 2021, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund. Funds from interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund 
shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 through 
December 31, 2021. All other funds from the Disaster Emergency Fund shall be 
available until expended. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 296 
 
 
December 30, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 
2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and this 
Executive Order, shall expire on January 25, 2021, unless amended further by Executive Order, 
except that the funds described in Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and 
extended, shall remain available for the purposes and the time period stated in Section II.C. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 
176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, and D 2020 
290, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 .  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this thirtieth day of 
December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-297
full_text:
D 2020 297
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation  
Declaration of Vote on Amendment 76 - Citizenship Qualification of Electors 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Amendment 76.  
I.
Background and Purpose
Amendment 76 was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election held
on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that she 
had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a majority of 
the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
referred to the people on November 3, 2020: 
Shall there be an amendment to the Colorado constitution requiring that to be qualified to 
vote at any election an individual must be a United States citizen? 
Yes - 1,985,239 (62.90 percent) 
No - 1,171,137 (37.10 percent) 

Executive Order D 2020 297 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2  
 
 
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this thirty-first day of  
December, 2020.  
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-298
full_text:
D 2020 298

Declaration of Vote on Amendment 77 - Local Voter Approval of Gaming Limits in Black 
Hawk, Central City, and Cripple Creek 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Amendment 77.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
Amendment 77 was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election held 
on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that she 
had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a majority of 
the votes cast.  
 
 
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
 
II. 
Declaration  
 
 
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure

Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-299
full_text:
D 2020 299
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Amendment B - Repeal Property Tax Assessment Rates 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Amendment B.  
I.
Background and Purpose
Amendment B was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election held
on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that she 
had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a majority of 
the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
referred to the people on November 3, 2020: 
Without increasing property tax rates, to help preserve funding for local districts that 
provide fire protection, police, ambulance, hospital, kindergarten through twelfth grade 
education, and other services, and to avoid automatic mill levy increases, shall there be 
an amendment to the Colorado constitution to repeal the requirement that the general 
assembly periodically change the residential assessment rate in order to maintain the 
statewide proportion of residential property as compared to all other taxable property 
valued for property tax purposes and repeal the nonresidential property tax assessment 
rate of twenty-nine percent? 
Yes - 1,740,395 (57.52 percent) 
No - 1,285,136 (42.48 percent) 

Executive Order D 2020 299 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2  
 
 
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this thirty-first day of  
December, 2020.  
 
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-301
full_text:
D 2020 301 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Proposition 114 - Restoration of Grey Wolves 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Proposition 114.  
I.
Background and Purpose
Proposition 114 was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election
held on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that 
she had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a 
majority of the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
referred to the people on November 3, 2020: 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes concerning the restoration of 
gray wolves through their reintroduction on designated lands in Colorado located west of 
the continental divide, and, in connection therewith, requiring the Colorado parks and 
wildlife commission, after holding statewide hearings and using scientific data, to 
implement a plan to restore and manage gray wolves; prohibiting the commission from

caused by gray wolves? 
Yes – 1,590,299 (50.91 percent) 
No – 1,533,313 (49.09 percent)

GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thirty-

Jared Polis

title: D 2020-302
full_text:
D 2020 302 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Proposition 116 - State Income Tax Rate Reduction 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Proposition 116.  
I.
Background and Purpose
Proposition 116 was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election
held on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that 
she had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a 
majority of the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
referred to the people on November 3, 2020: 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes reducing the state income tax 
rate from 4.63% to 4.55%? 
Yes – 1,821,702 (57.86 percent) 
No – 1,327,025 (42.14 percent)  

Executive Order D 2020 302 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thirty-
first day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

Certain Fee-Based Enterprises

Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on

Background and Purpose

held on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that 
she had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a

Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved

II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
