title: D 2020-304
full_text:
D 2020 304 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Proposition 118 - Paid Family and Medical Leave Insurance 
Program 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Proposition 118.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
Proposition 118 was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election 
held on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that 
she had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a 
majority of the votes cast.  
 
 
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
 
II. 
Declaration  
 
 
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure 
referred to the people on November 3, 2020:  
 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes concerning the creation of a 
paid family and medical leave program in Colorado, and, in connection therewith, 
authorizing paid family and medical leave for a covered employee who has a serious 
health condition, is caring for a new child or for a family member with a serious health 
condition, or has a need for leave related to a family member’s military deployment or for 
safe leave; establishing a maximum of 12 weeks of family and medical leave, with an 
additional 4 weeks for pregnancy or childbirth complications, with a cap on the weekly 
benefit amount; requiring job protection for and prohibiting retaliation against an 
employee who takes paid family and medical leave; allowing a local government to opt 
out of the program; permitting employees of such a local government and self-employed 

Executive Order D 2020 304 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
individuals to participate in the program; exempting employers who offer an approved 
private paid family and medical leave plan; to pay for the program, requiring a premium 
of 0.9% of each employee’s wages, up to a cap, through December 31, 2024, and as set 
thereafter, up to 1.2% of each employee’s wages, by the director of the division of family 
and medical leave insurance; authorizing an employer to deduct up to 50% of the  
 
premium amount from an employee’s wages and requiring the employer to pay the 
remainder of the premium, with an exemption for employers with fewer than 10 
employees; creating the division of family and medical leave insurance as an enterprise 
within the department of labor and employment to administer the program; and 
establishing an enforcement and appeals process for retaliation and denied claims? 
 
Yes – 1,804,546 (57.75 percent)  
No – 1,320,386 (42.25 percent)  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thirty-
first day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-305
full_text:
D 2020 305 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Proposition EE - Cigarette Tobacco and Nicotine Products Tax 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote on 
Proposition EE. 
I.
Background and Purpose
Proposition EE was referred to the people of the state at the biennial regular election held
on November 3, 2020. Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 11, 2020 that she 
had canvassed the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measure was approved by a majority of 
the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures in initiated 
or referred to the people of the state shall become law or part of the constitution when approved 
by a majority of the votes cast, and shall take effect from and after the date of the official 
declaration of the vote by proclamation of the governor, but not later than thirty days after the 
vote has been canvassed.  
II.
Declaration
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following measure
referred to the people on November 3, 2020: 
Shall state taxes be increased by $294,000,000 annually by imposing a tax on nicotine 
liquids used in e-cigarettes and other vaping products that is equal to the total state tax on 
tobacco products when fully phased in, incrementally increasing the tobacco products tax 
by up to 22% of the manufacturer’s list price, incrementally increasing the cigarette tax 
by up to 9 cents per cigarette, expanding the existing cigarette and tobacco taxes to apply 
to sales to consumers from outside the state, establishing a minimum tax for moist snuff 
tobacco products, creating an inventory tax that applies for future cigarette tax increases, 
and initially using the tax revenue primarily for public school funding to help offset 
revenue that has been lost as a result of the economic impacts related to COVID-19 and 
then for programs that reduce the use of tobacco and nicotine products, enhance the 
voluntary Colorado preschool program and make it widely available for free, and 

Executive Order D 2020 305 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
maintain the funding for programs that currently receive revenue from tobacco taxes, 
with the state keeping and spending all of the new tax revenue as a voter-approved 
revenue change?  
 
Yes – 2,134,608 (67.56 percent) 
No – 1,025,182 (32.44 percent)  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thirty-
first day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-306
full_text:
D 2020 306 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Order D 2020 203 Declaring Insufficient 
Revenues Available for Expenditures and Ordering Mandatory Furloughs for Certain 
State Employees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 24- 
2-102(4), I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive
Order amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 203 declaring that there are
insufficient revenues available for expenditures and ordering mandatory furloughs for
certain state employees.
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, we have evidence of 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 
10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. On March 25, 2020, I requested that 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant. Colorado is facing its toughest 
economic crisis since 2008, and by some measures one of the toughest of all time. Because 
of the challenges brought on by the COVID-19 pandemic and the resulting budget 
constraints, leaders across the State are facing difficult decisions. Agencies are focused on 
providing services that Coloradans depend on while protecting crucial programs and jobs. 
We have taken steps to shore up and protect our economy, but we have not been immune to 
the economic downturn caused by COVID-19. To meet the budget reductions already 
established in the Fiscal Year 2020-21 Long Bill, a limited number of furloughs are 
necessary to manage personal service costs while continuing to protect and maintain 
operations of State services and programs. 
I understand that these furloughs are a hardship for many state employees and their 
families, as well as those who rely on the services that those employees provide. But these 

Executive Order D 2020 306 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
 
difficult times demand sacrifices from all of us. We must implement the State's budget in a 
fair and responsible manner, while continuing to protect public health and contain the 
spread of COVID-19.   
 
Staff in the Governor’s Office and the State Personnel Director worked in 
partnership with Colorado Workers for Innovative and New Solutions (WINS) to come to 
agreement, both in philosophy and in implementation, on how these mandatory furloughs 
impact state employees, as required by the Executive Order.  
 
On September 23, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 203 order that state 
employees, unless exempt, must take mandatory furlough days. The number of mandatory 
furlough days is based on an employee’s annual salary. Exempted employees are those 
necessary to respond to the COVID-19 pandemic, assist Coloradans in finding jobs, and 
protect public safety and roads. Additionally, workers earning fifty thousand dollars 
($50,000) per year or less are not furloughed.  A description of the positions that are 
exempted from mandatory furloughs is set forth in the Directives of Executive Order D 
2020 203, as amended. State employees may take mandatory furlough days at any time 
during State Fiscal Year 2020-2021.  
 
I will continue to work with the Executive Directors, the State Personnel Director, 
other elected officials, certified employee organizations, and frontline employees to 
implement budget reductions with the smallest possible impact to government services and 
state employees. While the December 2020 forecast shows improvement, significant 
uncertainty about the pace of economic recovery remains, and additional reductions may be 
required in this or following fiscal years. 
 
In addition, the State constitution vests management of the Judicial and Legislative 
branches in the heads of those branches, so employees of those branches will be exempt 
from the furloughs ordered by this Executive Order. State law provides the leaders of the 
State’s institutions of higher education with the independent authority to furlough non-
classified employees. Through this Executive Order, I also direct the State’s institutions of 
higher education to furlough classified employees if needed to manage their budgets 
accordingly. The departments of Law, State, Treasury and Education are not included in 
this Executive Order.  
II.  
Amendment 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 203 is amended as follows:  
1. Add new Section II.B.5 as follows:  
Salaries listed above are full time base salaries. The full time equivalent base 
salary shall be used to determine the number of furlough days for part time 
employees.  
2. Strike “Medicare” Section II.F.2 and replace with “Medicaid” 

Executive Order D 2020 306 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 203, as amended and extended by this Executive Order, remains 
in effect until March 31, 2021 unless extended further by Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this thirty-
first day of December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2020-307
full_text:
D 2020 307 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Providing Protections for Coloradans Tenants Due to COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order providing protections to residential 
and commercial tenants from late fees and notice of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, 
Section 502.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose  
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to help economic 
security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
 
 
This Executive Order provides protections to residential and commercial tenants from 
late fees and notice of the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, Section 502.  
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2020 307 
 
 
December 31, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
II.  
Directives 
 
A. No landlord, mobile home park owner, property management entity, or any individual or 
entity acting on behalf of a landlord, mobile home park owner, or property management 
entity shall charge a monetary sum, fee, or other penalty against a tenant or mobile home 
owner for failure to timely pay any portion of rent, beginning the day this Executive 
Order is executed. Any fee or penalty assessed on or after January 31, 2021, shall apply 
only to rent due on or after that date.  
 
B. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2021, Section 502 is the law of the United States 
including within the State of Colorado. 
 
 III.     Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from December 31, 2020, unless 
extended further by Executive Order.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this thirty first day of 
December, 2020. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-001
full_text:
D 2021 001 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 098, D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, and D 2020 
211, D 2020 239, and D 2020 270 Providing Relief to Public Utility Customers affected by 
COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, D 2020 
211, D 2020 239, and D 2020 270, for an additional thirty (30) days, providing relief to public 
utility customers to mitigate, respond to, and recover from the current economic disruption due 
to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. The economic impacts of COVID-19 are 
significant, and threaten to undermine the economic stability of many Coloradans and local 
businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple 
businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. 
We must take action to shore up economic security, employment, community cohesion, and 
community recovery.  
 

Executive Order D 2021 001 
 
 
January 1, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 181, D 2020 211, D 2020 239, and D 2020 270, which 
temporarily provides relief and State support to public utility customers to mitigate, respond to, 
and recover from the current economic disruption due to the presence of COVID19 in Colorado. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 132, D 2020 157, 
D 2020 181, D 2020 211, D 2020 239, D 2020 270, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from January 1, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 098, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 132, D 2020 157, D 2020 
181, D 2020 211, D 2020 239, and D 2020 270, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this first 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-002
full_text:
D 2021 002

Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158,

title: D 2021-003
full_text:
D 2021 003  
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, 
D 2020 214, D 2020 241, and D 2020 272 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain 
Eligibility for Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 
241, and D 2020 272 temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for 
Coloradans enrolled in Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
 
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, and D 2020 

Executive Order D 2021 003 
 
 
January 2, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
272, which ensures compliance with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily 
suspending certain statutes to allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those 
individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, 
and directs the Executive Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing 
(HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic 
Health Plan. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 2020 136, 
D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from January 2, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 104, D 
2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, and D 2020 272, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this second day of 
January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-004
full_text:
D 2021 004 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, and D 2020 
215, D 2020 242, and D 2020 273 Ordering Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim 
Processing to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19

and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020

(COVID19) in Colorado.

003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 
076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications 
that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten 

Executive Order D 2021 004 
 
 
January 2, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely on unemployment 
insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, and D 2020 273, which 
expedites processing of unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will help protect the 
economic well-being of Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, 
D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from January 2, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as extended by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, and D 2020 273, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
second day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-005
full_text:
D 2021 005 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, D 2020 243, and D 2020 274 
Temporarily Suspending Statute to Facilitate Learning Pods Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19 in the State of Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, D 2020 243, and D 2020 274 temporarily suspending statute to 
facilitate learning pods due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
In light of COVID-19, many school districts across the State are utilizing remote 
instruction in place of, or in conjunction with, in-person learning. In response, some families are 
forming groups to facilitate child supervision and remote learning during the school day 
(“learning pods”).  
State child care licensing law generally requires licensing for anyone regularly caring for 
or supervising more than four (4) unrelated children. To allow for more school-age children to be 

Executive Order D 2021 005 
January 4, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
served in licensed child care, I encourage the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS) 
to grant waivers of sections 7.707, 7.702, and 7.712 under 12 CCR 2509-8 to allow licensed 
family child care homes to care for one (1) additional school-age child, and to allow child care 
centers and school-age child care centers to care for two (2) additional school-age children. I also 
encourage CDHS to grant waivers of section 7.707.22 to allow licensed family child care homes 
of all license types to exclude their own school-age children from counting toward the maximum 
number of children allowed by their license type. I encourage CDHS to promote the availability 
of these waivers to licensed child care programs throughout the State. 
On September 10, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 188, temporarily suspending 
statute to facilitate learning pods due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado and to balance 
health and safety with needed flexibility for supervision and care of children who are learning 
remotely during this pandemic.  I extended Executive Order D 2020 188 on October 9, 2020, 
November 7, 2020, and December 6, 2020. 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 188, D 2020 217, D 2020 243, 
and D 2020 274 for an additional thirty (30) days. 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 188, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 217, D 2020 243, 
D 2020 274, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 4, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 188, as 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 217, D 2020 243, and D 2020 274, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fourth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-006
full_text:
D 2021 006 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 071, D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, 
D 2020 218, D 2020 244, and D 2020 275 Concerning the Suspension of Statutes to Allow 
the Operation of Alternate Care Sites in Colorado Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 071, D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, 
D 2020 244, and D 2020 275 concerning the suspension of statutes to allow the operation of 
alternate care sites in Colorado due to the presence of COVID-19.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. It is imperative that we ensure health care facilities have 
sufficient resources and to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-19 patients who no longer 
require inpatient hospitalization but still require some medical care and observation while 
recovering from COVID-19.  
 
Two of five alternative care sites, The Ranch Complex in Loveland and the Western 
Memory Care Center in Grand Junction, are being decommissioned. Decommissioning these two 

Executive Order D 2021 006 
January 4, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
sites will save the State money while ensuring that we still have the necessary capacity to 
respond to a surge of COVID-19 patients.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, D 2020 244, 
and D 2020 275 for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure health care facilities have sufficient 
resources and to ensure the safety and well-being of COVID-19 patients who no longer require 
inpatient hospitalization but still require some medical care and observation while recovering 
from COVID-19. 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 107,
D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, D 2020 244, D 2020 275, and this Executive 
Order shall expire thirty (30) days from January 4, 2021, unless extended further by Executive 
Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 071, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 107, D 2020 137, D 2020 163, D 2020 189, D 2020 218, D 2020 244, and D 2020 
275, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fourth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-007
full_text:
D 2021 007 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 
138,  D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, and 
D 2020 281 Ordering Individuals in Colorado to Wear Non-Medical Face Coverings 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 
190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, and D 2020 281 ordering individuals in 
Colorado to wear a medical or non-medical face covering due to the presence of coronavirus-
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID infections. As we 
take steps to return Coloradans to work, we must continue to take measures to facilitate 
reopening the economy while protecting public health by taking steps to incorporate best 
practices to protect individuals from infection. 

Executive Order D 2021 007 
 
 
January 6, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
We have learned that widespread mask use is a low cost and highly effective way to 
reduce the spread of COVID-19 infections by as much as 65%. Broad adoption of mask wearing 
in Colorado may have significant economic benefits by allowing the State to prevent re-closures 
of businesses and schools and, ultimately, return to our normal lives more quickly. A recent 
study from Goldman Sachs concluded that a federal mask mandate could save the U.S. economy 
from taking a 5% hit to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). When I first implemented this 
Executive Order, thirty-nine Colorado counties and municipalities already had mandatory mask 
orders in place. 
 
Under Executive Order D 2020 110, mask wearing is mandatory for employees, 
contractors, and others providing services for Mass Transportation Operations and Critical 
Businesses as well as for State and county employees and the individuals they serve at 
Government Offices and Facilities. These requirements remain intact with this Executive Order. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, 
D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, 
D 2020 276, and D 2020 281 to protect Coloradans by requiring individuals in Colorado to wear 
a non-medical face covering over their nose and mouth, subject to several exceptions. 
 
II.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from January 6, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, and D 2020 281 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this sixth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-008
full_text:
D 2021 008 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 
131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, and D 2021 002 Ordering 
the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes and Rules to Expand the Healthcare 
Workforce for Hospitals and Other Inpatient Treatment Facilities Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19 
 Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 
2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, and D 
2021 002, ordering the temporary suspension of certain statutes and rules to expand the 
healthcare workforce for hospitals, inpatient treatment facilities, and administration of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) vaccinations in outpatient settings due to the presence of 
COVID-19 in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose
 On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 
2021 008. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.     
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, 
D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, and D 2021 002, for an additional thirty (30) days to 

Executive Order D 2021 008
January 7, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
ensure that inpatient healthcare facilities have sufficient resources and personnel to treat patients 
suffering from COVID-19 and to ensure that inpatient facilities and outpatient settings have 
sufficient resources to administer COVID-19 vaccinations. 
II.
Amendment
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive
Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 
240, D 2020 271, and D 2021 002, is amended as follows:  
1. Section II.A is amended to read as follows:
I direct the Executive Director of DORA, through the Director of the Division of
Professions and Occupations (DPO), to promulgate and issue temporary emergency rules
to permit the licensed professionals listed below to cross train, supervise, and delegate
responsibilities concerning the temporary care and treatment of patients to the
professionals listed in Section II.B., below, in hospitals, inpatient medical facilities,
including emergency departments, and outpatient settings, including but not limited to
providing the COVID-19 vaccine, as long as such delegated responsibilities are
appropriate based on each delegated professional’s education, training, and experience
III.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063,
D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 
2021 002, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 7, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 2020 038, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097,  D 2020 131, D 2020 158, 
D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, and D 2021 002 shall remain in full force 
and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this seventh day of 
January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-009
full_text:
D 2021 009
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296 Declaring a Disaster 
Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296 declaring a state of disaster emergency 
and providing additional funds for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. 
§ 24-33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread 
from person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and this Executive Order. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 

Executive Order D 2021 009 
January 8, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296 to 
allocate an additional $48,084,723 from the Disaster Emergency Fund to CDPHE for the 
following purposes: (1) purchasing and distributing COVID-19 antigen testing kits to educators 
across Colorado ($38,000,000); (2) covering COVID-19 vaccine distribution costs for CDPHE 
($2,061,120) and Local Public Health Agencies ($5,200,000) for the month of January; (3) 
administering a paid vaccine media campaign ($2,000,000); and (4) supporting the Residential 
Strike Task Force that supports the needs of residential care facilities for coordination, staffing, 
and other COVID challenges ($823,603). Given that there are insufficient funds currently 
available in the Disaster Emergency Fund, this Executive Order also transfers $8,447,751 from 
the Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund and 
$39,636,972 from the Major Medical Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund.  
II.
Amendments
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, 
D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296, is amended to read as follows:  
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred eighty three million five 
hundred twenty thousand eight hundred ninety six dollars ($183,520,896) was transferred 
from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for 
COVID-19 related purposes; five million dollars ($5,000,000) was transferred from the 
Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for 
COVID-19 related purposes; thirty three million dollars ($33,000,000) was transferred 
from the Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster 
Emergency Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; and thirty nine million six hundred 
thirty six thousand nine hundred seventy two dollars ($39,636,972) was transferred from 
the Major Medical Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes.  
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that five hundred thirty one million one 
hundred fifty seven thousand eight hundred sixty eight dollars ($531,157,868) from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. 
Of these funds, two hundred and five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds 

Executive Order D 2021 009 
January 8, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
coming from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred eighty four million 
four hundred forty four thousand seven hundred ninety dollars ($184,444,790) shall be 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19; 
zero dollars ($0) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Public 
Safety for response activities related to COVID-19;  twelve million eight hundred eight 
thousand six hundred eighty five dollars ($12,808,685) shall be encumbered and made 
available to the Colorado Department of Corrections for response activities related to 
COVID-19; four million eight hundred and ninety eight thousand two hundred and 
twenty-seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Colorado Department of Labor and Employment for response activities related to 
COVID; one million one hundred and fifty nine thousand eight hundred and ninety 
dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Governor’s 
Office of Information and Technology for response activities related to COVID-19; and 
one million six hundred eighty eight thousand four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408) 
shall be encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Agriculture for 
response activities related to COVID-19.  
In addition, I order that four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred 
fourteen dollars ($4,417,614) from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund be 
encumbered and made available to the Colorado Department of Public Health and 
Environment for response activities related to COVID-19.  
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund 
shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 31, 2021, 
and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. Funds from 
interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available for expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 31, 2021. All other funds from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  

Executive Order D 2021 009
January 8, 2021
Page 4 of 4  
III.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, and this Executive Order, shall expire on January 25, 2021, unless amended further 
by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available for the purposes and the time 
period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, and D 2020 296 shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated.   
GIVEN unde r my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado,  this eighth day of 
January, 2021
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-010
full_text:
D 2021 010 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Ordering the Suspension of Certain Statutes and Directing the Department of Health Care 
Policy and Financing to Provide Additional Funds to Nursing Homes and Other 
Congregate Care Facilities Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order suspending certain statutes 
and directing the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to provide 
additional funds to nursing homes, select community-based providers, and other congregate care 
facilities that care for Coloradans who are aging and/or have disabilities due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296 and D 2021 009. On March 25, 2020, I requested that 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to 
the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. Approximately forty percent of the deaths from COVID-19 in 
Colorado have occurred in nursing homes and other congregate care facilities that serve older 
Coloradans and people living with disabilities. On April 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 054 ordering the suspension of certain statutes and directed HCPF to provide additional 
funds to Nursing Homes and other congregate care facilities. Executive Order D 2020 054, as 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 086, expired on June 27, 2020.  

Executive Order D 2021 010 
 
 
January 8, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
This Executive Order directs HCPF to provide an increase analogous to approximately 
eight (8) percent of the per diem Medicaid Member rate to Nursing Facilities and Intermediate 
Care Facilities. Additionally, this Executive Order directs HCPF to increase certain Home and 
Community-Based Service rates by either eight (8) and ten (10) percent. This Executive Order 
ensures that Coloradans have access to a safe environment and adequate health care facilities and 
facilities serving Coloradans who are aging and/or have disabilities have the resources necessary 
to protect their residents from contracting COVID-19.  
 
II. 
Directives 
 
A. 
I temporarily suspend the requirements of C.R.S. § 25.5-6-202(5) that establish 
the mechanism for determining the amount of supplemental payments for 
Medicaid-certified nursing facilities. 
 
B. 
I temporarily suspend the requirements of C.R.S. §§ 25.5-6-204(1)(a), (1)(b), and 
(2)(a) that establish the rate for reimbursement under the Medicaid program for 
intermediate care facilities to increase reimbursement rates for certified 
intermediate care facilities. 
 
C. 
I direct HCPF to promulgate and issue temporary emergency rules through the 
Medical Services Board, subject to federal upper payment limits, to provide 
supplemental payments by an amount totaling an increase of approximately eight 
(8) percent of the per-diem rate from HCPF to Medicaid-certified nursing 
facilities, as defined in C.R.S. § 25.5-6-202, and intermediate nursing facilities as 
defined in C.R.S. § 25.5-6-204, to protect such facilities’ residents from COVID-
19. HCPF shall make these increased payments through March 31, 2021. HCPF 
must direct the funds to be used by the facilities to provide services that protect 
health and result in better care, higher quality of life for their residents, and 
support for Direct Care Workers during the COVID-19 emergency. 
 
D. 
I direct the Executive Director of HCPF to temporarily increase reimbursement 
rates through March 31, 2021 by eight (8) percent for the following select Home 
and Community-Based Services programs: Group Residential Services and 
Supports (Developmental Disabilities waiver), Brain Injury Supportive Living 
Program (Brain Injury [BI] waiver), and Alternative Care Facilities (Elderly, 
Blind, and Disabled [EBD] waiver and Community Mental Health Supports 
[CMHS] waiver). HCPF must direct the funds to be used by the providers to 
render services that protect health and result in better care, higher quality of life 
for their members, and support for Direct Care Workers during the COVID-19 
emergency. 
 
E. 
I direct the Executive Director of HCPF to temporarily increase reimbursement 
rates programs through March 31, 2021 by ten (10) percent for the following 
Home and Community-Based Services programs: Adult Day Services (EBD, BI, 
CMHS, and Spinal Cord Injury [SCI] waivers), Day Habilitation (DD and 

Executive Order D 2021 010 
January 8, 2021 
Page 3 of 3  
Supported Living Services [SLS] waivers), Non-Medical Transportation (EBD, 
BI, CMHS, SCI, DD, and SLS waivers), and Supported Employment (DD and 
SLS waivers). HCPF must direct the funds to be used by the providers to render 
services that protect health and result in better care, higher quality of life for their 
members, and support for Direct Care Workers during the COVID-19 emergency. 
III.
Duration
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from January 8, 2021, unless extended
further by Executive Order. 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this eighth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-011
full_text:
D 2021 011
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, 
D 2020 220, D 2020 247, and D 2020 277 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain 
Statutes to Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce and Eliminate Cost Sharing for 
COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for Medicaid Enrollees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 
247, and D 2020 277 temporarily suspending certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home 
health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing 
and treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, and D 2020 
277, which suspend certain statutes to preserve the State’s Medicaid home health workforce and 
protect Medicaid enrollees from COVID-19 by reducing the need for in person visits. This 

Executive Order D 2021 011 
January 9, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
Executive Order also extends the temporary suspension of statutory requirements for cost sharing 
for COVID-19 testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139,
D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220,  D 2020 247, D 2020 277, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from January 9, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 
2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, and D 2020 277 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this ninth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-012
full_text:
D 2021 012 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112,  
D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, and D 2020 278 Temporarily 
Suspending Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Criminal Justice  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 016, as amended and extended by D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, 
D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, and D 2020 278 concerning the suspension 
of certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice due to the presence of coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 012 
 
 
January 9, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection.  On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order 
D 2020 016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive 
Orders D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire. On June 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 112, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  On July 
19, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 140, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an 
additional thirty (30) days. On August 17, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 166, which 
extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  On September 15, 2020, I 
issued Executive Order D 2020 192, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional 
thirty (30) days. On October 14, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 221, which extended 
Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On November 12, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 248, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) 
days.  On December 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 278, which extended Sections 
II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. 
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days, 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance.  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, 
D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, 
D 2020 278, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 9, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 016, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, 
D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, and D 2020 278, shall remain in full force 
and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this ninth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-013
full_text:
D 2021 013 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 
145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, D 2020 225, D 2020 249, and D 2020 279 Permitting the 
Limited Recommencement of Voluntary or Elective Surgeries and Procedures in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, 
D 2020 225, D 2020 249, and D 2020 279 to allow voluntary or elective surgeries and 
procedures to proceed under certain conditions due to coronavirus 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, 
D 2020 225, D 2020 249, and D 2020 279, for an additional thirty (30) days to allow voluntary 
or elective surgeries and procedures under certain conditions. 

Executive Order D 2021 013 
 
 
January 10, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
II.   
Amendment 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, D 2020 225, D 2020 
249, D 2020 279, is amended as follows:  
1. Section II.E is amended to read as follows:  
Beginning November 18, 2020, I direct all hospitals to report the following daily in 
EMResource at 10:00 a.m.:  
1. The maximum number of staffed intensive care unit (ICU) beds that can be made 
available for patients in need of ICU level care; and 
2. The maximum number of staffed medical and surgical beds available for patients 
in need of non-ICU hospitalization.  
III. 
Duration 
         Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 080, 
D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, D 2020 225, D 2020 249, D 2020 279, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 10, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and extended 
by D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, D 2020 225, D 2020 249, 
and D 2020 279, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this tenth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-014
full_text:
D 2021 014 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 
111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, and D 2020 280 
Authorizing the Executive Directors of Certain State Agencies to Promulgate and Issue 
Emergency Rules Extending the Expiration Date of Licenses and Other Documents Due to 
the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, 
D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, and D 2020 280 authorizing the executive directors of 
certain State agencies to promulgate and issue emergency rules extending the expiration date of 
licenses and other documents for an additional thirty (30) days due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 

Executive Order D 2021 014 
 
 
January 11, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
  
  
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, 
D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, and D 2020 280, for an additional thirty (30) days. 
II.   
Amendments 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 
224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, is amended as follows:  
1. Delete Section II.D.  
2. Delete Section II.G.  
III. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders, D 2020 
042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, 
D 2020 280, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 11, 2021, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 015, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 
141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, and D 2020 280, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eleventh day of January, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-015
full_text:
D 2021 015 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Activating the Colorado National Guard to Assist with the 59th Presidential Inauguration 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in particular, 
pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order activating those 
members of the Colorado National Guard deemed necessary by the Adjutant General of 
Colorado to assist with the 59th Presidential Inauguration in Washington, D.C. 
I.
Background and Purpose
The peaceful transition of power between Presidential administrations is a hallmark of
our Nation.  The men and women of the Colorado National Guard are being called upon to assist 
in the transition of this power at the 59th Presidential Inauguration in Washington, D.C.  
The United States Secretary of Homeland Security has designated the 59th Presidential 
Inauguration a National Special Security Event.  This designation has allowed the United States 
Secretary of Defense to authorize use of federal funds for security-related purposes during the 
59th Presidential Inauguration, including funding the activation of Colorado National Guard 
personnel. 
The National Guard Bureau and the Commanding General of the District of Columbia 
National Guard have requested that the Colorado National Guard provide personnel to support 
the 59th Presidential Inauguration.  By activating those members of the Colorado National Guard 
deemed necessary by the Adjutant General of Colorado, the National Guard will support the 
DCNG and the National Capital Region with the forces required to safely conduct the 
inauguration. 
II.
Declarations and Directives
A.
On January 13, 2021, the Commanding General of the District of Columbia
National Guard (DCNG) requested support from the Colorado National Guard for
the 59th Presidential Inauguration.  The DCNG has made a similar request to
numerous states.  The United States Secretary of Homeland Security has
designated the 59th Presidential Inauguration a National Special Security Event.
B.
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, the Governor shall be the commander in chief of
the military forces and may employ the Colorado National Guard for service in a

Executive Order D 2021 015 
 
 
January 14, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
National Special Security Event.  I hereby activate those members of the 
Colorado National Guard deemed necessary by the Adjutant General of Colorado 
to assist with the 59th Inauguration of the President of the United States.  The
