activation of the Colorado National Guard ordered pursuant to this Executive 
Order shall be limited to the authority and funding available pursuant to 
32 U.S.C. § 502(f), and shall not be paid for with State funds. 
III.      Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire on January 31, 2021, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this fourteenth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-016
full_text:
D 2021 016 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Directing the Colorado Division of Insurance to Promulgate a Rule Regarding Rates for 
COVID-19 Vaccine Administration 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order directing the Division of 
Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing 
a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance carriers will reimburse providers for the 
cost of COVID-19 vaccine administration.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296 and D 2021 009. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has recently authorized two vaccines for 
prevention of COVID-19, and other COVID-19 vaccines are under development. As COVID-19 
vaccines become more widely available, it is imperative that all Coloradans are able to receive a 
vaccine at no cost.  
To facilitate the prompt and effective delivery of the COVID-19 vaccine and mitigate 
financial disincentives of providing the vaccine, providers who administer the COVID-19 

Executive Order D 2021 016 
 
 
January 15, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
vaccine shall be reasonably compensated by health insurance carriers for the cost of 
administering the vaccine.  
 
This Executive Order directs the Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of 
Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which 
health insurance carriers will reimburse providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine 
administration.  Consistent with federal law, providers receiving taxpayer dollars for 
administering the COVID-19 vaccine shall not collect payment from anyone receiving the 
vaccine.  
 
II. 
Directives 
 
A. 
Pursuant to federal law, any provider that receives taxpayer dollars for 
administering the COVID-19 vaccine shall not collect payment of any kind from 
an individual receiving the COVID-19 vaccine. 
 
B. 
The insurance status of an individual seeking to receive the COVID-19 vaccine 
shall not be used to the individual’s detriment for the delivery of the vaccine. 
 
C. 
In order to ensure the COVID-19 vaccine is widely distributed, I direct the 
Colorado Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory 
Agencies to promulgate a rule, pursuant to authority in C.R.S. §§ 10-1-108(7) and 
10-1-109, establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance 
carriers will reimburse providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine 
administration. 
 
III.     Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from January 15, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this fifteenth day 
of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-017
full_text:
D 2021 017 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, and D 2020 282 Concerning 
the Temporary Suspension of Certain Requirements Preventing Issuance of Marriage 
Licenses Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as extended by D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, 
D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, and D 2020 282, concerning the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing the issuance of marriage licenses when county clerk and 
recorder offices are closed, due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2021 017 
 
 
January 16, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19.  I 
have extended this Executive Order multiple times due to the continuing impact of COVID-19 in 
Colorado.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, and 
D 2020 282, for an additional thirty (30) days. This extension will enable Coloradans to obtain 
marriage licenses while observing social distancing guidelines and minimizing the risk of 
spreading COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
          Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 16, 2021, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 014, 
as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, and D 2020 282, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this sixteenth day of 
January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-018
full_text:
D 2021 018 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, 
D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, and D 2020 283 
Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Due to the Presence 
of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, 
D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, and D 2020 283, concerning the 
temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend the practice of “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet 
or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 018 
 
 
January 16, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 011 for an additional thirty (30) 
days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, 
D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, and 
D 2020 283. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, 
D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from January 16, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, 
D 2020 229, D 2020 257, and D 2020 283 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this sixteenth day of 
January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-019
full_text:
D 2021 019 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency and Authorizing Employment of the Colorado National 
Guard to Assist with Potential Civil Unrest in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of 
disaster emergency due to potential civil unrest in Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and 
C.R.S. §§ 16-2.5-144 and 28-3-104, I hereby activate the Colorado National Guard for the
purposes of protecting life, property, and public health and safety and authorize employing
armed and unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard to assist with potential civil
unrest in Colorado.
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin . . . .” 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3).
During a joint session of the United States Senate and United States House of 
Representatives held on January 6, 2021, to formally count Electoral College votes and certify 
the results of the presidential election, rioters supporting President Trump overwhelmed security 
at the United States Capitol in Washington, D.C.  The rioters occupied the building for several 
hours and five people died, including a Capitol Police officer. 
Following the attack on the United States Capitol, insurrectionists have called for armed 
protests in the capitals of all fifty states leading up to, and on, January 20, 2021, the date of the 
59th Presidential Inauguration.  Over twenty thousand National Guard troops from across the 
nation have been called to provide security in Washington, D.C.  I authorized the activation of 
members of the Colorado National Guard to assist with the inauguration in Washington, D.C. in 
Executive Order D 2021 015. Federal and State officials are closely monitoring the security 
situation in Washington, D.C. and in all state capitals, and the Federal Bureau of Investigation 

Executive Order D 2021 019 
 
 
January 19, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
alerted all states to the risk of additional violence in the next few days, particularly at their 
capitol buildings. 
II.  
Declarations and Directives 
  
A. 
The potential civil unrest in Colorado constitutes a disaster emergency under 
C.R.S § 24-33.5-701, et seq.  I hereby declare a disaster emergency due to 
potential civil unrest in Colorado. 
 
B. 
The State Emergency Operations Plan is hereby activated. All State departments 
and agencies shall take whatever actions may be required and requested by the 
Director or Acting Director of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to 
the Emergency Support Function 5 Annex of the State Emergency Operations 
Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and equipment as necessary. 
 
C. 
Pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and 
C.R.S. §§ 16-2.5-144 and 28-3-104, I have determined that it is in the best interest 
of the people of Colorado to activate and employ armed and unarmed members of 
the Colorado National Guard to protect life, property, and public health and 
safety, and to assist with law enforcement during potential civil unrest in 
Colorado leading up to, and on, January 20, 2021. To ensure timely 
reimbursement to the Colorado National Guard, I authorize encumbering one 
hundred fifty thousand dollars ($150,000) from the Disaster Emergency Fund, 
established in C.R.S § 24-33.5-706(2)(a), to pay the Colorado National Guard for 
costs associated with personnel. Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in 
the Disaster Emergency Fund are hereby found insufficient to pay for the 
Colorado National Guard authorized in this Executive Order. I therefore transfer 
one hundred fifty thousand dollars ($150,000) into the Disaster Emergency Fund 
from the Major Medical Insurance Fund. 
 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate funding to the Colorado 
National Guard and execute awards, purchase orders, or other mechanisms to 
effect the allocation of the funds. The funds described in Section II(C) above shall 
remain available for this purpose until six (6) months from the date of this 
Executive Order, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund. 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 019 
 
 
January 19, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III.     Duration 
  
This Executive Order shall expire on January 23, 2021, unless extended further by 
Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C above shall remain available 
for the described purposes for six (6) months from the date of this Executive Order. 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this nineteenth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-020
full_text:
D 2021 020 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 260 and D 2020 286 Authorizing the Colorado 
Department of Public Health and Environment to Order Hospitals and Freestanding 
Emergency Departments to Transfer or Cease the Admission of Patients to Respond to the 
Current COVID-19 Disaster Emergency in Colorado
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., including 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II), I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue
this Executive Order extending Executive Orders D 2020 260 and D 2020 286 authorizing the
Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) to order hospitals and
freestanding emergency departments to transfer or cease the admission of patients to respond to
the current disaster emergency due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, CDPHE’s public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive
positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. 
I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, 
D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, 
D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID-19 infections.   
Unfortunately, given the increase in infections, the number of persons seeking medical 
treatment at hospitals may far exceed the capacity of any given hospital. Hospitals who have 
reached capacity or are reasonably anticipated to reach capacity during the duration of this 
Executive Order may need to cease admitting patients or may need to transfer patients to a 

Executive Order D 2021 020 
 
 
January 20, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
separate facility without first obtaining the individual’s written or informed consent for such 
transfer. The transfer of patients from hospitals that have reached capacity to other specified care 
facilities will help to ensure that Coloradans have adequate care as we combat the current public 
health emergency due to COVID-19. 
 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II) specifically empowers the Governor, in the event of an 
emergency epidemic that has been declared a disaster emergency, to act by Executive Order to 
order hospitals “to transfer or cease admission of patients or perform medical examinations of 
persons.”  
 
 
The federal Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA), 
42 U.S.C. § 1395dd, requires hospitals receiving Medicare funds to provide appropriate medical 
screening to determine whether a patient has an emergency medical condition. EMTALA 
prohibits a hospital from transferring a patient with an emergency medical condition unless and 
until that condition has been stabilized. One of the purposes of EMTALA is to prevent hospital 
emergency departments from “dumping” patients who are unable to pay. EMTALA also states: 
“The provisions of this section do not preempt any State or local law requirement, except to the 
extent that the requirement directly conflicts with a requirement of this section.” 
42 U.S.C. § 1395dd(f). The Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Centers for 
Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) have waived 42 U.S.C. § 1395dd(a) so that hospitals 
can screen patients at a location offsite from the hospital’s campus to prevent the spread of 
COVID-19. This Executive Order is not intended to conflict with EMTALA, and hospitals must 
still comply with all non-waived sections of EMTALA. 
 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 260 and D 2020 286 authorizing 
CDPHE to order hospitals and freestanding emergency departments to transfer or cease the 
admission of patients to respond to the current disaster emergency due to COVID-19 in Colorado 
within the requirements of federal law. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 020 
January 20, 2021 
Page 3 of 3  
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 260, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 286 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 20, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 260, as amended and 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 286, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twentieth day 
of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-021
full_text:
D 2021 021 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
  
Rescinding Executive Orders D 2020 005, D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, 
D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, D 2020 231, D 2020 263, and  
D 2020 288 Concerning Limiting In-Person Contact for the 2020 Elections and the 
Secretary of State’s Operations Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order rescinding Executive Order 
D 2020 005, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 008, D 2020 036, 
D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, D 2020 231, 
D 2020 263, and D 2020 288 concerning changes to 2020 election activity to limit in-person 
contact while allowing the elections to proceed safely and without interruption to the Secretary 
of State’s operations due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test 
result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and 
we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
and D 2021 009. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States 
declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. We must continue to take action to 
minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our daily lives. The virus that 
causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and through 
respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2021 021 
January 21, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
On January 20, 2021, I signed HB 21-1001, Concerning Authorization for Remote 
Participation in Political Party Committee Meetings.  This bill allows, through the end of 
2021, for remote participation in certain party matters if allowed by the party’s rules.  The 
bill also permits certain party committees to adopt party rules or bylaws to implement the 
bill’s remote participation provisions.  
In light of HB 21-1001 becoming law, Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended 
and extended by D 2020 008, D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, 
D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, D 2020 231, D 2020 263, and D 2020 288, is no 
longer necessary and is rescinded.   
II.
Directive
I rescind Executive Order D 2020 005, as amended and extended by D 2020 008, 
D 2020 036, D 2020 061, D 2020 095, D 2020 129, D 2020 155, D 2020 179, D 2020 209, 
D 2020 231, D 2020 263, and D 2020 288. 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-first 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-022
full_text:
D 2021 022 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009 Declaring a 
Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and D 2021 009 declaring a state of disaster 
emergency and providing additional funds for response activities due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State. 
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” 
C.R.S. § 24- 33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can
spread from person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 

Executive Order D 2021 022 
 
 
January 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and this Executive 
Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, and 
D 2021 009 to extend the state of disaster emergency and the deployment of the Colorado 
National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they 
respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State.  
 
II.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 
25, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section 
II.C of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available for the 
described purposes and the described time period stated in Section II.C.  In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, and D 2021 009 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-fifth 
day of January, 2021.  
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-023
full_text:
D 2021 023 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 235, D 2020 265, and D 2020 289 Establishing 
Directives for the COVID-19 Dial Framework 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265 and D 2020 289, 
establishing directives for the Dial Framework due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and D 2021 022. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes, speaks, or sings. It can 
also be spread through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we 
practice Social Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other 
people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at 
Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured 
that Coloradans who needed it had access to healthcare resources.  

Executive Order D 2021 023 
 
 
January 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended 
and extended by Executive Order D 2020 024, taking a wide array of actions to mitigate the 
effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect against overwhelming our health care 
resources. Stay at Home required Coloradans to stay at home, subject to certain limited 
exceptions, and ordered the Executive Director of the CDPHE to issue a public health order 
(PHO) defining critical emergency personnel, infrastructure, government functions, and other 
activities exempt from the Stay at Home directives. Under Stay at Home and social distancing, 
the increase in cases and rate of infection slowed.  
 
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044, as amended and extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 079, and the Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive 
Order 2020 091, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 123, D 2020 142, D 2020 
144, D 2020 170, D 2020 199, and D 2020 213, I took steps to return Coloradans to work while 
continuing the efforts to “flatten the curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home 
and Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to practice 
Social Distancing from others, limit social interactions, take extra precautions for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wear non-medical masks while in public to facilitate 
reopening the economy.  
 
On July 9, 2020, I issued Protect Our Neighbors Executive Order D 2020 127, as 
amended or extended by D 2020 154, D 2020 178, D 2020 207, which implemented a number of 
measures giving local communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still protecting 
the public’s health. Under Protect Our Neighbors, local communities that have met certain 
qualifications and have an approved mitigation and containment plan may allow all activities to 
occur at fifty perfect (50%) capacity with Social Distancing with no more than five hundred 
(500) people in any one setting. Qualifying local communities are permitted to issue more 
detailed guidelines or public health orders to meet the needs of their community as long as the 
capacity did not exceed the Statewide limit. Because each community and each part of our State 
has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows communities to create unique and flexible 
plans within the statewide guidelines as we continue to open our economy and support people 
who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19.  
 
On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 235, superseding and replacing 
Executive Orders Protect Our Neighbors and Safer at Home in the Vast, Great Outdoors and 

Executive Order D 2021 023 
 
 
January 25, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
 
establishing directives for the COVID-19 Dial Framework. On November 28, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 265, amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 235 to include 
Level Purple. On December 27, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 289, extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 235 and D 2020 265 for an additional thirty days. The Dial Framework 
recognizes unique local circumstances and uses an intuitive dial to visualize a community’s 
success in containing the spread of COVID-19.  
 
Each county or region is at one of the six levels represented by colors on the Dial, which 
range from least restrictive to most restrictive. The goal of each level is to strike the important 
balance between enabling economic and social activity while ensuring that our testing, contact 
tracing, and health care systems are able to contain the virus. Each level is defined by objective 
scientific metrics such as the number of new cases, percent positivity of COVID-19 tests, and 
hospitalizations. Based on these metrics, the dial assigns certain limitations and restrictions to 
contain the virus, including capacity limitations. A community may move between dial levels 
based on the metrics and in consultation with State and local public health officials to ensure 
unique local conditions are appropriately considered. If a county is moved “back to normal” off 
the dial entirely because of a major reduction in transmission or risk, it will no longer be subject 
to this Executive Order and the corresponding PHO.   
 
 
 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 265 and D 2020 289, for an additional thirty (30) days due to the 
continued presence of COVID-19 in Colorado. 
 
  

Executive Order D 2021 023 
January 25, 2021 
Page 4 of 4  
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265,
D 2020 289, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 25, 2021, 
unless amended further by Executive Order.  In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 235, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265 and D 2020 289, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-fifth 
day of January, 2021.

title: D 2021-024
full_text:
D 2021 024 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, and D 2020 291 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, 
and Behavioral Health Due to the Presence of COVID-19  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, and D 2020 291, ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile justice, regional centers, and 
behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and 
D 2021 022. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 024 
 
 
January 27, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.  I amended and extended that Executive Order in Executive 
Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, 
D 2020 236, D 2020 266, and D 2020 291.   
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A, II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, II.H, and II.I for an 
additional thirty (30) days, to enable CDHS and the Division of Youth Services to continue to 
respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of COVID-19 in 
their facilities. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from January 27, 2021, 
unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 034, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, 
D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, and D 2020 291 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the State 
of Colorado, this twenty-
seventh day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

full_text:

EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Order D 2020 292 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Statute to 
Allow the Use of Telehealth Services for Veterinarians Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 292, ordering the temporary suspension of statute to allow the use of telehealth services 
for veterinarians due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and D 2021 022. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and preserve 
our health care resources. COVID-19 is a highly contagious viral disease that has spread 
throughout many Colorado communities, across the country, and across the world. To protect the 
public health and mitigate exposure to and the spread of COVID-19, Colorado veterinarians, 
must be able to use telehealth services whenever possible. These services, including low-cost 
telephone, internet, audio-only, and live video. On December 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order

GIVEN under my hand and the

to reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies due to the presence 
of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in the State.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, CDPHE’s public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive
positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. 
I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 
2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 
205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, and D 2021 022. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. On May 16, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 066, which reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies during 
State Fiscal Year 2019-2020.  
On December 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 293, which directed CDPHE to 
reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies to COVID-19 response 
activities and permitted the use of certain funds to conduct research to better serve minority and 
traditionally disadvantaged communities.  

Executive Order D 2021 026 
 
 
January 27, 2021

This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 293 for an additional thirty (30) 
days, to allow CDPHE to reprioritize the use of existing funds and provide flexibility to local 
public health agencies as they respond to COVID-19 during State Fiscal Year 2020-2021. 
II.   
Duration 
 
  
Executive Order D 2020 293, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from January 27, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 293 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-seventh 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-027
full_text:
D 2021 027 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Order D 2020 294 Temporarily Suspending Certain Deadlines to 
Allow the Continued Use of Federal Funds from the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and 
Economic Security Act of 2020 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 294, temporarily suspending certain deadlines to allow the continued use of federal funds 
from Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020, Public Law 116-136 
(CARES Act) due to the presence of due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, and D 2021 
022. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that
request on March 28, 2020.
On March 27, 2020, the federal CARES Act was enacted to provide emergency 
assistance in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CARES Act established the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund, which provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments navigating the 
impact of COVID-19. On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended and 
extended, to direct the expenditure of these funds. In Executive Order D 2020 070, I allocated 
seventy million dollars to the General Fund for eligible expenditures pursuant to the CARES Act 
Coronavirus Relief Fund. The CARES Act originally required these funds only cover expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 until December 30, 2020. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021, which was signed into law on December 28, 2020, extends the time period during which 
state can incur expenses covered by the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from December 
30, 2020 to December 31, 2021. A number of Colorado state statutes and enacted legislation 

Executive Order D 2021 027 
 
 
January 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
appropriating CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars conformed to the previous federal 
deadline. These deadlines were imposed by the General Assembly to comply with the original 
CARES Act deadline. This deadline was extended by the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 294 for an additional thirty (30) 
days to temporarily suspend certain deadlines to allow the continued use of Coronavirus Relief 
Fund dollars from the CARES Act. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from January 28, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 294 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth 
day of January, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-046
full_text:
D 2021 046 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 260, D 2020 286, and D 2021 020 Authorizing the 
Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment to Order Hospitals and 
Freestanding Emergency Departments to Transfer or Cease the Admission of Patients to 
Respond to the Current COVID-19 Disaster Emergency in Colorado
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., including 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II), I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue
this Executive Order extending Executive Orders D 2020 260, D 2020 286, and D 2021 020
authorizing the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) to order
hospitals and freestanding emergency departments to transfer or cease the admission of patients
to respond to the current disaster emergency due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in
Colorado.
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, CDPHE’s public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive
positive COVID-19 test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. 
I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, 
D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, 
D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID-19 infections.   
Unfortunately, given the increase in infections, the number of persons seeking medical 
treatment at hospitals may far exceed the capacity of any given hospital. Hospitals who have 

Executive Order D 2021 046 
 
 
February 18, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
reached capacity or are reasonably anticipated to reach capacity during the duration of this 
Executive Order may need to cease admitting patients or may need to transfer patients to a 
separate facility without first obtaining the individual’s written or informed consent for such 
transfer. The transfer of patients from hospitals that have reached capacity to other specified care 
facilities will help to ensure that Coloradans have adequate care as we combat the current public 
health emergency due to COVID-19. 
 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704.5(1)(e)(II) specifically empowers the Governor, in the event of an 
emergency epidemic that has been declared a disaster emergency, to act by Executive Order to 
order hospitals “to transfer or cease admission of patients or perform medical examinations of 
persons.”  
 
 
The federal Emergency Medical Treatment and Active Labor Act (EMTALA), 
42 U.S.C. § 1395dd, requires hospitals receiving Medicare funds to provide appropriate medical 
screening to determine whether a patient has an emergency medical condition. EMTALA 
prohibits a hospital from transferring a patient with an emergency medical condition unless and 
until that condition has been stabilized. One of the purposes of EMTALA is to prevent hospital 
emergency departments from “dumping” patients who are unable to pay. EMTALA also states: 
“The provisions of this section do not preempt any State or local law requirement, except to the 
extent that the requirement directly conflicts with a requirement of this section.” 
42 U.S.C. § 1395dd(f). The Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Centers for 
Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) have waived 42 U.S.C. § 1395dd(a) so that hospitals 
can screen patients at a location offsite from the hospital’s campus to prevent the spread of 
COVID-19. This Executive Order is not intended to conflict with EMTALA, and hospitals must 
still comply with all non-waived sections of EMTALA. 
 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 260, D 2020 286, and 
D 2021 020, authorizing CDPHE to order hospitals and freestanding emergency departments to 
transfer or cease the admission of patients to respond to the current disaster emergency due to 
COVID-19 in Colorado within the requirements of federal law. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 046 
 
 
February 18, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
II.      Duration 
 
 
Executive Orders D 2020 260, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 286, D 2021 020, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from February 
18, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order 
D 2020 260, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 286 and D 2021 020, shall 
remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this eighteenth day of 
February, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. On May 16, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 066, which reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies during 
State Fiscal Year 2019-2020.  
On December 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 293, which directed CDPHE to 
reallocate existing funds from contracts with local public health agencies to COVID-19 response 
activities and permitted the use of certain funds to conduct research to better serve minority and 
traditionally disadvantaged communities. On January 27, 2021, I issued Executive Order 2021 
026, extending Executive Order D 2020 293.  

Executive Order D 2021 050 
 
 
February 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 293 and 2021 026 for an 
additional thirty (30) days, to allow CDPHE to reprioritize the use of existing funds and provide 
flexibility to local public health agencies as they respond to COVID-19 during State Fiscal Year 
2020-2021. 
II.   
Duration 
  
 
Executive Order D 2020 293, as extended by Executive Order 2021 026, and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from February 25, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 293, as extended by Executive 
Order 2021 026, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-fifth day 
of February, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-051
full_text:
D 2021 051 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 294 and D 2021 027 Temporarily Suspending Certain 
Deadlines to Allow the Continued Use of Federal Funds from the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, 
and Economic Security Act of 2020 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 294 and D 2021 027, temporarily suspending certain deadlines to allow the 
continued use of federal funds from Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 
2020, Public Law 116-136 (CARES Act) due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
On March 27, 2020, the federal CARES Act was enacted to provide emergency 
assistance in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CARES Act established the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund, which provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments navigating the 
impact of COVID-19. On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended and 
extended, to direct the expenditure of these funds. In Executive Order D 2020 070, I allocated 
seventy million dollars to the General Fund for eligible expenditures pursuant to the CARES Act 
Coronavirus Relief Fund. The CARES Act originally required these funds only cover expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 until December 30, 2020. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021, which was signed into law on December 28, 2020, extends the time period during which 
states can incur expenses covered by the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from December 

Executive Order D 2021 051 
February 26, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
30, 2020 to December 31, 2021. A number of Colorado state statutes and enacted legislation 
appropriating CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars conformed to the previous federal 
deadline. These deadlines were imposed by the General Assembly to comply with the original 
CARES Act deadline. This deadline was extended by the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 294 and D 2021 027 for an 
additional thirty (30) days to temporarily suspend certain deadlines to allow the continued use of 
Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars from the CARES Act. 
II.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by Executive Order D 2021 027 and this
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from February 26, 2021 unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2021 027, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-sixth 
day of February, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in

307, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2021 029, providing protections to 
residential and commercial tenants from late fees and notice of the Consolidated Appropriations 
Act, 2021, Section 502.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020

States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the

residential and commercial tenants from late fees and notice of the Consolidated Appropriations

extended by Executive Order D 2021 029, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the
