title: D 2021-054
full_text:
D 2021 054 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, 
D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, and D 2021 031 Temporarily Suspending 
Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 
241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, and D 2021 031, which temporarily suspend certain statutes to 
maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 
009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
 
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 

Executive Order D 2021 054 
 
 
March 1, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
272, D 2021 003, and D 2021 031, which ensures compliance with the Families First 
Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending certain statutes to allow for continued 
eligibility or benefits status for those individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 2020, and 
to prevent any changes in eligibility, and directs the Executive Director of the Department of 
Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees for those 
enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, 
D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 1, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 
272, D 2021 003, and D 2021 031, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this first 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-055
full_text:
D 2021 055 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, 
D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, and D 2021 032 Ordering Expedited Unemployment 
Insurance Claim Processing to Provide Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, and D 2021 032, ordering expedited 
unemployment insurance (UI) claim processing due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 
076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 
2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications 
that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten 

Executive Order D 2021 055 
 
 
March 1, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely on unemployment 
insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, 
D 2021 004, and D 2021 032, which expedites processing of unemployment insurance claims for 
Coloradans and will help protect the economic well-being of Colorado’s communities impacted 
by COVID-19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, 
D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 1, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended 
by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 
273, D 2021 004, and D 2021 032, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this first 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-056
full_text:
D 2021 056 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, 
D 2020 138,  D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, 
D 2020 281, D 2021 007, and D 2021 035 Ordering Individuals in Colorado to Wear 
Non-Medical Face Coverings 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, 
D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, and 
D 2021 035, ordering individuals in Colorado to wear a medical or non-medical face covering 
due to the presence of coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. While we have seen indications that our efforts 
to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten Coloradans’ 
way of life and livelihoods and current data shows a recent increase in COVID infections. As we 
take steps to return Coloradans to work, we must continue to take measures to facilitate 
reopening the economy while protecting public health by taking steps to incorporate best 
practices to protect individuals from infection. 
 

Executive Order D 2021 056 
March 5, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
We have learned that widespread mask use is a low cost and highly effective way to 
reduce the spread of COVID-19 infections by as much as 65%. Broad adoption of mask wearing 
in Colorado may have significant economic benefits by allowing the State to prevent re-closures 
of businesses and schools and, ultimately, return to our normal lives more quickly. A recent 
study from Goldman Sachs concluded that a federal mask mandate could save the U.S. economy 
from taking a 5% hit to the Gross Domestic Product (GDP). When I first implemented this 
Executive Order, thirty-nine Colorado counties and municipalities already had mandatory mask 
orders in place. 
 
Under Executive Order D 2020 110, mask wearing is mandatory for employees, 
contractors, and others providing services for Mass Transportation Operations and Critical 
Businesses as well as for State and county employees and the individuals they serve at 
Government Offices and Facilities. These requirements remain intact with this Executive Order. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, 
D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, 
D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, and D 2021 035 to protect Coloradans by requiring 
individuals in Colorado to wear a non-medical face covering over their nose and mouth, subject 
to several exceptions. 
 
II.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from March 5, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, 
D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, and D 2021 035 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fifth 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-057
full_text:
D 2021 057 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, 
D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, and D 2021 036 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes and Rules to Expand the 
Healthcare Workforce for Hospitals and Other Inpatient Treatment Facilities Due to the 
Presence of COVID-19 
         Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant

D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 
131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, 
and D 2021 036, ordering the temporary suspension of certain statutes and rules to expand the 
healthcare workforce for hospitals, inpatient treatment facilities, and administration of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) vaccinations in outpatient settings due to the presence of 
COVID-19 in Colorado. 
I.       Background and Purpose 
         On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 
032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 
2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.     
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, 
D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, and D 2021 036, for an additional thirty (30) 

Executive Order D 2021 056 
 
 
March 6, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
days to ensure that inpatient healthcare facilities have sufficient resources and personnel to treat 
patients suffering from COVID-19 and to ensure that inpatient facilities and outpatient settings 
have sufficient resources to administer COVID-19 vaccinations. 
II.  
Duration 
         Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, 
D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 
2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from 
March 6, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 
2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097,  D 2020 131, 
D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, and D 
2021 036 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this sixth 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-059
full_text:
D 2021 059 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, 
D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, and D 2021 038 Ordering the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Statutes to Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce and 
Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for Medicaid Enrollees 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 
247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, and D 2021 038, which temporarily suspend certain statutes to 
increase the Medicaid home health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
 
I.         Background and Purpose  
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United 
States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The 
President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 
277, D 2021 011, and D 2021 038, which suspend certain statutes to preserve the State’s 
Medicaid home health workforce and protect Medicaid enrollees from COVID-19 by reducing 

Executive Order D 2021 059 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
the need for in person visits. This Executive Order also extends the temporary suspension of 
statutory requirements for cost sharing for COVID-19 testing and treatment for Medicaid 
enrollees. 
II.   
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, 
D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220,  D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 8, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 077, as extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 
277, D 2021 011, and D 2021 038, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this eighth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-060
full_text:
D 2021 060 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112,  
D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, D 2020 278, D 2021 012, and 
D 2021 039 Temporarily Suspending Certain Regulatory Statutes Concerning  
Criminal Justice  
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 016, as amended and extended by D 2020 043, D2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, 
D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, D 2020 278, D 2021 012, and D 2021 039, 
concerning the suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 060 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection.  On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order 
D 2020 016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive 
Orders D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire.  
 
On June 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 112, which extended Sections II.A 
and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On July 19, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 140, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On August 
17, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 166, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an 
additional thirty (30) days.  On September 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 192, which 
extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On October 14, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 221, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) 
days. On November 12, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 248, which extended Sections 
II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On December 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 278, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On January 
9, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 012, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an 
additional thirty (30) days. On February 7, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 039, which 
extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. 
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days, 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 060 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, 
D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, 
D 2020 278, D 2021 012, D 2021 039, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from March 8, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive 
Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, D 2020 078, 
D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, D 2020 278, 
D 2021 012, and D 2021 039, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this eighth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-061
full_text:
D 2021 061 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, and D 2021 045 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of 
Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and 
D 2021 045 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional funds for response 
activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.  
Background and Purpose 
  
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 

Executive Order D 2021 061 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045, to allocate an additional $78,494,066 
from the Disaster Emergency Fund to Executive Branch Departments, including, $70,000,000 to 
the Department of Public Health and Environment for response activities related to COVID-19; 
$2,356,022 to the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing for response activities related 
to COVID-19; $4,600,000 to the Department of Corrections for response activities related to 
COVID-19; and, $1,538,044 to the Department of Human Services for response activities related 
to COVID-19.  
 
II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, 
D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045, is 
amended to read as follows:  
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred sixty two million fourteen 
thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars ($162,014,962) was transferred from the 
Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes; five million dollars ($5,000,000) was transferred from the Unclaimed 
Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes; thirty three million dollars ($33,000,000) was transferred from the 
Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund 
for COVID-19 related purposes; and thirty nine million six hundred thirty six thousand 
nine hundred seventy two dollars ($39,636,972) was transferred from the Major Medical 
Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 related purposes.  
 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that five hundred nine million six hundred 
fifty one thousand nine hundred thirty four dollars ($509,651,934) from the Disaster 
Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these 
funds, two hundred five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds coming from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred eighty four million four hundred forty 
four thousand seven hundred ninety dollars ($184,444,790) shall be encumbered and 
made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19; zero dollars ($0) 

Executive Order D 2021 061 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Public Safety for response 
activities related to COVID-19; twelve million eight hundred eight thousand six hundred 
eighty five dollars ($12,808,685) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Department of Corrections for response activities related to COVID-19; four million 
eight hundred ninety eight thousand two hundred twenty-seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall 
be encumbered and made available to the Department of Labor and Employment for 
response activities related to COVID-19; one million one hundred fifty nine thousand 
eight hundred ninety dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Governor’s Office of Information and Technology for response activities related to 
COVID-19; and one million six hundred eighty eight thousand four hundred eight dollars 
($1,688,408) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Agriculture 
for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
In addition, I order that four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred 
fourteen dollars ($4,417,614) from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund be 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund 
shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 31, 2021, 
and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. Funds from 
interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available for expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 31, 2021. All other funds from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 061 
 
 
March 8, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
 
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire on March 16, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds 
described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain 
available for the described purposes and the described time period stated in Section II.C. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, 
D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, 
D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, and D 2021 045 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this eighth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-062
full_text:
D 2021 062 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 
2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, D 2020 225, D 2020 249, D 2020 279, D 2021 013, 
and D 2021 040 Permitting the Limited Recommencement of Voluntary or Elective 
Surgeries and Procedures in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 045, D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, 
D 2020 225, D 2020 249, D 2020 279, D 2021 013, and D 2021 040 to allow voluntary or 
elective surgeries and procedures to proceed in Colorado. 
 
 
I.         Background and Purpose  
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral

This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 080, D 2020 114, D 2020 145, D 2020 169, D 2020 198, 
D 2020 225, D 2020 249, D 2020 279, D 2021 013, and D 2021 040, for an additional thirty (30) 

Executive Order D 2021 062 
 
 
March 9, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
 
days to allow voluntary or elective surgeries and procedures to proceed with certain reporting

II.

Section II of Executive Order D 2020 045, as amended and extended by Executive

but to eliminate the other limitations in this Executive Order as follows: 
1. Strike sections II.A., II.B., II.F., II.G., and II.H. 
2. Strike section II.C. and replace with the following text:  
I direct the Executive Director of CDPHE to issue a PHO consistent with the

III.

GIVEN under my hand and the

Jared Polis
