title: D 2021-063
full_text:
D 2021 063 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, 
D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, and D 2021 041 
Authorizing the Executive Directors of Certain State Agencies to Promulgate and Issue 
Emergency Rules Extending the Expiration Date of Licenses and Other Documents Due to 
the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 
195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, and D 2021 041, authorizing the 
executive directors of certain State agencies to promulgate and issue emergency rules extending 
the expiration date of licenses and other documents for an additional thirty (30) days due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I.         Background and Purpose  
  
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 

Executive Order D 2021 063 
 
 
March 10, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
  
 This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, 
D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, and D 2021 041, for an additional thirty (30) 
days. 
II. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders, D 2020 
042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, 
D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from March 10, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 
2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 
280, D 2021 014, and D 2021 041, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this tenth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-064
full_text:
D 2021 064 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to Severe Winter Weather Conditions in Colorado 
and Authorizing Deployment of the Colorado National Guard 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the severe winter weather conditions in Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado constitution and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I authorize employing the Colorado National Guard to provide search, rescue, 
and shelter assistance related to the severe winter weather conditions in Colorado. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented 
by disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster 
as “the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin . . . .” C.R.S. § 24-33.5­
703(3). 
On March 11, 2021, the National Weather Service predicted that up to 30 inches of snow 
may fall in portions of Colorado’s northern metropolitan area from March 12-15, 2021. Winter 
weather may also impact Colorado’s mountains and eastern plains. The Colorado Department of 
Transportation is encouraging people in Colorado to avoid driving during the storm because 
similar past winter storms have stranded vehicles and people in impacted areas.  
Local first responder capabilities may be exceeded by the impacts of the storm, and such 
impacted localities may require State support. The Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management activated the State Emergency Operations Center to support and coordinate search 
and rescue activities and other requests from impacted jurisdictions. The Division of Homeland 
Security and Emergency Management Regional Field Managers are supporting local response 
efforts. 
At approximately 11:50 AM on March 11, 2021, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
for the severe winter weather conditions in Colorado and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. I also verbally authorized employment and use of the Colorado 
National Guard for the purpose of providing search and rescue and shelter assistance related to 

Executive Order D 2021 064 
 
 
March 12, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
the severe winter weather conditions in Colorado from 12:00 PM on March 12, 2021 through 
12:00 PM on March 15, 2021. 
II. 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The severe winter weather conditions and associated impacts in Colorado constitute a 
disaster emergency under C.R.S § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My March 11, 2021 verbal order 
declaring a disaster emergency is memorialized by this Executive Order and shall have 
the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive Order. 
B. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, my March 11, 2021 verbal order authorizing the 
employment and use of the Colorado National Guard to provide search, rescue, and 
shelter assistance related to the severe winter weather conditions in Colorado from 12:00 
PM on March 12, 2021 through 12:00 PM on March 15, 2021 is memorialized by this 
Executive Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained 
within this Executive Order. 
C. 
The State Emergency Operations Plan is activated. All State departments and agencies 
shall take whatever actions necessary as requested by the Director or Acting Director of 
the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to the Emergency Support Function 5 
Annex of the State Emergency Operations Plan, including providing appropriate staff and 
equipment. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), the funds regularly appropriated to State and local 
agencies are insufficient to address this disaster emergency. Therefore, I order that two 
hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) be transferred from the Controlled Maintenance 
Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund and that two hundred thousand dollars 
($200,000) be encumbered from the Disaster Emergency Fund to pay for the State-level 
response, recovery, and mitigation efforts related to this disaster emergency.  These funds 
shall remain available for this purpose until six (6) months from the date of this 
Executive Order, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency 
Fund. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the appropriate 
government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute awards, purchase orders, 
or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of the funds.  

Executive Order D 2021 064 
 
 
March 12, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
III. 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from March 11, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except as described in Section II.D above. 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twelfth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-065
full_text:
D 2021 065 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2021 016 and D 2021 042 Directing the Colorado Division of 
Insurance to Promulgate a Rule Regarding Rates for COVID-19 Vaccine Administration 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2021 016 and D 2021 042 directing the Division of Insurance in the Colorado 
Department of Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) 
by which health insurance carriers will reimburse providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine 
administration.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has recently authorized two vaccines for 
prevention of COVID-19, and other COVID-19 vaccines are under development. As COVID-19 
vaccines become more widely available, it is imperative that all Coloradans are able to receive a 
vaccine at no cost.  
 
To facilitate the prompt and effective delivery of the COVID-19 vaccine and mitigate 
financial disincentives of providing the vaccine, providers who administer the COVID-19 

Executive Order D 2021 065 
 
 
March 14, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
 
vaccine shall be reasonably compensated by health insurance carriers for the cost of 
administering the vaccine.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2021 016 and D 2021 042 directing 
the Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a 
rule establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance carriers will reimburse 
providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine administration.  Consistent with federal law, 
providers receiving taxpayer dollars for administering the COVID-19 vaccine shall not collect 
payment from anyone receiving the vaccine.  
 
II.     Duration 
Executive Order D 2021 016, as extended by Executive Order D 2021 042 and this 
Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 14, 2021, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 2021 016, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2021 042, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fourteenth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-066
full_text:
D 2021 066 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, and 
D 2021 043 Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain Requirements Preventing 
Issuance of Marriage Licenses Due to the Presence of COVID-19 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as extended by D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, 
D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, and D 2021 043, 
concerning the temporary suspension of certain statutes preventing the issuance of marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed, due to the presence of coronavirus 
disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of 
the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2021 066 
 
 
March 15, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
 
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19.  I 
have extended this Executive Order multiple times due to the continuing impact of COVID-19 in 
Colorado.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, D 2021 017, and D 2021 043, for an additional thirty (30) days. This extension will 
enable Coloradans to obtain marriage licenses while observing social distancing guidelines and 
minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
          Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from March 15, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, D 2021 017, and D 2021 043, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fifteenth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-067
full_text:
D 2021 067 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, 
D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, 
D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, and D 2021 044 Concerning the Temporary 
Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, 
D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, and 
D 2021 044, concerning the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend the practice of “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet 
or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 067 
 
 
March 15, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 011 for an additional 
thirty (30) days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, 
D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, 
D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, and D 2021 044.  The amendment temporarily suspends 
statute related to Title Board proceedings in order to facilitate remote participation. 
 
II.  
Amendment 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, 
D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, and D 2021 044, is 
amended to include the following:  
 
W. 
I temporarily suspend the provisions in C.R.S. § 1-40-106 that specify the time, place, 
and manner that the Title Board must conduct its business, and direct the Secretary of 
State to issue guidance outlining the time, place, and manner in which Title Board 
hearings will be conducted for the duration of this Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 067 
 
 
March 15, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III. 
Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, 
D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, D 2021 044, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from March 15, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, 
D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, and D 2021 044, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fifteenth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-068
full_text:
D 2021 068 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022,  
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061 Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the 
Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, and D 2021 061 declaring a state of disaster emergency and providing additional 
funds for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” 
C.R.S. § 24- 33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can
spread from person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act.
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 

Executive Order D 2021 068 
 
 
March 16, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 2020, I requested 
that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, and D 2021 061 for an additional thirty (30) 
days to extend the state of disaster emergency and the deployment of the Colorado National 
Guard to support and provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to 
the presence of COVID-19 in the State. 
 
 
II.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and this Executive 
Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 16, 2021, unless extended further by Executive 
Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended, shall remain available for the described purposes and the described time 
period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, 
D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, and D 2021 061, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this sixteenth day of 
March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-069
full_text:
D 2021 069 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 235, D 2020 265, D 2020 289, D 2021 
023, and D 2021 047 Establishing Directives for the COVID-19 Dial Framework 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by D 2020 265, D 2020 289, D 2021 
023, and D 2021 047 establishing directives for the Dial Framework due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 
045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the 
United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. 
The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes, speaks, or sings. It can 
also be spread through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we 
practice Social Distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other 
people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. This is especially important for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19. Evidence shows that Social Distancing and the Stay at 
Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended, helped to slow the rate of infection and ensured 
that Coloradans who needed it had access to healthcare resources.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  

Executive Order D 2021 069 
March 24, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
On March 25, 2020, I issued the Stay at Home Executive Order D 2020 017, as amended 
and extended by Executive Order D 2020 024, taking a wide array of actions to mitigate the 
effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect against overwhelming our health care 
resources. Stay at Home required Coloradans to stay at home, subject to certain limited 
exceptions, and ordered the Executive Director of the CDPHE to issue a public health order 
(PHO) defining critical emergency personnel, infrastructure, government functions, and other 
activities exempt from the Stay at Home directives. Under Stay at Home and social distancing, 
the increase in cases and rate of infection slowed.  
Through the Safer at Home Executive Order D 2020 044, as amended and extended by 
Executive Order D 2020 079, and the Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors Executive 
Order 2020 091, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 123, D 2020 142, D 2020 
144, D 2020 170, D 2020 199, and D 2020 213, I took steps to return Coloradans to work while 
continuing the efforts to “flatten the curve” and protect the public’s health. Under Safer at Home 
and Safer at Home and in the Vast, Great Outdoors, Coloradans were encouraged to practice 
Social Distancing from others, limit social interactions, take extra precautions for individuals at 
risk of severe illness from COVID-19, and wear non-medical masks while in public to facilitate 
reopening the economy.  
On July 9, 2020, I issued Protect Our Neighbors Executive Order D 2020 127, as 
amended or extended by D 2020 154, D 2020 178, D 2020 207, which implemented a number of 
measures giving local communities the opportunity to create flexible plans while still protecting 
the public’s health. Under Protect Our Neighbors, local communities that have met certain 
qualifications and have an approved mitigation and containment plan may allow all activities to 
occur at fifty perfect (50%) capacity with Social Distancing with no more than five hundred 
(500) people in any one setting. Qualifying local communities are permitted to issue more
detailed guidelines or public health orders to meet the needs of their community as long as the
capacity did not exceed the Statewide limit. Because each community and each part of our State
has different needs, Protect Our Neighbors allows communities to create unique and flexible
plans within the statewide guidelines as we continue to open our economy and support people
who are at increased risk for severe illness from COVID-19.
On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 235, superseding and replacing 
Executive Orders Protect Our Neighbors and Safer at Home in the Vast, Great Outdoors and 
establishing directives for the COVID-19 Dial Framework. On November 28, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 265, amending and extending Executive Order D 2020 235 to include 
Level Purple. On December 27, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 289, extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 235 and D 2020 265 for an additional thirty days. On January 25, 
2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 023, extending Executive Orders D 2020 235, D 2020 
265, and D 2020 289 for an additional thirty days. On February 23, 2021, I issued Executive 
Order D 2021 047, extending Executive Orders D 2020 235, D 2020 265, D 2020 289, and  D 
2021 023, for an additional thirty days. The Dial Framework recognizes unique local 
circumstances and uses an intuitive dial to visualize a community’s success in containing the 
spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 069 
March 24, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
Each county or region is at one of the six levels represented by colors on the Dial, which 
range from least restrictive to most restrictive. The goal of each level is to strike the important 
balance between enabling economic and social activity while ensuring that our testing, contact 
tracing, and health care systems are able to contain the virus. Each level is defined by objective 
scientific metrics such as the number of new cases, percent positivity of COVID-19 tests, and 
hospitalizations. Based on these metrics, the dial assigns certain limitations and restrictions to 
contain the virus, including capacity limitations. A community may move between dial levels 
based on the metrics and in consultation with State and local public health officials to ensure 
unique local conditions are appropriately considered. If a county is moved “back to normal” off 
the dial entirely because of a major reduction in transmission or risk, it will no longer be subject 
to this Executive Order and the corresponding PHO.   
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended 
and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265 D 2020 289, D 2021 023, and D 2021 047, and 
until April 16, 2021 due to the continued presence of COVID-19 in Colorado. 
II.
Amendment
Section III of Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive
Orders D 2020 265, D 2020 289, D 2021 023, and D 2021 047, is amended as follows: 
1. In Section III.C., strike the words “Blue and Yellow” in the first sentence and
replace with the phrase “Blue, Yellow, and Orange”. 

Executive Order D 2021 069 
March 24, 2021 
Page 4 of 4  
III.
Duration
Executive Order D 2020 235, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265 
D 2020 289, D 2021 023, D 2021 047, and this Executive Order, shall expire April 16, 2021 
unless amended further by Executive Order.  In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 235, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 265 D 2020 289, D 2021 023, and D 2021 
047, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-fourth 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-070
full_text:
D 2021 070 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, 
D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, 
D 2021 024, and D 2021 048 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory 
Statutes Concerning Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, and Behavioral Health  
Due to the Presence of COVID-19  
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, 
D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, and 
D 2021 048, ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile 
justice, regional centers, and behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 

Executive Order D 2021 070 
 
 
March 26, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  
 
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities.  I amended and extended that Executive Order in Executive 
Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, 
D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, and D 2021 048.   
 
This Executive Order extends the prior Executive Orders for an additional thirty (30) 
days and amends those Executive Orders so that the following provisions expire:  Section II.A, 
concerning the use of seclusion; and Section II.I, concerning detention for individuals who are 
eighteen years of age or older.   
 
II.  
Amendments 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, and D 2021 048, is amended as follows: 
 
 
i. 
Delete Section II.A. 
 
ii. 
Delete Section II.I.   
 
iii. 
Sections II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, and II.H are extended for an additional thirty (30) 
days.   
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 070 
 
 
March 26, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days 
from March 26, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, and D 2021 048, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-sixth day 
of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-071
full_text:
D 2021 071 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 292, D 2021 025, and D 2021 049 Ordering the 
Temporary Suspension of Statute to Allow the Use of Telehealth Services for Veterinarians 
Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 292, D 2021 025, and D 2021 049, ordering the temporary suspension of statute 
to allow the use of telehealth services for veterinarians due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and preserve 
our health care resources. COVID-19 is a highly contagious viral disease that has spread 
throughout many Colorado communities, across the country, and across the world. To protect the 
public health and mitigate exposure to and the spread of COVID-19, Colorado veterinarians must 
be able to use telehealth services whenever possible. These services, including low-cost 
telephone, internet, audio-only, and live video. On December 29, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 292, temporarily suspending statute to allow the use of telehealth services for 
veterinarians due to the presence of COVID-19 in Colorado. On January 27, 2021, I issued 
Executive Order D 2021 025, extending Executive Order D 2020 292. 

Executive Order D 2021 071 
 
 
March 26, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 292, D 2021 025, and D 2021 
049 for an additional thirty (30) days. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 292, as extended by Executive Orders D 2021 025, D 2021 049, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 26, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 292, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2021 025 and D 2021 049, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-sixth day 
of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-072
full_text:
D 2021 072 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, and D 2021 051 Temporarily 
Suspending Certain Deadlines to Allow the Continued Use of Federal Funds from the 
Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, and D 2021 051, temporarily suspending certain deadlines to 
allow the continued use of federal funds from Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security 
Act of 2020, Public Law 116-136 (CARES Act) due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
On March 27, 2020, the federal CARES Act was enacted to provide emergency 
assistance in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CARES Act established the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund, which provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments navigating the 
impact of COVID-19. On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended and 
extended, to direct the expenditure of these funds. In Executive Order D 2020 070, I allocated 
seventy million dollars to the General Fund for eligible expenditures pursuant to the CARES Act 
Coronavirus Relief Fund. The CARES Act originally required these funds only cover expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 until December 30, 2020. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021, which was signed into law on December 28, 2020, extends the time period during which 

Executive Order D 2021 072 
 
 
March 27, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
states can incur expenses covered by the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from December 
30, 2020 to December 31, 2021. A number of Colorado state statutes and enacted legislation 
appropriating CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars conformed to the previous federal 
deadline. These deadlines were imposed by the General Assembly to comply with the original 
CARES Act deadline. This deadline was extended by the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, and D 2021 
051 for an additional thirty (30) days to temporarily suspend certain deadlines to allow the 
continued use of Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars from the CARES Act. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by Executive Order D 2021 027, D 2021 051, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 27, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2021 027 and D 2021 051, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-seventh 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021-073
full_text:
D 2021 073 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 307, D 2021 029, and D 2021 052, Providing 
Protections for Colorado Tenants Due to COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order D 2020 
307, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2021 029 and D 2021 052, providing 
protections to residential and commercial tenants from late fees and notice of the Consolidated 
Appropriations Act, 2021, Section 502.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to help economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
 

Executive Order D 2021 073 
 
 
March 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 307, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2021 029 and D 2021 052, for an additional thirty (30) days, to provide 
protections to residential and commercial tenants from late fees and notice of the Consolidated 
Appropriations Act, 2021, Section 502. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 307, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2021 029, 
D 2021 052, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from March 28, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 307, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2021 029 and D 2021 052, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth 
day of March, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor
