title: D 2021-087
full_text:
D 2021 087 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068 Declaring a 
Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq. (Act), I, Jared 
Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068, declaring a state of disaster emergency 
and providing additional funds for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
  
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
 
I.  
Background and Purpose 
  
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” 
C.R.S. § 24- 33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can 
spread from person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 

Executive Order D 2021 087 
 
 
April 14, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068, 
for an additional thirty (30) days to extend the state of disaster emergency and the deployment of 
the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State and local 
authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State. The amendment 
reallocates $9,600,000 that was originally allocated to CDPHE to the Department of Corrections. 
The source of funding is the Coronavirus Relief Fund (CRF) sub-account of the Disaster 
Emergency Fund (DEF). 
 
II.  
Amendments 
 
Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, 
D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, 
D 2021 061, and D 2021 068, is amended to read as follows:  
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed insufficient 
pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred sixty two million fourteen 
thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars ($162,014,962) was transferred from the 
Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes; five million dollars ($5,000,000) was transferred from the Unclaimed 
Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes; thirty three million dollars ($33,000,000) was transferred from the 
Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund 
for COVID-19 related purposes; and thirty nine million six hundred thirty six thousand 
nine hundred seventy two dollars ($39,636,972) was transferred from the Major Medical 
Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 related purposes.  
 

Executive Order D 2021 087 
 
 
April 14, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that five hundred nine million six hundred 
fifty one thousand nine hundred thirty four dollars ($509,651,934) from the Disaster 
Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of these 
funds, two hundred five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the funds coming from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred seventy four million eight hundred 
forty four thousand seven hundred ninety dollars ($174,844,790) shall be encumbered 
and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19; zero dollars 
($0) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Public Safety for 
response activities related to COVID-19; twenty two million four hundred eight thousand 
six hundred eighty five dollars ($22,408,685) shall be encumbered and made available to 
the Department of Corrections for response activities related to COVID-19; four million 
eight hundred ninety eight thousand two hundred twenty-seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall 
be encumbered and made available to the Department of Labor and Employment for 
response activities related to COVID-19; one million one hundred fifty nine thousand 
eight hundred ninety dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Governor’s Office of Information and Technology for response activities related to 
COVID-19; and one million six hundred eighty eight thousand four hundred eight dollars 
($1,688,408) shall be encumbered and made available to the Department of Agriculture 
for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
In addition, I order that four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred 
fourteen dollars ($4,417,614) from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund be 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund 
shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 31, 2021, 
and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. Funds from 
interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available for expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 31, 2021. All other funds from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 087 
 
 
April 14, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
  
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, 
D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from April 14, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available for the described purposes and the 
described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, and D 2021 068, shall remain in full force 
and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
fourteenth day of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 088
full_text:
D 2021 088 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Providing Assistance for Tenants at Risk for Eviction Due to COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order providing assistance to residential 
tenants at risk for eviction due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the 
President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the 
Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice social 
distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. Keeping people housed can help limit the spread of COVID-19 
during a public health disaster.  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 have been significant and have undermined the 
economic stability of many Coloradans. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 
necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 

Executive Order D 2021 088 
 
 
April 20, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
sectors of the economy have been hit the hardest. The State and federal government have 
provided rental assistance for residential tenants who are in danger of losing their housing due to 
the economic impact of COVID-19. The demand for this State and federal aid has been 
immense, and these programs need time to provide aid to tenants.  
 
This Executive Order provides assistance to Colorado residential tenants at risk for 
eviction while State and federal funds are distributed. We must continue to take steps to provide 
relief to Colorado renters.  
 
II.  
Directives  
 
 
A. 
I temporarily suspend those portions of C.R.S. §§ 38-12-204(1), 38-12-204.3(2), 
and 13-40-104(1)(d) that require landlords to provide all residential tenants with 
ten (10) days of notice of any default for nonpayment of rent, during which time 
the tenant has the opportunity to cure the default. Instead, landlords must provide 
tenants with thirty (30) days’ notice of any default for nonpayment that occurred 
on or after March 10, 2020 before initiating or filing an action for forcible entry 
and detainer. Such 30-day notice may extend beyond the expiration of this 
Executive Order. During this thirty (30) day period, tenants shall have the 
opportunity to cure any default for nonpayment. 
 
B. 
Nothing in this Executive Order shall be construed as relieving an individual from 
their obligation to make rent payments. 
 
III.     Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from April 20, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twentieth day of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 089
full_text:
D 2021 089 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, and 
D 2021 070 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes 
Concerning Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, and Behavioral Health Due to the Presence 
of COVID-19  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, and 
D 2021 070, ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes concerning juvenile 
justice, regional centers, and behavioral health due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 

Executive Order D 2021 089 
 
 
April 24, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  
 
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities. I amended and extended that Executive Order in Executive 
Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, 
D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, and D 2021 070. 
 
This Executive Order extends for an additional thirty (30) days the remaining provisions 
from the prior Executive Orders, Sections II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, and II.H.   
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, D 2021 070, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from April 24, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, and D 2021 070, shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-fourth 
day of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 090
full_text:
D 2021 090 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, D 2021 051, and D 2021 072 
Temporarily Suspending Certain Deadlines to Allow the Continued Use of Federal Funds 
from the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act of 2020 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, D 2021 051, and D 2021 072, temporarily suspending certain 
deadlines to allow the continued use of federal funds from Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and 
Economic Security Act of 2020, Public Law 116-136 (CARES Act) due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
On March 27, 2020, the federal CARES Act was enacted to provide emergency 
assistance in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. The CARES Act established the Coronavirus 
Relief Fund, which provides payments to state, local, and tribal governments navigating the 
impact of COVID-19. On May 18, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 070, as amended and 
extended, to direct the expenditure of these funds. In Executive Order D 2020 070, I allocated 
seventy million dollars to the General Fund for eligible expenditures pursuant to the CARES Act 
Coronavirus Relief Fund. The CARES Act originally required these funds only cover expenses 
incurred from March 1, 2020 until December 30, 2020. The Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021, which was signed into law on December 28, 2020, extends the time period during which 

Executive Order D 2021 090 
 
 
April 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
states can incur expenses covered by the CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund from December 
30, 2020 to December 31, 2021. A number of Colorado state statutes and enacted legislation 
appropriating CARES Act Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars conformed to the previous federal 
deadline. These deadlines were imposed by the General Assembly to comply with the original 
CARES Act deadline. This deadline was extended by the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 
2021.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 294, D 2021 027, D 2021 051, 
and D 2021 072 for an additional thirty (30) days to temporarily suspend certain deadlines to 
allow the continued use of Coronavirus Relief Fund dollars from the CARES Act. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 294, as extended by Executive Order D 2021 027, D 2021 051, 
D 2021 072, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from April 25, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 294, as 
extended by Executive Orders D 2021 027, D 2021 051, and D 2021 072, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-fifth day 
of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 091
full_text:
D 2021 091 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2021 075 Ordering the Department of Revenue to Extend 
the Individual Income Tax Payment Deadline Due to the Presence of COVID-2019 in 
Colorado
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order D 2021 
075 ordering the Department of Revenue to extend the income tax payment deadline for all 
Colorado individual income taxpayers in order to quickly provide relief from payment and 
penalties due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I requested 
that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
On March 11, 2021, President Joseph R. Biden signed into federal law the American 
Rescue Plan Act of 2021 (Act). Among other things, the Act made retroactive changes to federal 
tax laws. On March 17, 2021, the Internal Revenue Service announced that it would extend the 
due date for individuals to file and pay their 2020 income taxes from April 15, 2021 to May 17, 
2021.  

Executive Order D 2021 091 
 
 
April 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2021 075 to temporarily suspend the 
state income tax payment deadlines to quickly provide relief from payment and penalties for all 
Colorado individual income taxpayers.   
 II.        Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire at 12:00 AM on May 18, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. 
 
  
  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth day 
of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 092
full_text:
D 2021 092 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 
160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, 
and D 2021 076 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for Medicaid and 
Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees, Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce, 
and Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for Medicaid Enrollees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, 
D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, and D 2021 076, temporarily 
suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in Medicaid and the 
Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
This Executive Order also incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 
191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 
081, which temporarily suspends certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health 
workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing and 
treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 
009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  

Executive Order D 2021 092 
 
 
April 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 3  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
 
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, 
D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, and D 2021 076, which ensures compliance 
with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending certain statutes to 
allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of 
March 18, 2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, and directs the Executive Director of 
the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to delay the collection of annual 
fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
 
 To consolidate Executive Orders issued due to COVID-19, this Executive Order also 
incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, 
D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 081, which temporarily suspend 
certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for 
COVID-19 testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees.  
 
II. 
Amendments 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, 
D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 
2021 031, D 2021 054, and D 2021 076, is amended to include the following:  
 
D. 
I temporarily suspend the following statutes:  
 
1. The definition of direct and indirect health care services costs in C.R.S. § 
25.5-6-201(15)(a); and 
2. C.R.S. §§ 25.5-4-209(1)(b) and (c) requiring copayments or member cost 
sharing for COVID-19 testing, vaccines, specialized equipment, and therapies 
to comply with the Family First Coronavirus Response Act. 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 092 
 
 
April 28, 2021 
Page 3 of 3  
  
III. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, 
D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 
2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from 
April 28, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive 
Order D 2020 068, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 
2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, and D 
2021 076, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth day 
of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 093
full_text:
D 2021 093 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, 
D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, and D 2021 077 Ordering 
Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim Processing to Provide Relief to Coloradans 
Affected by COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, and D 2021 
077, ordering expedited unemployment insurance (UI) claim processing due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 
076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 
2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068 and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 
sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up economic security, 
employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have seen indications 

Executive Order D 2021 093 
 
 
April 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus continues to threaten 
Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely on unemployment 
insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, 
D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, and D 2021 077, which expedites processing of 
unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will help protect the economic well-being of 
Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, 
D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 
2021 055, D 2021 077, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from April 28, 
2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 
100, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 
2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, and D 2021 077, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth 
day of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 094
full_text:
D 2021 094 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Rescinding Executive Orders D 2020 077, D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, 
D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 081 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes to Increase the Medicaid Home 
Health Workforce and Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for 
Medicaid Enrollees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order rescinding Executive Order 
D 2020 077, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 
165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, 
and D 2021 081, because Executive Order D 2021 092 incorporates all remaining directives of 
Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) test result in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has 
continued to climb, and we have evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally 
declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the 
corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 
176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, 
D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 
2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic.  
On May 22, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 077, temporarily suspending certain 
statutes to increase the Medicaid home health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for COVID-

Executive Order D 2021 094 
 
 
April 28, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
19 testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees. Executive Order D 2020 077 was extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, 
D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, and D 2021 059. On April 6, 2021, I amended and 
extended the series of Executive Orders to extend only sections II.A.4 and II.A.5, continuing the 
temporary suspension of statutory requirements for cost sharing for COVID-19 testing and 
treatment for Medicaid enrollees and the direct and indirect health care services costs. On April 
28, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 092, which incorporated the remaining provisions of 
Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 
2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 
038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 081.  
  
In light of Executive Order D 2021 092, Executive Order D 2020 077 as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, 
D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 081, is no longer 
necessary and is rescinded.  
  
II. 
Directive 
  
I rescind Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 
011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 081. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this twenty-eighth 
day of April, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 095
full_text:
D 2021 095 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092,  
D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, 
D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, and D 2021 079  
Ordering Individuals in Colorado to Wear Non-Medical Face Coverings 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, 
D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, 
D 2021 035, D 2021 056, and D 2021 079, ordering individuals in Colorado to wear a medical or 
non-medical face covering due to the presence of coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
A statewide vaccination effort is now underway, and the general population of the State 
of Colorado became eligible to receive the COVID-19 vaccine as of April 2, 2021. As more and 
more Coloradans are vaccinated, we are further protecting ourselves, our families and friends, 
and our communities from COVID-19. As we take steps to return Coloradans to work and return 

Executive Order D 2021 095 
 
 
May 2, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
to life as normal, we must continue to take measures to facilitate reopening the economy while 
protecting public health by incorporating best practices to protect individuals from infection. 
 
Executive Order D 2021 079 amended, restated, and extended the prior Executive Orders 
relating to face coverings. All of the directives set out in Executive Order D 2021 079 remain in 
effect. This Executive Order extends the prior Executive Orders relating to face coverings to 
protect Coloradans by requiring individuals in Colorado to wear face coverings over their nose 
and mouth in certain locations and situations, and includes an additional amendment that permits 
individuals to remove their medical or non-medical cloth face coverings in Public Indoor Spaces 
if 80% of the individuals in the Public Indoor Space have shown proof of vaccination.  
 
II. 
Amendment 
 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, 
D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, 
D 2021 056, D 2021 079, is amended to include the following:  
 
Q.  
Notwithstanding any provision of this Executive Order, individuals are permitted 
to remove their medical or non-medical cloth face coverings in Public Indoor 
Spaces if 80% of the individuals in the Public Indoor Space have shown proof of 
vaccination.  
 
 
All of the directives in Executive Order D 2021 079 remain in effect. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 095 
 
 
May 2, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, D 2021 079, and 
this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from May 2, 2021, unless extended further by 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, 
D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, 
D 2021 056, and D 2021 079, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Jared Polis 
Governor  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this second 
day of May, 2021.

title: D 2021 096
full_text:
D 2021 096 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, 
D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 
2021 057, and D 2021 080 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes and 
Rules to Expand the Healthcare Workforce for Hospitals and Other Inpatient Treatment 
Facilities Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 
131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, 
D 2021 036, D 2021 057, and D 2021 080 ordering the temporary suspension of certain statutes 
and rules to expand the healthcare workforce for hospitals and other inpatient treatment facilities 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I requested 
that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, 
pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, 
D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 2021 057, and D 2021 080, 

Executive Order D 2021 096 
 
 
May 3, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure that inpatient healthcare facilities have sufficient 
resources and personnel to treat patients suffering from COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
         Executive Order D 2020 038, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, 
D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 
2021 008, D 2021 036, D 2021 057, D 2021 080, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from May 3, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects 
Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 
2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 
002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 2021 057, and D 2021 080, shall remain in full force and effect 
as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this third 
day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 097
full_text:
D 2021 097 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 016, D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112,  
D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, D 2020 278, D 2021 012, 
D 2021 039, D 2021 060, and D 2021 082 Temporarily Suspending Certain Regulatory 
Statutes Concerning Criminal Justice  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 016, as amended and extended by D 2020 043, D2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, 
D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, D 2020 278, D 2021 012, D 2021 039, 
D 2021 060, and D 2021 082, concerning the suspension of certain regulatory statutes 
concerning criminal justice due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in 
Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087.  On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 

Executive Order D 2021 097 
 
 
May 5, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  
 
 
On March 25, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 016, temporarily suspending 
certain regulatory statutes concerning criminal justice to provide the Colorado Department of 
Corrections (DOC) with the discretion necessary to combat the spread of COVID-19 in State 
prisons and protect our correctional officers and staff from infection.  On April 23, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 043, which extended Executive Order D 2020 016 and added provisions 
regarding intensive supervision and community corrections programs. On May 22, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 078, which extended Sections II.A and II.E of Executive Order D 2020 
016 for an additional thirty (30) days, while permitting all other provisions of Executive Orders 
D 2020 016 and D 2020 043 to expire.  
 
On June 20, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 112, which extended Sections II.A 
and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On July 19, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 
140, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On August 17, 
2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 166, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an 
additional thirty (30) days.  On September 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 192, which 
extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On October 14, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 221, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) 
days. On November 12, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 248, which extended Sections 
II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On December 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order 
D 2020 278, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days. On January 
9, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 012, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an 
additional thirty (30) days. On February 7, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 039, which 
extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  On March 8, 2021, I issued 
Executive Order D 2021 060, which extended Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) 
days. On April 6, 2021, I issued Executive Order D 2021 082, which extended Sections II.A and 
II.E for an additional thirty (30) days.  
 
This Executive Order extends Sections II.A and II.E for an additional thirty (30) days, 
allowing DOC to continue to manage its inmate intake process to prevent the introduction of 
inmates who may be symptomatic or asymptomatic with COVID-19 to DOC, in compliance with 
CDPHE and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention guidance.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 097 
 
 
May 5, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 043, 
D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, D 2020 248, 
D 2020 278, D 2021 012, D 2021 039, D 2021 060, D 2021 082, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from May 5, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 016, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 043, D 2020 078, D 2020 112, D 2020 140, D 2020 166, D 2020 192, D 2020 221, 
D 2020 248, D 2020 278, D 2021 012, D 2021 039, D 2021 060, and D 2021 082 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this fifth 
day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 098
full_text:
D 2021 098 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 
111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, 
D 2021 041, D 2021 063, and D 2021 083 Authorizing the Executive Directors of Certain 
State Agencies to Promulgate and Issue Emergency Rules Extending the Expiration Date of 
Licenses and Other Documents Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 015, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, 
D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, D 2021 063, and D 
2021 083, authorizing the executive directors of certain State agencies to promulgate and issue 
emergency rules extending the expiration date of licenses and other documents for an additional 
thirty (30) days due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I.         Background and Purpose  
  
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045 D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I requested that 
the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to 
the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
wide array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our healthcare resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 

Executive Order D 2021 098 
 
 
May 7, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hit hard. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery.  
  
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, 
D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, D 2021 063, and D 
2021 083, continuing authorization for the Executive Director of the Department of Local Affairs 
to promulgate and issue emergency rules extending the expiration date of licenses and other 
documents, while allowing authorization for the remaining departments to expire.  
  
II. 
Amendments 
  
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive 
Orders, D 2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 
224, D 2020 251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, D 2021 063, and D 2021 083, is 
amended as follows:  
1. Delete Section II.B. 
2. Delete Section II.I.  
III. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders, D 2020 
042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 251, 
D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, D 2021 063, D 2021 083, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from May 7, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 015, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 042, D 2020 075, D 2020 111, D 2020 141, D 2020 167, D 2020 195, D 2020 224, D 2020 
251, D 2020 280, D 2021 014, D 2021 041, D 2021 063, and D 2021 083, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this seventh day of 
May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 099
full_text:
D 2021 099 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, and D 2021 084  
Directing the Colorado Division of Insurance to Promulgate a Rule Regarding Rates for 
COVID-19 Vaccine Administration 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, and D 2021 084, directing the Division of 
Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing 
a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance carriers will reimburse providers for the 
cost of COVID-19 vaccine administration.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order 
D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has recently authorized three vaccines for 
prevention of COVID-19, and other COVID-19 vaccines are under development. As COVID-19 
vaccines become more widely available, it is imperative that all Coloradans are able to receive a 
vaccine at no cost.  

Executive Order D 2021 099 
 
 
May 11, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
To facilitate the prompt and effective delivery of the COVID-19 vaccine and mitigate 
financial disincentives of providing the vaccine, providers who administer the COVID-19 
vaccine shall be reasonably compensated by health insurance carriers for the cost of 
administering the vaccine.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, 
and D 2021 084 directing the Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory 
Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance 
carriers will reimburse providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine administration.  Consistent 
with federal law, providers receiving taxpayer dollars for administering the COVID-19 vaccine 
shall not collect payment from anyone receiving the vaccine.  
 
II.     Duration 
Executive Order D 2021 016, as extended by Executive Orders D 2021 042, D 2021 065, 
D 2021 084, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from May 11, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 2021 016, as 
extended by Executive Orders D 2021 042, D 2021 065, and D 2021 084, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this eleventh day of 
May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor
