title: D 2021 101
full_text:
D 2021 101 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, 
D 2021 043, D 2021 066, and D 2021 085 Concerning the Temporary Suspension of Certain 
Requirements Preventing Issuance of Marriage Licenses Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as extended by D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, 
D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, 
D 2021 066, and D 2021 085, concerning the temporary suspension of certain statutes preventing 
the issuance of marriage licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed, due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 

Executive Order D 2021 101 
 
 
May 12, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19.  I 
have extended this Executive Order multiple times due to the continuing impact of COVID-19 in 
Colorado.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, and D 2021 085, for an additional thirty (30) 
days. This extension will enable Coloradans to obtain marriage licenses while observing social 
distancing guidelines and minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
          Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, 
D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, D 2021 085, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from May 12, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, 
D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, 
D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, and D 2021 085, shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this twelfth 
day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 102
full_text:
D 2021 102 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 
2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087 Declaring a 
Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 
045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087 declaring a state of disaster emergency and 
providing additional funds for response activities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
I.
Background and Purpose
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 

Executive Order D 2021 102 
 
 
May 13, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and this Executive Order. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 
2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 
205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087 
for an additional thirty (30) days to extend the state of disaster emergency and the deployment of 
the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State and local 
authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State.  
 
The amendment rescinds an allocation of $77,636,972 from CDPHE and transfers 
$77,636,972 from the Disaster Emergency Fund to the CWCB Construction Fund ($33,000.000), 
the Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund ($5,000,000), and the Major Medical 
Insurance Fund ($39,636,972), which are three cash funds included in the FY 2020-21 State 
Emergency Reserve. The amendment also reallocates $16,902,133 from CDPHE to the 
Department of Corrections (DOC) ($11,702,133), Colorado Department of Human Services 
(CDHS) ($5,000,000), and Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) ($200,000). The 
source of these reallocations is the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster 
Emergency Fund. The amendment also allocates $269,772 to the Colorado Department of 
Personnel and Administration (DPA) from interest accrued to the Coronavirus Relief Fund.  
 
II.  
Amendments 
 
The text of Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 
2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 
061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087 is replaced with the following:  
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed 
insufficient pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred sixty two 
million fourteen thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars ($162,014,962) was 
transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; zero dollars ($0) was transferred from the 

Executive Order D 2021 102 
 
 
May 13, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; zero dollars ($0) was transferred from the 
Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; and zero dollars ($0) was transferred from 
the Major Medical Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes.  
 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that four hundred thirty two million 
fourteen thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars (432,014,962) from the Disaster 
Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of 
these funds, two hundred five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the 
funds coming from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred forty 
five million forty two thousand six hundred fifty seven ($145,042,657) shall be 
encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related to 
COVID-19; zero dollars ($0) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Department of Public Safety for response activities related to COVID-19; thirty 
four million one hundred ten thousand eight hundred eighteen dollars 
($34,110,818) shall be encumbered and made available to the DOC for response 
activities related to COVID-19; four million eight hundred ninety eight thousand 
two hundred twenty-seven dollars ($4,898,227) shall be encumbered and made 
available to the Department of Labor and Employment for response activities 
related to COVID-19; one million one hundred fifty nine thousand eight hundred 
ninety dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
Governor’s Office of Information and Technology for response activities related to 
COVID-19; five million ($5,000,000) shall be encumbered and made available to 
the CDHS for response activities related to COVID-19; two hundred thousand 
($200,000) shall be encumbered and made available to the CDOT for response 
activities related to COVID-19; and one million six hundred eighty eight thousand 
four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408) shall be encumbered and made available to 
the Department of Agriculture for response activities related to COVID-19.  
 
In addition, I order four million six hundred eighty seven thousand three hundred 
eighty six dollars ($4,687,386) from interest that accrued to the Coronavirus Relief 
Fund be encumbered and made available for response activities related to COVID-
19, of which four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred fourteen 
dollars ($4,417,614) shall be encumbered and made available to CDPHE and two 
hundred sixty nine thousand seven hundred seventy two dollars ($269,772) shall be 
encumbered and made available to DPA.  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency 
Fund shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 
31, 2021, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
Funds from interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available 

Executive Order D 2021 102 
 
 
May 13, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
  
for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 31, 2021. All other 
funds from the Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  
 
 
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 
2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 
296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from May 13, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 
2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available for the described purposes and the 
described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 003, 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 
076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, 
D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 
2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, and D 2021 087 shall remain in full force and 
effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
thirteenth day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 103
full_text:
D 2021 103 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Amending, Restating, and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067,  
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, D 2021 079, and 
D 2021 095 Ordering Individuals in Colorado to Wear Face Coverings in Certain Settings 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending, restating, and 
extending Executive Orders D 2020 039, D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, 
D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, 
D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, D 2021 079, and D 2021 095, ordering individuals in 
Colorado to wear a medical or non-medical face covering in certain settings due to the presence 
of coronavirus-2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and 
D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
Coloradans started to access the COVID-19 vaccine on December 14, 2020, and the 
general population of the State of Colorado became eligible to receive the COVID-19 vaccines 
on April 2, 2021. As more and more individuals are vaccinated in Colorado and throughout the 

Executive Order D 2021 103 
 
 
May 14, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
globe, evidence is growing that those who are fully vaccinated can safely resume their normal 
activities without fear of contracting or spreading COVID-19. On May 13, 2021, the Centers for 
Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) outlined new mask guidance for vaccinated and 
unvaccinated individuals. In accordance with this guidance, we must continue to take measures 
to facilitate reopening the economy while protecting public health by incorporating best practices 
to protect individuals from infection. 
 
Executive Order D 2021 079 amended, restated, and extended the prior Executive Orders 
relating to face coverings. Executive Order D 2021 095 amended and extended Executive Order 
D 2021 079. This Executive Order amends, restates, and extends the prior Executive Orders 
relating to face coverings to align guidance in Colorado with the new CDC guidance.  
 
II. 
Directives 
 
A. 
Any individual, age eleven (11) and older, who is not fully vaccinated, is 
encouraged to wear a medical or non-medical cloth face covering that covers the 
nose and mouth when entering or within an indoor space where members of 
different households are present. 
 
B. 
Any fully vaccinated individual may go without any type of face covering in any 
setting, subject to the exceptions in this Executive Order.   
 
C. 
Notwithstanding Sections II.A and II.B above, certain individuals, age eleven (11) 
and older, must wear a medical or non-medical cloth face covering that covers the 
nose and mouth in the following settings: 
 
1. 
Preschool through grade 12 schools (including extracurricular activities) 
and child care centers and services; however, fully vaccinated individuals, 
including vaccinated children ages 16-18, in a classroom, cohort, or other 
group of children may remove masks where the teacher(s), caregiver(s), or 
other staff whose primary responsibility is education or childcare have 
provided proof of fully completed vaccination to their employer;  
 
2. 
Unvaccinated or not fully vaccinated staff of Colorado Division of Motor 
Vehicle offices;  
 
3. 
Unvaccinated or not fully vaccinated residents, staff, and visitors to 
congregate care facilities, including nursing facilities, assisted living 
residences, intermediate care facilities, and group homes; except in 
situations where removal is authorized by the Centers for Medicare & 
Medicaid Services;  
 
4. 
Unvaccinated or not fully vaccinated residents, staff, and visitors to 
Prisons;  
 

Executive Order D 2021 103 
 
 
May 14, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
5. 
Unvaccinated or not fully vaccinated residents, staff, and visitors to Jails;  
 
6. 
Unvaccinated or not fully vaccinated personnel in emergency medical and 
other healthcare settings (including hospitals, ambulance service centers, 
urgent care centers, non-ambulatory surgical structures, clinics, doctors’ 
offices, and non-urgent care medical structures).  
 
D. 
As allowed under state law, owners, operators, and managers of any business or 
service may, at their discretion, continue to require individuals entering or within 
their locations to wear face coverings or show proof of full vaccination. 
 
E. 
For purposes of this Executive Order, an individual is “fully vaccinated” two (2) 
weeks after their second dose in a two-dose series of the COVID-19 vaccine, such 
as the Pfizer or Moderna vaccine, or two (2) weeks after their single-dose vaccine, 
such as Johnson & Johnson’s Janssen vaccine.   
 
F. 
The following individuals are exempt from the requirements of this Executive 
Order:  
 
1.  
Individuals ten (10) years old and younger; and  
 
2.  
Individuals who cannot medically tolerate a face covering. 
 
G. 
Individuals performing the following activities are exempt from the requirements 
of Section II.C of this Executive Order while the activity is being performed: 
 
1. 
Individuals who are hearing impaired or otherwise disabled or who are 
communicating with someone who is hearing impaired or otherwise 
disabled and where the ability to see the mouth is essential to 
communication; 
 
2. 
Individuals who enter a business or receive services and are asked to 
temporarily remove a face covering for identification purposes;  
 
3. 
Individuals who are actively engaged in a public safety role, such as law 
enforcement officers, firefighters, or emergency medical personnel; 
 
4. 
Individuals who are officiating or participating in a life rite or religious 
service where the temporary removal of a face covering is necessary to 
complete or participate in the life rite or religious service.  
 
H. 
These directives shall be applied in a manner consistent with the American with 
Disabilities Act (42 U.S.C. § 12101 et seq.), Title VII of the Civil Rights Act 
(42 U.S.C. § 2000e et seq.), the Colorado Anti-Discrimination Act 
(C.R.S. § 24-34-401 et seq.), and any other relevant federal or State law.  

Executive Order D 2021 103 
 
 
May 14, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
  
 
I. 
Nothing in this Executive Order changes or abrogates the CDC’s Order on 
January 29, 2021, requiring the wearing of masks by travelers to prevent the 
spread of COVID-19. All Coloradans must abide by the CDC’s Order, which can 
be viewed here: https://www.cdc.gov/quarantine/masks/mask-travel-
guidance.html 
 
III.  
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 067, 
D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, D 2020 237, 
D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, D 2021 079, 
D 2021 095, and as amended, restated, and extended by this Executive Order, shall go into effect 
on May 15, 2021 and will expire on June 1, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In 
all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 039, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 067, D 2020 092, D 2020 110, D 2020 138, D 2020 164, D 2020 190, D 2020 219, 
D 2020 237, D 2020 245, D 2020 276, D 2020 281, D 2021 007, D 2021 035, D 2021 056, 
D 2021 079, and D 2021 095, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado, this fourteenth day of 
May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 104
full_text:
D 2021 104 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Colorado Jumpstart 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order directing the Colorado 
Department of Labor and Employment (CDLE) to make direct payments to eligible individuals 
who return to full time employment between May 16, 2021 and June 26, 2021, which will assist 
with the economic recovery from the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic in 
Colorado. These payments are economic support to those suffering from employment or business 
interruptions due to COVID-19-related business closures. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, including combating the economic 
impact of COVID-19 and investing in economic recovery. The economic impacts of COVID-19 
have been significant and have threatened the economic stability of many Coloradans and local 
businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple 
businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the economy were hard hit.  
In March 2021, Colorado’s unemployment rate remained unchanged from February at 
6.4% and the labor market recovery has been slow.  Job openings continue to outpace hiring 
throughout the State. I understand that starting a new job takes resources, like transportation, 

Executive Order D 2021 104 
May 19, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
child care, and clothing, so the payments in this Executive Order will provide Coloradans with 
these resources to return to the workforce more quickly and assist with the statewide economic 
recovery from COVID-19. This Executive Order directs CDLE to make direct payments to 
eligible individuals who return to full time employment between May 16, 2021 and June 26, 
2021, which will assist with the economic recovery from COVID-19.  These payments are 
economic support to those suffering from employment or business interruptions due to COVID-
19-related business closures. 
 
 
II. 
Directives 
 
A. 
I direct the Unemployment Insurance Division within CDLE to make direct 
payments to eligible individuals who return to full time employment between 
May 16, 2021 and June 26, 2021. The payments will be made as follows: 
 
1. Individuals whose first week of full employment is between May 16, 2021 
and May 29, 2021 will be eligible for total compensation up to one thousand 
six hundred dollars ($1600). 
 
2. Individuals whose first full week of employment is May 30, 2021 through 
June 26, 2021 will be eligible for total compensation up to one thousand two 
hundred dollars ($1200). 
 
3. The payments will be split in half, with fifty (50) percent being distributed 
after four (4) weeks of employment, and the remaining fifty (50) percent after 
eight (8) weeks of employment. The total paid to any eligible individual shall 
not exceed the amounts set above. If an individual returns to unemployment at 
any time during the eight (8) week period, they will have forfeited the 
remainder of their payment. 
 
B. 
For purposes of this Executive Order, the term “eligible individual” means any 
individual who received at least one week of unemployment insurance 
compensation during the period of time between March 28, 2021 and May 16, 
2021 and: 
 
1. Was monetarily eligible, by meeting the eligibility requirements in C.R.S. § 8-
73-107(1)(e), for a weekly benefit amount of at least twenty five dollars ($25); 
 
2. Has verified their identity in accordance with Unemployment  Insurance 
Program Letter No. 16-21; 
 
3. Certifies that they have returned to work full time within the eligible dates of 
this program; 
 
4. Resides within the state of Colorado; 

Executive Order D 2021 104 
May 19, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
 
5. Returns to work within the state of Colorado; and  
 
6. Is not receiving unemployment benefits pursuant to C.R.S. § 8-75-201 et seq. 
 
C. 
For purposes of this Executive Order, the term “unemployment insurance 
compensation” means:  
 
1. Unemployment Insurance Compensation administered pursuant to Articles 70 
to 82 of the Colorado Employment Security Act, C.R.S. § 8-70-101, et seq.; 
 
2. Unemployment Compensation for Federal Employees (UCFE), under 5 
U.S.C. § 8501, et seq., and Unemployment Compensation for Ex-Service 
members (UCX), under 5 U.S.C. § 8521, et seq.; or  
 
3. Pandemic Emergency Unemployment Compensation (PEUC), under section 
2107 of the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act, H.R.748, 
116th Cong. (2019-2020) (CARES Act). 
 
D. 
The allocated funds for this program and any costs associated with the 
administration of those payments shall not be billed against the CDLE 
Unemployment Insurance fund or any administrative grant provided through the 
United States Department of Labor (USDOL) for program administration but 
shall instead be solely charged to the funds encumbered by this Executive Order 
and extensions thereof.  
 
E. 
Prior to the commencement of any activity by CDLE related to the distribution of 
the funds transferred, documented approval from the USDOL is required in 
accordance with the requirements of 2 C.F.R. 200.407, Subpart E. Any 
administrative costs borne by CDLE to implement this Executive Order shall be 
proportionately allocated pursuant to 2 C.F.R. Part 200, Subpart E and as 
provided through any relevant USDOL Guidance including but not limited to 
Training and Employment Guidance Letters No. 6-05, 15-09, 6-02 regarding how 
to properly apply cost allocation principles.  
 
F. 
Standard eligibility rules for the receipt of unemployment insurance are not 
applicable to this incentive program and any decision to provide or deny 
payments under the program is not subject to appeal rights. 
 
G. 
I order that five hundred thousand dollars ($500,000) from the Coronavirus Relief 
Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund be reallocated from the 
Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) to the Department of 
Labor and Employment (CDLE) and that it be encumbered and made available to 
CDLE to cover the expenses associated with the first month of implementation of 
Colorado Jumpstart.  

Executive Order D 2021 104 
May 19, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from May 19, 2021, unless amended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.G shall remain available 
for the described purposes until September 4, 2021. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
nineteenth day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 105
full_text:
D 2021 105 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Order D 2021 088 Providing Assistance for Tenants at Risk for 
Eviction Due to COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order D 2021 
088, providing assistance to residential tenants at risk for eviction due to coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087 and D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice social 
distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. Keeping people housed can help limit the spread of COVID-19 
during a public health disaster.  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 have been significant and have undermined the 
economic stability of many Coloradans. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 
necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 

Executive Order D 2021 105 
May 19, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
sectors of the economy have been hit the hardest. The State and federal governments have 
provided rental assistance for residential tenants who are in danger of losing their housing due to 
the economic impact of COVID-19. The demand for this State and federal aid has been 
immense, and these programs need time to provide aid to tenants.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2021 088 for an additional fourteen 
(14) days to provide assistance to Colorado residential tenants at risk for eviction while State and 
federal funds are distributed. We must continue to take steps to provide relief to Colorado 
renters.  
II.     Duration 
Executive Order D 2021 088, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire fourteen 
(14) days from May 19, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2021 088 shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
nineteenth day of May, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 106
full_text:
D 2021 106 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 034, D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048,  
D 2021 070, and D 2021 089 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory 
Statutes Concerning Juvenile Justice, Regional Centers, and Behavioral Health Due to the 
Presence of COVID-19  
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 034, as amended and extended by D 2020 060, D2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, 
D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, 
D 2021 070, and D 2021 089 ordering the temporary suspension of certain regulatory statutes 
concerning juvenile justice, regional centers, and behavioral health due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, 
D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and 
D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to 
our daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between 
people and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 

Executive Order D 2021 106 
May 23, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  
 
 
The potential spread of COVID-19 in State facilities poses a significant threat to 
individuals and staff in those facilities, as well as the communities to which those individuals 
will return. 
 
 
On April 11, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 034, temporarily suspending certain 
regulatory statutes related to the Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS), including 
Division of Youth Services centers, and public and private behavioral health facilities, to enable 
CDHS to respond to the pandemic and undertake efforts to prevent or contain the spread of 
COVID-19 in CDHS facilities. I amended and extended that Executive Order in Executive 
Orders D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, 
D 2020 236, D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, D 2021 070, and D 2021 089. 
 
This Executive Order extends for an additional thirty (30) days the remaining provisions 
from the prior Executive Orders, Sections II.C, II.E, II.F, II.G, and II.H.   
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 060, 
D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, D 2020 266, 
D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, D 2021 070, D 2021 089, and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from May 23, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 034, as amended and extended by Executive Orders 
D 2020 060, D 2020 094, D 2020 126, D 2020 153, D 2020 177, D 2020 206, D 2020 236, 
D 2020 266, D 2020 291, D 2021 024, D 2021 048, D 2021 070, and D 2021 089 shall remain in 
full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-third day of May, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 107
full_text:
D 2021 107 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, 
D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, and 
D 2021 092 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for Medicaid and 
Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees, Increase the Medicaid Home Health Workforce, 
and Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for Medicaid Enrollees 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 
241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, and D 2021 092 
temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in 
Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan.  
 
This Executive Order also incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 
191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, and D 2021 
081, which temporarily suspends certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health 
workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing and 
treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 
009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 
102. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.  
 

Executive Order D 2021 107 
May 27, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
 
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, 
D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031,  D 2021 054, D 2021 076, and D 2021 092 which ensures 
compliance with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily suspending certain 
statutes to allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those individuals enrolled in 
Medicaid as of March 18, 2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, and directs the 
Executive Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) to delay the 
collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic Health Plan. 
 
 To consolidate Executive Orders issued due to COVID-19, this Executive Order also 
incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, 
D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, D 2021 081, and D 2021 094, which 
temporarily suspend certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health workforce and 
eliminate cost sharing for COVID-19 testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees.  
 
 

Executive Order D 2021 107 
May 27, 2021 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, 
D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 
2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 2021 092, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from May 27, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 
2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 
031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, and D 2021 092 shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-seventh day of May, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 108
full_text:
D 2021 108 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, 
D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, and D 2021 093 
Ordering Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim Processing to Provide Relief to 
Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, 
and D 2021 093 ordering expedited unemployment insurance (UI) claim processing due to the 
presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020. On March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated the closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have 
seen indications that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus 

Executive Order D 2021 108 
May 27, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
 
continues to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely 
on unemployment insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, 
D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, and D 2021 093, which expedites processing 
of unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will help protect the economic well-being 
of Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, 
D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 
2021 055, D 2021 077, D 2021 093, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from 
May 27, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, 
and D 2021 093, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-seventh day of May, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

full_text:

EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, 
D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 
2021 057, D 2021 080, and D 2021 096 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain 
Statutes and Rules to Expand the Healthcare Workforce for Hospitals and Other Inpatient

 Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in
