title: D 2021 110
full_text:
D 2021 110 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2021 088  and D 2021 105 Providing Assistance for Tenants 
at Risk for Eviction Due to COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2021 088 and D 2021 105, providing assistance to residential tenants at risk 
for eviction due to coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose  
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087 and D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  
 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It can also be spread 
through contact with contaminated surfaces. Public health experts recommend we practice social 
distancing, or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet or more from other people, as a way 
to slow the spread of COVID-19. Keeping people housed can help limit the spread of COVID-19 
during a public health disaster.  
 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 have been significant and have undermined the 
economic stability of many Coloradans. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 
necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all 

Executive Order D 2021 110 
June 1, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
sectors of the economy have been hit the hardest. The State and federal governments have 
provided rental assistance for residential tenants who are in danger of losing their housing due to 
the economic impact of COVID-19. The demand for this State and federal aid has been 
immense, and these programs need time to provide aid to tenants.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2021 088, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2021 105, for an additional thirty (30) days to provide assistance to Colorado residential 
tenants at risk for eviction while State and federal funds are distributed. We must continue to 
take steps to provide relief to Colorado renters.  
II.     Amendments 
 
Section II of Executive Order D 2021 088, as extended by Executive Order D 2021 105, 
is amended to include the following:  
C. No individual shall file or initiate actions for forcible entry and detainer (i.e. eviction), 
including any demand for rent under C.R.S. § 13-40-101, et seq., unless the individual 
has notified the tenant in writing of the resources available to tenants and landlords. To 
meet this requirement, the individual must provide as notice a copy of the Department of 
Local Affairs (DOLA) resources found at https://drive.google.com/file/d/1e_wzHePhG-
1aONBlvEzMnJFovC4YfsJi/view .  
D. I direct the Executive Director of DOLA to continue working with landlords to 
implement the model rent repayment agreements created by DOLA, which can be found 
at https://drive.google.com/file/d/1mWh7qSpXRMcavBnjnZdQmR9goEcrq6b0/view, to 
assist individuals who are unable to pay rent.  
III.  
Duration 
Executive Order D 2021 088, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2021 105 
and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 1, 2021, unless extended further 
by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2021 088, as extended by Executive 
Order D 2021 105, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this first 
day of June, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 111
full_text:
D 2021 111 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, D 2021 084,  
and D 2021 099 Directing the Colorado Division of Insurance to Promulgate a Rule 
Regarding Rates for COVID-19 Vaccine Administration 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, D 2021 084, and D 2021 099, directing the 
Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule 
establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which health insurance carriers will reimburse 
providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine administration.  
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 
2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 
258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, 
D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
 
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has recently authorized three vaccines for 
prevention of COVID-19, and other COVID-19 vaccines are under development. As COVID-19 
vaccines become more widely available, it is imperative that all Coloradans are able to receive a 
vaccine at no cost.  
 

Executive Order D 2021 111 
June 9, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
To facilitate the prompt and effective delivery of the COVID-19 vaccine and mitigate 
financial disincentives of providing the vaccine, providers who administer the COVID-19 
vaccine shall be reasonably compensated by health insurance carriers for the cost of 
administering the vaccine.  
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2021 016, D 2021 042, D 2021 065, D 
2021 084, and D 2021 099, directing the Division of Insurance in the Colorado Department of 
Regulatory Agencies to promulgate a rule establishing a reasonable rate (or rates) by which 
health insurance carriers will reimburse providers for the cost of COVID-19 vaccine 
administration.  Consistent with federal law, providers receiving taxpayer dollars for 
administering the COVID-19 vaccine shall not collect payment from anyone receiving the 
vaccine.  
 
II.     Duration 
Executive Order D 2021 016, as extended by Executive Orders D 2021 042, D 2021 065, 
D 2021 084, D 2021 099, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 9, 
2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects Executive Order 2021 
016, as extended by Executive Orders D 2021 042, D 2021 065, D 2021 084, and D 2021 099, 
shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this ninth 
day of June, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 112
full_text:
D 2021 112 
 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, 
D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283,  
D 2021 018, D 2021 044, D 2021 067, D 2021 086, and D 2021 100 Concerning the 
Temporary Suspension of Certain Regulatory Statutes Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 011, D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 
147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, D 2021 044, 
D 2021 067, D 2021 086, and D 2021 100, concerning the temporary suspension of certain 
regulatory statutes due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 
102.  On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.  
 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people and 
through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health experts 
recommend the practice of “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) feet 
or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19.  

Executive Order D 2021 112 
June 10, 2020 
Page 2 of 2 
  
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 has necessitated closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 011 for an additional thirty (30) 
days, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 
2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 
283, D 2021 018, D 2021 044, D 2021 067, D 2021 086, and D 2021 100.   
 
II. 
Duration 
 
 
Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, 
D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 
2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, D 2021 044, D 2021 067, D 2021 086, D 2021 
100, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 10, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 011, as amended and 
extended by Executive Orders D 2020 029, D 2020 052, D 2020 084, D 2020 093, D 2020 118, 
D 2020 147, D 2020 173, D 2020 201, D 2020 229, D 2020 257, D 2020 283, D 2021 018, D 
2021 044, D 2021 067, D 2021 086, and D 2021 100 shall remain in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this tenth 
day of June, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 113
full_text:
D 2021 113
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, 
D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 
2021 043, D 2021 066, D 2021 085, and D 2021 101 Concerning the Temporary Suspension 
of Certain Requirements Preventing Issuance of Marriage Licenses Due to the Presence of 
COVID-19 in Colorado 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 014, as extended by D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 
2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 
066, D 2021 085, and D 2021 101, concerning the temporary suspension of certain statutes 
preventing the issuance of marriage licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed, 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068,  D 2021 087, and D 2021 102. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
We must take action to minimize the duration of this epidemic and the disruption to our 
daily lives. The virus that causes COVID-19 is spread primarily by close contact between people 
and through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Public health 
experts recommend we practice “social distancing,” or maintaining a physical distance of six (6) 
feet or more from other people, as a way to slow the spread of COVID-19. 

Executive Order D 2021 113 
June 10, 2021 
Page 2 of 2  
On March 26, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 014 ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes preventing county clerk and recorder offices from issuing marriage 
licenses when county clerk and recorder offices are closed to the public due to COVID-19.  I 
have extended this Executive Order multiple times due to the continuing impact of COVID-19 in 
Colorado.  
This Executive Order extends Executive Orders D 2020 014, D 2020 028, D 2020 048, D 
2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 2020 
282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, D 2021 085, and D 2021 101 for an additional seven 
(7) days. This extension will enable Coloradans to obtain marriage licenses while observing
social distancing guidelines and minimizing the risk of spreading COVID-19.
II.
Duration
 Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 028, D 2020 048, 
D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 228, D 2020 256, D 
2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, D 2021 085, D 2021 101 and this Executive 
Order, shall expire seven (7) days from June 10, 2021, unless extended further by Executive 
Order. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2020 014, as extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 028, D 2020 048, D 2020 083, D 2020 117, D 2020 146, D 2020 172, D 2020 200, D 2020 
228, D 2020 256, D 2020 282, D 2021 017, D 2021 043, D 2021 066, D 2021 085, and D 2021 
101, shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this tenth 
day of June, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 114
full_text:
D 2021 114 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Extending Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 
058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, 
D 2020 258, D2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 
2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Presence of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in 
Colorado 
  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701 et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending and extending 
Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, 
D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 
2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 
045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102 declaring a state of disaster 
emergency and providing additional funds for response activities due to the presence of 
coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
  
Further, pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
I hereby authorize the continued employment of the Colorado National Guard to support and 
provide planning resources to State and local authorities as they respond to the presence of 
COVID-19 in the State.  
 
I.  
Background and Purpose 
  
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Act defines a disaster as “the occurrence or imminent 
threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or property resulting from any 
natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to . . . epidemic.” C.R.S. § 24- 
33.5-703(3). The threat currently posed by COVID-19, a respiratory illness that can spread from 
person to person, constitutes a disaster for purposes of the Act. 
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 

Executive Order D 2021 114 
June 11, 2021 
Page 2 of 4 
 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, and this Executive Order. 
On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
 
My administration, along with other State, tribal, local, and federal authorities, has taken 
a wide array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and 
protect against overwhelming our health care resources. The actions we have undertaken to date 
have made progress in these areas, but we must continue taking aggressive action to minimize 
the duration of this epidemic and of the disruption to our daily lives. 
 
This Executive Order amends and extends Executive Orders D 2020 003, D 2020 018, D 
2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 
205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, 
D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 
2021 102 for an additional thirty (30) days to extend the state of disaster emergency and the 
deployment of the Colorado National Guard to support and provide planning resources to State 
and local authorities as they respond to the presence of COVID-19 in the State.  
 
The amendment rescinds an allocation of $5,600,000 from CDPHE and reallocates 
$1,250,000 to the Department of Higher Education (CDHE) for response activities related to 
COVID-19 and $4,350,000 to the Department of Labor and Employment (CDLE) for response 
activities related to COVID-19. The source of these funds is the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-
account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. The allocation to the Department of Higher Education 
will support the Colorado Comeback Cash Scholarship program. The allocation to the 
Department of Labor and Employment will support the Colorado Jumpstart program. 
 
II.  
Amendments 
 
The text of Section II.C. of Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, 
D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 
2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 
061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102 is replaced with the following:  
 
On April 8, 2020, the funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund were deemed 
insufficient pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), and one hundred sixty two 
million fourteen thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars ($162,014,962) was 
transferred from the Controlled Maintenance Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; zero dollars ($0) was transferred from the 
Unclaimed Property Tourism Promotion Trust Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; zero dollars ($0) was transferred from the 

Executive Order D 2021 114 
June 11, 2021 
Page 3 of 4 
 
Colorado Water Conservation Board Construction Fund to the Disaster Emergency 
Fund for COVID-19 related purposes; and zero dollars ($0) was transferred from 
the Major Medical Insurance Fund to the Disaster Emergency Fund for COVID-19 
related purposes.  
 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4), I order that four hundred thirty two million 
fourteen thousand nine hundred sixty two dollars ($432,014,962) from the Disaster 
Emergency Fund be encumbered for response activities related to COVID-19. Of 
these funds, two hundred five million dollars ($205,000,000) shall come from the 
Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency Fund. Of the 
funds coming from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account, one hundred fifty two 
million three hundred forty two thousand six hundred fifty seven ($152,342,657) 
shall be encumbered and made available to CDPHE for response activities related 
to COVID-19; zero dollars ($0) shall be encumbered and made available to the 
CDPS for response activities related to COVID-19; thirty four million one hundred 
ten thousand eight hundred eighteen dollars ($34,110,818) shall be encumbered and 
made available to the DOC for response activities related to COVID-19; nine 
million two hundred forty eight thousand two hundred twenty seven dollars 
($9,248,227) shall be encumbered and made available to the CDLE for response 
activities related to COVID-19; one million one hundred fifty nine thousand eight 
hundred ninety dollars ($1,159,890) shall be encumbered and made available to 
OIT for response activities related to COVID-19; five million ($5,000,000) shall be 
encumbered and made available to the CDHS for response activities related to 
COVID-19; two hundred thousand ($200,000) shall be encumbered and made 
available to the CDOT for response activities related to COVID-19; one million six 
hundred eighty eight thousand four hundred eight dollars ($1,688,408) shall be 
encumbered and made available to the CDA for response activities related to 
COVID-19; and one million two hundred fifty thousand dollars ($1,250,000) shall 
be encumbered and made available to the CDHE for response activities related to 
COVID-19.  
 
In addition, I order four million six hundred eighty seven thousand three hundred 
eighty six dollars ($4,687,386) from interest that accrued to the Coronavirus Relief 
Fund be encumbered and made available for response activities related to COVID-
19, of which four million four hundred seventeen thousand six hundred fourteen 
dollars ($4,417,614) shall be encumbered and made available to CDPHE and two 
hundred sixty nine thousand seven hundred seventy two dollars ($269,772) shall be 
encumbered and made available to DPA.  
 
Funds from the Coronavirus Relief Fund sub-account of the Disaster Emergency 
Fund shall remain available for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 to December 
31, 2021, and any unexpended funds shall remain in the Disaster Emergency Fund. 
Funds from interest earned on the Coronavirus Relief Fund shall remain available 
for expenses incurred from March 1, 2020 through December 31, 2021. All other 
funds from the Disaster Emergency Fund shall be available until expended.  
 

Executive Order D 2021 114 
June 11, 2021 
Page 4 of 4 
  
III.  
Duration 
Executive Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, 
D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 
2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 
296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, 
D 2021 102, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) days from June 11, 2021, unless 
extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended, shall remain available for the described purposes 
and the described time period stated in Section II.C. In all other respects, Executive Order D 
2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, 
D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 
2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 
022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, and D 2021 102 shall 
remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated.  
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
eleventh day of June, 2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 115
full_text:
D 2021 115
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Order D 2021 104 
Colorado Jumpstart 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2021 104, directing the Colorado Department of Labor and Employment (CDLE) to make 
direct payments to eligible individuals who return to full time employment between May 16, 
2021 and June 26, 2021 to assist with the economic recovery from the coronavirus disease 2019 
(COVID-19) pandemic in Colorado. These payments are economic support to those suffering 
from employment or business interruptions due to COVID-19-related business closures. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 
2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 
234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, 
D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, and D 
2021 114. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major 
Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that 
request on March 28, 2020.  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, including combating the economic 
impact of COVID-19 and investing in economic recovery. The economic impacts of COVID-19 
have been significant and threatened the economic stability of many Coloradans and local 
businesses. The risk of contamination posed by COVID-19 necessitated closure of multiple 
businesses. Employers and employees in virtually all sectors of the economy were hard hit.  
In March 2021, Colorado’s unemployment rate remained unchanged from February at 
6.4% and the labor market recovery has been slow.  Job openings continue to outpace hirings 

Executive Order D 2020 120 
June 27, 2020 
Page 2 of 3 
  
throughout the State. I understand that starting a new job takes resources, like transportation, 
child care, and clothing, so the payments in this Executive Order will provide Coloradans with 
these resources to return to the workforce more quickly and assist with the statewide economic 
recovery from COVID-19. This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2021 104 for an 
additional thirty (30) days, directing CDLE to make direct payments to eligible individuals who 
return to full time employment between May 16, 2021 and June 26, 2021, which will assist with 
the economic recovery from COVID-19.  These payments are economic support to those 
suffering from employment or business interruptions due to COVID-19-related business 
closures. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2021 104, as extended by this Executive Order, shall expire thirty (30) 
days from June 17, 2021, unless amended further by Executive Order, except that the funds 
described in Section II.G shall remain available for the described purposes until September 4, 
2021. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2021 104 remains in full force and effect as 
originally promulgated.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
seventeenth day of June, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 116
full_text:
\ 
D 2021 116
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Ordering the Temporary Suspension of Certain Statutes and Directing the Department of 
Health Care Policy and Financing to Provide Additional Funds to Nursing Homes Due to 
the Presence of COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order temporarily suspending 
certain statutes and directing the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Financing 
(HCPF) to provide additional funds to nursing homes due to the presence of coronavirus disease 
2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 
003, as amended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, 
D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, and D 2021 114. On March 25, 2020, I 
requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. Approximately forty percent of the deaths from COVID-19 in 
Colorado have occurred in nursing homes and other congregate care facilities that serve older 
Coloradans and people living with disabilities. On April 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 
2020 054 ordering the suspension of certain statutes and directed HCPF to provide additional 
funds to Nursing Homes and other congregate care facilities. Executive Order D 2020 054, as 
extended by Executive Order D 2020 086, expired on June 27, 2020. On January 8, 2021, I 
issued Executive Order D 2021 010, which similarly ordered the suspension of certain statutes 
and directed HCPF to provide additional funds to Nursing Homes. Executive Order D 2021 010, 
as extended by Executive Orders D 2021 037 and D 2021 058, expired on March 31, 2021. 

Executive Order D 2021 116 
June 22, 2021 
Page 2 of 3 
 
  
 
This Executive Order directs HCPF to provide an increase analogous to approximately 
ten (10) percent of the per diem Medicaid Member rate to Nursing Facilities. This Executive 
Order ensures that Coloradans have access to a safe environment and adequate health care 
facilities and that facilities serving Coloradans who are aging and/or have disabilities have the 
resources necessary to protect their residents from contracting COVID-19.  
 
II. 
Directives 
 
A. I temporarily suspend the requirements of C.R.S. § 25.5-6-202(5) that establish the 
mechanism for determining the amount of supplemental payments for Medicaid-certified 
nursing facilities. 
 
B. I authorize HCPF to issue State-only payments while obtaining Federal approval from the 
Center for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). Federal payments must be used to 
offset this expense once available.  
 
C. I direct HCPF to promulgate and issue temporary emergency rules through the Medical 
Services Board, subject to federal upper payment limits, to provide supplemental 
payments by an amount totaling an increase of approximately ten (10) percent of the per-
diem rate from HCPF to Medicaid-certified nursing facilities, as defined in C.R.S. § 25.5-
6-202 to protect such facilities’ residents from COVID-19. HCPF shall make these 
increased payments through June 30, 2021. HCPF must direct the funds to be used by the 
facilities to provide services that protect health and result in better care, higher quality of 
life for their residents, and support for Direct Care Workers during the COVID-19 
emergency. 
 
III.     Duration 
 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from June 22, 2021, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-second day of June, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 117
full_text:
D 2021 117
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, 
D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 
2021 092, and D 2021 107 Temporarily Suspending Statutes to Maintain Eligibility for 
Medicaid and Children’s Basic Health Plan Enrollees, Increase the Medicaid Home Health 
Workforce, and Eliminate Cost Sharing for COVID-19 Testing and Treatment for 
Medicaid Enrollees 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive 
Orders D 2020 068, D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 
241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 2021 092, and D 2021 
107 temporarily suspending certain statutes to maintain eligibility for Coloradans enrolled in 
Medicaid and the Children’s Basic Health Plan.  
This Executive Order also incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077 
as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 
191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, D 2021 081, 
and D 2021 094 which temporarily suspends certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home 
health workforce and eliminate cost sharing for coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) testing 
and treatment for Medicaid enrollees. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of widespread community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency on March 10, 2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive 
Order D 2020 003, as amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 2020 018, D 2020 032, 
D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 
2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 
009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, 
and D 2021 114. On March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a 
Major Disaster for the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved 
that request on March 28, 2020.  

Executive Order D 2020 117 
June 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has undertaken a 
broad array of actions to respond to and mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further 
spread, preserve our health care resources, and provide needed flexibility to address the collateral 
consequences of the pandemic. 
 
 
The Families First Coronavirus Response Act offers states participating in the Medicaid 
program a 6.2% increase in their Federal Medicaid Assistance Percentage (FMAP) if they 
maintain eligibility and benefit levels for Medicaid enrollees on and after March 18, 2020. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, 
D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 2021 031,  D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 2021 092, and D 2021 107, 
which ensures compliance with the Families First Coronavirus Response Act by temporarily 
suspending certain statutes to allow for continued eligibility or benefits status for those 
individuals enrolled in Medicaid as of March 18, 2020, and to prevent any changes in eligibility, 
and directs the Executive Director of the Department of Health Care Policy and Financing 
(HCPF) to delay the collection of annual fees for those enrolled in Colorado’s Children’s Basic 
Health Plan. 
 
 To consolidate Executive Orders issued due to COVID-19, this Executive Order also 
incorporates the directives from Executive Order D 2020 077, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 108, D 2020 139, D 2020 165, D 2020 191, D 2020 220, D 2020 247, 
D 2020 277, D 2021 011, D 2021 038, D 2021 059, D 2021 081, and D 2021 094, which 
temporarily suspend certain statutes to increase the Medicaid home health workforce and 
eliminate cost sharing for COVID-19 testing and treatment for Medicaid enrollees.  
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 104, 
D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 003, D 
2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 2021 092, D 2021 107,  and this Executive Order, shall 
expire thirty (30) days from June 25, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all 
other respects, Executive Order D 2020 068, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2020 104, D 2020 136, D 2020 160, D 2020 183, D 2020 214, D 2020 241, D 2020 272, D 2021 
003, D 2021 031, D 2021 054, D 2021 076, D 2021 092, and D 2021 107 shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-fifth day of June, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 118
full_text:
D 2021 118
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 100, D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, 
D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077,  D 2021 093, and 
D 2021 108 Ordering Expedited Unemployment Insurance Claim Processing to Provide 
Relief to Coloradans Affected by COVID-19 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Office of the Governor of the State of Colorado 
and, in particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 
184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, 
D 2021 093, and D 2021 108 ordering expedited unemployment insurance (UI) claim processing 
due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) in Colorado. 
I.
Background and Purpose
On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and we have 
evidence of community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on 
March 10, 2020. On March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended or extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, D 
2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 2020 
264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 028, 
D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, and D 2021 114. On March 25, 
2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for the State of 
Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources. 
The economic impacts of COVID-19 are significant, and threaten to undermine the 
economic stability of many Coloradans and local businesses. The risk of contamination posed by 
COVID-19 necessitated the closure of multiple businesses. Employers and employees in 
virtually all sectors of the economy have been hard hit. We must take action to shore up 
economic security, employment, community cohesion, and community recovery. While we have 

Executive Order D 2021 118 
June 25, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
seen indications that our efforts to “flatten the curve” are working, transmission of the virus 
continues to threaten Coloradans’ way of life and livelihoods. Many Coloradans continue to rely 
on unemployment insurance to meet their basic needs. 
 
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 133, D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, 
D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, D 2021 077, D 2021 093, and D 2021 108, which 
expedites processing of unemployment insurance claims for Coloradans and will help protect the 
economic well-being of Colorado’s communities impacted by COVID-19. 
 
II. 
Duration 
 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 133, 
D 2020 161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 
2021 055, D 2021 077, D 2021 093, D 2021 108, and this Executive Order, shall expire thirty 
(30) days from June 25, 2021, unless extended further by Executive Order. In all other respects, 
Executive Order D 2020 100, as amended and extended by Executive Order D 2020 133, D 2020 
161, D 2020 184, D 2020 215, D 2020 242, D 2020 273, D 2021 004, D 2021 032, D 2021 055, 
D 2021 077, D 2021 093, and D 2021 108, shall remain in full force and effect as originally 
promulgated. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
  
GIVEN under my hand and 
the Executive Seal of the 
State of Colorado, this 
twenty-fifth day of June, 
2021. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2021 119
full_text:
D 2021 119 
  
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
 
Extending Executive Orders D 2020 038, D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, 
D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 
2021 057, D 2021 080, D 2021 096, and D 2021 109 Ordering the Temporary Suspension of 
Certain Statutes and Rules to Expand the Healthcare Workforce for Hospitals and Other 
Inpatient Treatment Facilities Due to the Presence of COVID-19 
         Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order extending Executive Order 
D 2020 038, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 
131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, 
D 2021 036, D 2021 057, D 2021 080, D 2021 096, and D 2021 109, ordering the temporary 
suspension of certain statutes and rules to expand the healthcare workforce for hospitals and 
other inpatient treatment facilities due to the presence of coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) 
in Colorado. 
I.       Background and Purpose 
         On March 5, 2020, the Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment’s 
(CDPHE) public health laboratory confirmed the first presumptive positive COVID-19 test result 
in Colorado. Since then, the number of confirmed cases has continued to climb, and there is 
community spread throughout the State. I verbally declared a disaster emergency on March 10, 
2020, and on March 11, 2020, I issued the corresponding Executive Order D 2020 003, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2020 018, D 2020 032, D 2020 058, D 2020 076, 
D 2020 109, D 2020 125, D 2020 152, D 2020 176, D 2020 205, D 2020 234, D 2020 258, D 
2020 264, D 2020 268, D 2020 284, D 2020 290, D 2020 296, D 2021 009, D 2021 022, D 2021 
028, D 2021 045, D 2021 061, D 2021 068, D 2021 087, D 2021 102, and D 2021 114. On 
March 25, 2020, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster for 
the State of Colorado, pursuant to the Stafford Act. The President approved that request on 
March 28, 2020. 
My administration, along with other State, local, and federal authorities, has taken a wide 
array of actions to mitigate the effects of the pandemic, prevent further spread, and protect 
against overwhelming our health care resources.  

Executive Order D 2021 119 
June 30, 2021 
Page 2 of 2 
  
This Executive Order extends Executive Order D 2020 038, as amended and extended by 
Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, 
D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D 2021 008, D 2021 036, D 2021 057, D 2021 080, D 
2021 096, and D 2021 109, for an additional thirty (30) days to ensure that inpatient healthcare 
facilities have sufficient resources and personnel to treat patients suffering from COVID-19. 
II.     Duration 
 
         Executive Order D 2020 038, as extended by Executive Orders D 2020 063, D 2020 097, 
D 2020 131, D 2020 158, D 2020 182, D 2020 212, D 2020 240, D 2020 271, D 2021 002, D
