D 2024 016 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Pearl Fire in Larimer County and  
Authorizing Employment of the Colorado National Guard 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the Pearl Fire in Larimer County, enabling State and Federal agencies to 
coordinate fire suppression response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts. 
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-
104, I authorize employing unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard for the purpose of 
assisting with response and recovery efforts as deemed necessary by the Colorado Department of 
Public Safety. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On September 16, 2024, the Pearl Fire started in Larimer County. The fire was burning at 
approximately 9,400 feet of elevation on local terrain, exceeding local capacity to manage the 
fire. Mandatory evacuations were ordered for Crystal Lakes, south of Black Mountain and north 
of Erie Lake. Voluntary evacuations were ordered for Crystal Lakes, north of Black Mountain to 
the state line. There were properties at risk, including 11 homes within a one mile radius of the 
fire and 148 homes within a three mile radius of the fire.  
On September 16, 2024, at approximately 4:25 PM, I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency due to the Pearl Fire in Larimer County and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. At that time, I also verbally authorized employing unarmed 
members of the Colorado National Guard for the purpose of assisting with response and recovery 
efforts as deemed necessary by the Colorado Department of Public Safety.  
 

Executive Order D 2024 016 
October 15, 2024 
Page 2 of 3 
 
            As of September 30, 2024, the fire was 95% contained and burned 128 acres. No 
structures were destroyed during the fire. 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Pearl Fire and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency under 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of September 16, 2024, declaring a 
disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order, and shall 
have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive 
Order. 
B. 
Further, pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, my verbal order of September 16, 2024, 
authorizing employing unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard for the 
purpose of responding to the Pearl Fire, is hereby memorialized by this Executive 
Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within 
this Executive Order. 
C. 
My verbal order of September 16, 2024, activated the State Emergency 
Operations Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to take 
whatever actions may be required and requested by the Director or Acting 
Director of the Office of Emergency Management pursuant to the State 
Emergency Operations Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and 
equipment, as necessary. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts. Therefore, I order that five hundred 
sixty-six thousand dollars ($566,000) be transferred from the State Emergency 
Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber five hundred sixty-six 
thousand dollars ($566,000) in the DEF to pay for fire suppression, response, 
consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Pearl 
Fire in Larimer County. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the 
appropriate government agencies and nonprofit organizations and execute awards, 
purchase orders or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds 
described in Section II(D) above shall remain available for this purpose until 
seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive Order, and any 
unexpended funds shall remain in the DEF. 
 

Executive Order D 2024 016 
October 15, 2024 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from September 16, 2024, unless 
extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall 
remain available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this 
Executive Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the

Colorado this fifteenth  
day of October, 2024. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2024 017
full_text:
Executive Order D 2024 017 
October 31, 2024 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2024 017 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to Hurricane Milton and Authorizing  
Employment of the Colorado National Guard 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to Hurricane Milton, enabling State and Federal agencies to coordinate hurricane 
response support, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida.  
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-
104, I authorize employing unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard for the purpose of 
assisting with response and recovery efforts in Florida as deemed necessary by the Colorado 
Department of Public Safety as a result of Hurricane Milton. 
 
Background and Purpose 
As a party to the Emergency Management Assistance Compact (Compact), C.R.S. § 24-
60-2901, et seq., the State of Colorado is able to provide assistance to states enduring a disaster 
emergency. The State of Florida is also a party to the Compact. The purpose of the Compact “is 
to provide for mutual assistance among the states entering into [the Compact] in managing any 
emergency disaster that is duly declared by the Governor of the affected state,” arising from a 
natural disaster, among other emergencies. C.R.S. § 24-60-2902. 
On October 7, 2024, national weather outlets predicted a large, once in a generation 
storm, Hurricane Milton, to make landfall in Florida and other Gulf states in the following days. 
Hurricane Milton made landfall in Florida on October 9, 2024, as a category 3 storm and 
triggered tornadoes across the state.  On October 10, 2024, as Hurricane Milton moved away 
from Florida, millions were left without power as streets were flooded and homes were damaged.  
At least 11 individuals died as a result of the storm.  
On October 7, 2024, at approximately 7:10pm, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
in preparation for Hurricane Milton and activated the State of Colorado Emergency Operations 
Plan. At that time, I also verbally authorized employing unarmed members of the Colorado 
National Guard for the purpose of assisting with response and recovery efforts as deemed 
necessary by the Colorado Department of Public Safety. The State sent a Chinook Helicopter  

Executive Order D 2024 017 
October 31, 2024 
Page 2 of 3 
 
(1CH47) and its eight crew members to Florida to assist with preparation and relief efforts for 
Hurricane Milton.  Colorado also sent over 200 emergency assistance specialists to Florida, 
including firefighters, paramedics, physicians, structural engineers, hazardous materials 
technicians, heavy rigging specialists, and canine handlers to assist federal emergency response 
teams. 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
Hurricane Milton and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency under 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of October 7, 2024, declaring a 
disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order, and shall 
have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive 
Order. 
B. 
Further, pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, my verbal order of October 7, 2024, 
authorizing employing unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard for the 
purpose of responding to Hurricane Milton, is hereby memorialized by this 
Executive Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were 
contained within this Executive Order. 
C. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701(1)(f), as acting Governor of the State of 
Colorado, I hereby authorize personnel from Colorado to assist State and Federal 
agencies to coordinate hurricane response support, consequence management, 
mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-60-2901, et seq., I hereby implement the Emergency 
Management Assistance Compact.  The State of Colorado shall provide personnel 
to assist State and Federal agencies to coordinate hurricane response support, 
consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida. 
E. 
My verbal order of October 7, 2024, activated the State Emergency Operations 
Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to take whatever actions 
may be required and requested by the Director or Acting Director of the Office of 
Emergency Management pursuant to the State Emergency Operations Plan, 
including provision of appropriate staff and equipment, as necessary to assist 
State and Federal agencies to coordinate hurricane response support, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida. 
F. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for hurricane response support, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida. Therefore, I order that  

Executive Order D 2024 017 
October 31, 2024 
Page 3 of 3 
 
two hundred eighty-five thousand dollars ($285,000) be transferred from the State 
Emergency Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber two hundred 
eighty-five thousand dollars ($285,000) in the DEF to enable State and Federal 
agencies to coordinate hurricane response support, consequence management, 
mitigation, and recovery efforts in Florida. 
G. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(6), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the 
appropriate government agencies and nonprofit organizations and execute awards, 
purchase orders or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds 
described in Section II(F) above shall remain available for this purpose until 
seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive Order, and any 
unexpended funds shall remain in the DEF. 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from October 7, 2024, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(F) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive 
Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this thirty-first  
day of October, 2024. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2024 018
full_text:
Executive Order D 2024 018 
December 6, 2024 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2024 018 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Extreme Winter Storm Throughout  
Colorado and Authorizing Deployment of the Colorado National Guard  
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to extreme winter storm throughout Colorado, enabling State agencies to 
coordinate response, consequence management, hazard mitigation, and recovery efforts.  
Further, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and pursuant to 
C.R.S. § 28-3-104, I hereby authorize deploying the Colorado National Guard in an unarmed 
capacity for the purpose of responding to and providing support during the extreme winter storm 
throughout Colorado. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On November 6, 2024, portions of Colorado, including Las Animas, Elbert, Huerfano, El 
Paso, Otero, and Lincoln Counties, began receiving unprecedented amounts of snow, which 
accumulated 18-37 inches throughout the day, with an additional 2-7 inches expected overnight. 
The National Weather Service predicted an additional 12-36 inches of snow accumulation to fall 
in the region beginning November 8, 2024 and continuing through November 9, 2024. With 
these dangerous conditions, children, the elderly, people with disabilities, people with special 
medical needs, and unhoused individuals are especially vulnerable.   
At approximately 12:00 PM on November 6, 2024, Colorado State Patrol (CSP) 
mobilized high clearance vehicles and other resources to rescue and transport stranded motorists 
to warming shelters throughout the southern region of the State. As of 1:30 PM, CSP and 
Division of Fire Prevention and Control (DFPC) personnel executed multiple recovery missions 
for stranded motorists. With the continued snow fall, the Colorado Department of Transportation 
(CDOT) closed Interstate 25 south of Pueblo and Interstate 70 east of Limon, causing travelers to  

Executive Order D 2024 018 
December 6, 2024 
Page 2 of 3 
 
use alternate routes to bypass the closures and navigate unsafe and unplowed state highways and 
county roads. The snowstorm stranded numerous motorists throughout the region and created 
conditions stressing local jurisdictions’ resources.  
On November 7, 2024, at approximately 3:15 PM, I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency for the extreme winter storm impacts throughout Colorado, and activated the State 
Emergency Operations Plan and directed the Office of Emergency Management (OEM) to take 
all necessary and appropriate State actions to assist the affected jurisdictions with their response, 
consequence management, hazard mitigation, and recovery efforts. At the same time, I verbally 
authorized deployment and use of the Colorado National Guard in an unarmed capacity for the 
purpose of response, consequence management, hazard mitigation, and recovery efforts related 
to the extreme winter storm throughout the State.  
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The extreme winter storm and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency 
under C.R.S § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of November 7, 2024, 
declaring a disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order 
and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this 
Executive Order. 
B. 
Further, pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, my verbal order of November 7, 2024, 
authorizing deployment and use of the Colorado National Guard in an unarmed 
capacity for the purpose of response, consequence management, hazard 
mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the extreme winter storm response, is 
hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall have the full force and 
effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive Order. 
C. 
My verbal order of November 7, 2024, activated the State Emergency Operations 
Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to take whatever actions 
may be required and requested by the Director or his delegate of the OEM 
pursuant to the State Emergency Operations Plan, including provision of 
appropriate staff and equipment, as necessary. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for response, consequence management, hazard 
mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the extreme winter storm.  Therefore, I 
order that two hundred forty five thousand dollars ($245,000) be transferred from 
the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber two 
hundred forty five thousand dollars ($245,000) in the DEF to pay for response,  
 

Executive Order D 2024 018 
December 6, 2024 
Page 3 of 3 
 
consequence management, hazard mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the 
extreme winter storm throughout Colorado. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the Office of Emergency 
Management is hereby authorized and directed to allocate the funding to the 
appropriate government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute 
awards, purchase orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of the funds.  
The funds described in Section II(D) above shall remain available for this purpose 
until seventy-two (72) months from the date of this order, and any unexpended 
funds shall remain in the DEF. 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from November 7, 2024, unless 
extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall 
remain available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this 
Executive Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this sixth  
day of December, 2024. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

136 STATE CAPITOL 
DENVER, COLORADO 80203 
JARED POLIS 
GOVERNOR 
TEL 303-866-2471 
FAx 303-866-2003 
D 2024 019 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Rescission of Prior Executive Orders 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby rescind the following 208 Executive Orders that are 
unnecessary, outdated, or obsolete. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
While Colorado Governors use Executive Orders as an essential tool of governance in 
order to carry out their executive functions as mandated by the Colorado Constitution and State 
law, they can accumulate over time and become an operational burden to efficient government. 
Executive Orders can provide timely and necessary direction for and resources to pressing 
matters of statewide concern and advance the implementation of State objectives and policies, 
but can also lead to additional paperwork. 
Over time, Executive Orders may become unnecessary, outdated, or obsolete due to 
changed circumstances, the completion of their intended purposes, or redundancy with current 
laws and regulations. The continued retention of outdated Executive Orders may lead to 
confusion, inefficiency, or administrative burdens. To make state government more efficient, 
the Office of the Governor and Executive Branch agencies reviewed past Executive Orders to 
determine relevance and alignment with the State's current needs and priorities. My 
administration has determined that certain Executive Orders can be rescinded because they are 
unnecessary, outdated, or obsolete. 
II. 
Directive

Orders.

title: D 2024 020
full_text:
Executive Order D 2024 020 
December 17, 2024 
Page 1 of 4 
D 2024 020 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Proclamation 
Declaration of Vote on Measures Initiated or Referred to the 2024 Biennial Regular 
Election Ballot and Approved by a Majority of the Votes Cast 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado, and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this proclamation for measures initiated or 
referred to the 2024 biennial regular election ballot held on November 5, 2024, and approved by 
a majority of the votes cast.   
 
Background and Purpose 
Secretary of State Jena Griswold certified on December 6, 2024, that she had canvassed 
the “Abstract of Votes Cast,” and that the measures in this Proclamation were approved by a 
majority of the votes cast.  
Pursuant to Article V, Section 1(4)(a) of the Colorado Constitution, measures initiated or 
referred to the people of the State shall become law when approved by a majority of the votes 
cast and shall take effect from and after the date of the official declaration of the vote by 
proclamation of the Governor, but not later than thirty days after the vote has been canvassed. 
 
Declarations 
Accordingly, I hereby issue this proclamation declaring the vote of the following 
measures approved by the people on November 5, 2024.  
Amendment G - Modify Property Tax Exemption For Veterans with Disabilities  
Shall there be an amendment to the Colorado constitution concerning the expansion of 
eligibility for the property tax exemption for veterans with a disability to include a 
veteran who does not have a service-connected disability rated as a one hundred percent 
permanent disability but does have individual unemployability status? 
Yes - 2,212,022 (73.13%) 
No - 812,638 (26.87%) 
 

Executive Order D 2024 020 
December 17, 2024 
Page 2 of 4 
 
Amendment H - Judicial Discipline Procedures and Confidentiality 
Shall there be an amendment to the Colorado constitution concerning judicial discipline, 
and, in connection therewith, establishing an independent judicial discipline adjudicative 
board, setting standards for judicial review of a discipline case, and clarifying when 
discipline proceedings become public? 
Yes - 2,150,820 (73.05%) 
No - 793,642 (26.95%) 
 
Amendment I - Constitutional Bail Exception for First Degree Murder 
Shall there be an amendment to the Colorado constitution concerning creating an 
exception to the right to bail for cases of murder in the first degree when proof is evident 
or presumption is great? 
Yes - 2,058,063 (68.34%) 
No - 953,652 (31.66%)  
 
Amendment J - Repealing the Definition of Marriage in the Constitution 
Shall there be an amendment to the Colorado constitution removing the ban on same-sex 
marriages? 
Yes - 1,982,200 (64.33%) 
No - 1,099,228 (35.67%) 
 
Amendment 79 - Constitutional Right to Abortion 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado constitution recognizing the right to abortion, 
and, in connection therewith, prohibiting the state and local governments from denying, 
impeding, or discriminating against the exercise of that right, allowing abortion to be a 
covered service under health insurance plans for Colorado state and local government 
employees and for enrollees in state and local governmental insurance programs? 
Yes - 1,921,593 (61.97%) 
No - 1,179,261 (38.03%) 
 

Executive Order D 2024 020 
December 17, 2024 
Page 3 of 4 
 
Proposition JJ - Retain Additional Sports Betting Tax Revenue 
Without raising taxes, may the state keep and spend all sports betting tax revenue above 
voter-approved limits to fund water conservation and protection projects instead of 
refunding revenue to casinos? 
Yes - 2,340,370 (76.44%) 
No - 721,237 (23.56%) 
 
Proposition KK - Firearms and Ammunition Excise Tax 
Shall state taxes be increased by $39,000,000 annually to fund mental health services, 
including for military veterans and at-risk youth, school safety and gun violence 
prevention, and support services for victims of domestic violence and other violent 
crimes by authorizing a tax on gun dealers, gun manufacturers, and ammunition vendors 
at the rate of 6.5% of the net taxable sales from the retail sale of any gun, gun precursor 
part, or ammunition, with the state keeping and spending all of the new tax revenue as a 
voter-approved revenue change? 
Yes - 1,675,123 (54.37%) 
No - 1,406,112 (45.63%) 
 
Proposition 128 - Parole Eligibility for Crimes of Violence 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes concerning parole eligibility for 
an offender convicted of certain crimes, and, in connection therewith, requiring an 
offender who is convicted of second degree murder; first degree assault; class 2 felony 
kidnapping; sexual assault; first degree arson; first degree burglary; or aggravated 
robbery committed on or after January 1, 2025, to serve 85 percent of the sentence 
imposed before being eligible for parole, and requiring an offender convicted of any such 
crime committed on or after January 1, 2025, who was previously convicted of any two 
crimes of violence, not just those crimes enumerated in this measure, to serve the full 
sentence imposed before beginning to serve parole? 
Yes - 1,869,231 (62.11%) 
No - 1,140,284 (37.89%) 
 
 

Executive Order D 2024 020 
December 17, 2024 
Page 4 of 4 
 
Proposition 129 - Establishing Veterinary Professional Associates 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes creating a new veterinary 
professional associate profession, and, in connection therewith, establishing 
qualifications including a master's degree in veterinary clinical care or the equivalent as 
determined by the state board of veterinary medicine to be a veterinary professional 
associate; requiring registration with the state board; allowing a registered veterinary 
professional associate to practice veterinary medicine under the supervision of a licensed 
veterinarian; and making it a misdemeanor to practice as a veterinary professional 
associate without an active registration? 
Yes - 1,572,545 (52.76%) 
No - 1,407,814 (47.24%) 
 
Proposition 130 - Funding for Law Enforcement 
Shall there be a change to the Colorado Revised Statutes concerning state funding for 
peace officer training and support, and, in connection therewith, directing the legislature 
to appropriate 350 million dollars to the peace officer training and support fund for 
municipal and county law enforcement agencies to hire and retain peace officers; 
allowing the fund to be used for pay, bonuses, initial and continuing education and 
training, and a death benefit for a peace officer, police, fire and first responder killed in 
the line of duty; and requiring the funding to supplement existing appropriations? 
Yes - 1,583,118 (52.79%) 
No - 1,415,528 (47.21%) 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this seventeenth  
day of December, 2024 at 12:01 am. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2025 001
full_text:
Executive Order D 2025 001 
January 24, 2025 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2025 001 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Funding Provision of Executive Orders D 2022 004, D 2022 005, D 2022 012, and 
D 2022 018 Concerning the Disaster Emergency Due to the Marshall Fire in Boulder 
County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order to amend the funding 
provision of Executive Order D 2022 004, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 
2022 005, D 2022 012, and D 2022 018, due to the ongoing need for availability of funds to 
respond to and recover from the Marshall Fire in Boulder County. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
Around 10:30 a.m. on December 30, 2021, a grass fire, paired with straight-line winds in 
excess of one hundred miles per hour, quickly spread to populated areas within Boulder County 
and threatened lives and structures. Also on December 30, 2021, I verbally declared a state of 
disaster emergency due to the Marshall Fire in Boulder County and I activated the State of 
Colorado Emergency Operations Plan.  
Because of the severity of the Marshall Fire and the potential for continued widespread 
damage to private homes, a Fire Management Assistance Grant was approved by the Federal 
Emergency Management Agency’s (FEMA) Regional Administrator on December 30, 2021. On 
December 30, 2021, I requested that the President of the United States declare a Major Disaster 
for the State of Colorado. The President quickly approved the Major Disaster Declaration for 
Colorado on December 31, 2021, to supplement State and local response and recovery efforts in 
the affected area. As part of the President’s Major Disaster Declaration, FEMA’s Public 
Assistance and Individual Assistance programs were approved for Boulder County. 
 

Executive Order D 2025 001 
January 24, 2025 
Page 2 of 3 
 
On January 26, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 004, memorializing my verbal 
order and allocating forty million four hundred thirty-one thousand two hundred sixty-six dollars 
($40,431,266) to pay for fire suppression, response, consequence management, and recovery 
efforts related to the Marshall Fire and to employ two term-limited staff to advise on and 
coordinate wildfire recovery efforts. On January 28, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 005, 
which extended the disaster declaration. On February 25, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 
012, amending Executive Orders D 2022 004 and D 2022 005, to return ten million dollars 
($10,000,000) from the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) to the State Emergency Reserve Cash 
Fund (SERCF). On May 11, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 018, amending the prior 
Executive Orders to unencumber nineteen million one hundred sixty-eight thousand three 
hundred twenty-two dollars ($19,168,322) in the DEF due to the increased Federal government 
share of funds to manage and respond to the Marshall Fire in Boulder County. Pursuant to 
Executive Order D 2022 004, as amended and extended, the disaster expired on March 26, 2022, 
and the funding remains available for the described purposes for 36 months from January 26, 
2022. 
This Executive Order amends the funding provision of Executive Order D 2022 004, as 
amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2022 005, D 2022 012, and D 2022 018, due to 
the ongoing need for availability of funds to respond to and recover from the Marshall Fire in 
Boulder County. The thirty-six (36) month term is extended by another twenty-four (24) months, 
from January 26, 2025 to January 26, 2027, for a total of sixty (60) months, so that encumbered 
funds in the DEF may continue to be used for response and recovery efforts. 
 
Amendment 
Executive Order D 2022 004, as amended and extended by Executive Orders D 2022 005, 
D 2022 012, and D 2022 018, is amended so that the duration clause indicates that the funds 
described in Section II(E) of Executive Order D 2022 004 shall remain available for the 
described purposes for sixty (60) months from the date of Executive Order D 2022 004. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2025 001 
January 24, 2025 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2022 004, as amended 
and extended by Executive Orders D 2022 005, D 2022 012, and D 2022 018, shall remain in full 
force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twenty-fourth  
day of January, 2025. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2025 002
full_text:
Executive Order D 2025 002 
February 7, 2025 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2025 002 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Provisions of Executive Order D 2024 014 Concerning the Disaster Emergency 
Due to Flooding and Debris Flow in Ouray County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order to amend provisions of Executive 
Order D 2024 014 due to additional damage to State infrastructure identified in Montrose County 
by the Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) as a result of the same monsoonal 
weather system, and providing CDOT the ability to seek federal reimbursement for eligible 
expenses. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
Beginning August 5, 2024, rain from a monsoonal weather system caused flash flooding, 
landslides, and debris flows in Ouray County. Numerous roads became damaged and impassable 
and debris flows rerouted Beaver Creek, destroying the Town of Ridgeway’s drinking water 
collection system.  
On August 23, 2024, I verbally declared a disaster emergency due to the Flooding and 
Debris Flow in Ouray County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On September 
20, 2024, I issued Executive Order D 2024 014 memorializing my verbal declaration of a 
disaster emergency.  
On August 12, 2024, monsoonal storms produced a flood surge causing severe damage to 
the abutment embankment of bridge K-01-B, carrying CO 141 (MM 85.52) over the Dolores 
River, located in Montrose County. The precipitation runoff from the storm took place in a 
wildfire affected burn scar watershed which drains into a tributary stream feeding into the 
Dolores River, creating a 25 year flood surge in the location of the bridge. Massive damage took 
place to the south abutment embankment (Abutment 1) leaving approximately 25 feet of exposed 
abutment piling, compromising the approach roadway at the end of the bridge. Upon further  

Executive Order D 2025 002 
February 7, 2025 
Page 2 of 3 
 
inspection, additional damage was discovered to the adjacent pier (Pier 1), a primary 
foundational element required to safely hold up the adjacent spans of the bridge. The localized 
scour had also compromised the soil supporting the footing under Pier 1, leaving the bridge 
susceptible to failure during a future flooding event.  
Temporary repairs have been implemented by placing a layer of large stones to protect 
the soil from erosion (rip rap) around the existing damaged areas. However, CDOT needs to 
complete permanent repairs before the 2025 spring runoff to avoid further closures and 
disruptions to the lives of Coloradans. The permanent repairs require reinforcement with the use 
of micro piles around the foundation at Pier 1 to meet the requirements to resist future flooding 
events. The repairs will additionally include armoring of the embankment and channel around 
Abutment 1.  
CO 141 is a critical route connecting the rural communities of Norwood, Nucla, and 
Naturita to the City of Grand Junction for critical agricultural supplies and medical needs. If this 
bridge is closed, the detour route is 230 miles, or approximately 4 hours. County roads are not 
available as detours due to the rough canyon terrain that the highway travels. 
In December 2024, CDOT determined that repair expenses are expected to exceed three 
million dollars ($3,000,000) and may be eligible for federal reimbursement through the Federal 
Highway Administration (FHWA). This amendment to the Executive Order provides CDOT the 
ability to seek federal reimbursement for eligible expenses related to the bridge repair. 
 
Amendments 
1. 
Executive Order D 2024 014 is amended to include Montrose County in the 
disaster declaration due to extensive and serious damage to bridge K-01-B on CO 
141 in Montrose County. 
2. 
Section II.E of Executive Order D 2024 014 is amended to add as follows: 
E. I authorize CDOT to pursue federal transportation emergency relief funds under 
sections 120 and 125 of Title 23 of the United States Code. I request that the FHWA 
Division Administrator concur in the declaration of this disaster emergency for the 
purposes of securing FHWA Emergency Relief Program Funds to provide CDOT with 
personnel and resources to assist with slide area monitoring, mudslides and debris flows 
cleanup operations, and repair of any damaged or destroyed highway assets for CDOT 
Bridge Structure K-01-B, located at mile marker 85.52 on CO 141 in Montrose County. 
 
 

Executive Order D 2025 002 
February 7, 2025 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. In all other respects, Executive Order D 2024 014 shall remain 
in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this seventh  
day of February, 2025. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2025 003
full_text:
JARED POLIS 
GOVERNOR 
136 STATE CAPITOL 
DENVER, COLORADO 80203 
TEL 303-866-2471 
FAx 303-866-2003 
D 2025 003 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending and Restating Executive Order D 2023 018 and Superseding and Rescinding 
Executive Orders D 2022 016 and D 2019 016 Concerning the Greening of State 
Government 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order amending and restating Executive 
Order D 2023 018 and superseding and rescinding Executive Orders D 2022 016 and D 2019 016 
concerning the greening of State government to reflect the State's new goals and directives 
coordinated through the Office of Sustainability to ensure the State's commitment to efficient 
and sustainable government operations. 
I. 
Background and Purpose 
For over two decades, Colorado has practiced what we preach and demonstrated a 
sustained commitment to sustainability and energy efficiency in government operations through 
a series of 11 Executive Orders issued by four different Governors. These Greening Government 
Executive Orders have directed State agencies to pursue energy performance contracts, generate 
renewable energy, reduce water consumption, implement LED lighting retrofits, adopt 
environmentally preferred purchasing practices, and transition to electric and alternative fuel 
vehicles. 
State agencies have made significant progress toward the goals outlined in previous 
Executive Orders. Accomplishments include a 26% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from 
State operations, a 15% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions from regular use State fleet 
vehicles (and 7.5% for special use vehicles), and a 15% reduction in potable water consumption. 
State agencies have undergone significant LED retrofits, installation of solar photovoltaic 
systems, and water efficient landscape conversions. Our progress on sustainability across the 
State fleet and operations not only reduces emissions and saves taxpayer dollars- it also 
demonstrates that government can lead by example in building a cleaner, more efficient future. 
Most recently, Executive Order D 2023 018 created the Office of Sustainability, which 
reports to the Executive Director of the Department of Personnel and Administration (DPA). The 
Office of Sustainability is tasked to work across State government to advise and support State 
capital planning, procurement, and operations to achieve Colorado's greening government efforts 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 2 of 8 
and the updated goals outlined in Executive Order D 2023 018. The Office of Sustainability also 
leads the Greening Government Leadership Council (GGLC) to report yearly on our State's 
progress toward meeting statewide sustainability goals. 
Building on the accomplishments to date, this Executive Order sets new, ambitious goals 
to drive continued progress and consolidate the State's greening government efforts. By 
continuing to deploy greening government measures, the State will lead by example, save 
taxpayer money, and reduce the impact of State operations on our environment and public health. 
This Executive Order amends Executive Order D 2023 018 and supersedes and rescinds 
previous Greening Government Executive Orders D 2022 016 and D 2019 016. To accomplish 
these new goals and directives, each agency and department managed by Governor-appointed 
executives (collectively," Agency or Agencies") shall develop and implement plans, programs, 
and policies that incorporate the sustainability practices outlined in this Executive Order within 
existing budgets and authorities. 
II. 
Greening Government Goals 
I direct Agencies to: 
a) 
Reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 50% in State Operations by FY 2034 
over the FY 2019 baseline. 
b) 
Reduce greenhouse gas emissions by at least 32% in the State Fleet by FY 2034 
over the FY 2019 baseline. 
c) 
Reduce energy use per square foot in State Facilities by at least 20% by FY 2034 
over the FY 2019 baseline. 
d) 
Reduce potable water consumption across Agencies by at least 20% by FY 2034 
over the FY 2015 baseline. 
Each Agency should consider creating a Greening Government Executive Order (GGEO) 
management team to include a: ( 1) facility manager, (2) fleet coordinator, (3) budget director and 
(4) GGLC representative. The GGEO management teams of each Agency shall develop and 
implement plans, programs, and policies that incorporate the sustainability practices outlined in 
this Executive Order within existing budgets and authorities. The GGEO management teams 
should have knowledge on building maintenance and funding needs, building operations and 
usage, EV acquisition and utilization, and EV charging planning. 
The Office of Sustainability will provide leadership to and require accountability from 
Agencies regarding the ongoing sustainability directives in this Executive Order outlined below, 
which apply to all Agencies. 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 3 of 8 
A. 
Agency Sustainability Plans 
a) DPA will develop a Sustainability Plan template in coordination with the GGLC. 
Each Agency should prepare an annual Sustainability Plan to include the topics of 
Waste Diversion, Energy Management, Water Management, Petroleum Management, 
and other topics outlined in the Sustainability Plan template. Sustainability Plans will 
be due on or before May l st of each year starting in May 2026. 
B. 
Facility Management Directives to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Energy Usage 
a) Identify and pursue energy performance contracts (EPCs) where feasible and cost 
effective. Agencies shall pursue feasibility studies for any facility that has not 
undergone an energy performance contract by January l, 2029. The asset owner or 
facility manager is responsible for undergoing the EPCs unless otherwise established 
by an interagency agreement. 
1. 
Facilities that may be exempt from this requirement include: 
l . Any building without a heating, ventilation, or air conditioning system. 
2. Buildings with a conditioned space smaller than 5,000 square feet; 
conditioned space is defined as a space with both heating and cooling 
(i.e. a shop building or garage that only has heating or a facility that 
only has cooling would not be considered conditioned space). 
3. Temporary structures. 
11. 
Where EPCs are not feasible, Agencies shall explain why EPCs are not 
suitable during the capital construction and capital renewal budget process and 
why additional funding cannot be secured to support such budget requests. 
b) Identify and evaluate options that maximize cost-effective energy efficiency Heating, 
Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) systems, including the use of geothermal 
(i.e., passive geothermal or ground-source heat pump) systems for any major capital 
investments, as defined by the Office of State Architect (OSA); however, the 
Executive Director of DPA may grant a waiver for Agencies to install high-efficiency 
electric equipment (e.g., air source heat pumps) if the relevant Agency evaluates 
geothermal systems and establishes that geothermal will not work. 
c) Ensure contractors' evaluation of HV AC technology includes consideration of the 
social costs of carbon and methane consistent with cost principles identified in § 40­
3 .2-106( 4 ), C.R.S. 
d) Identify and pursue water heating systems changes for State buildings that are cost­
effective, to include electric instant hot water heaters, heat pump water heaters, or a 
combined air and water geothermal/thermal energy network system. The cost should 
include the analysis of any applicable and associated energy costs from heating water. 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 4 of 8 
C. 
Energy Use Reporting Directives 
a) To comply with internal statewide sustainability tracking, ensure accurate tracking 
and reporting of utility data monthly in EnergyCAP, the statewide energy and 
sustainability management software, or another Office of Sustainability-approved 
utility tracking database. 
b) Agencies must report on State-owned buildings 5,000 square feet and above in the 
utility reporting database. If the buildings exist in a campus setting, on a single meter, 
and the total gross square footage exceeds 5,000 square feet, they should be included 
in the utility reporting database. 
D. 
Capital Construction and Capital Renewal Request Directives 
Include the following in capital construction or capital renewal requests submitted to the 
OSA and the Governor's Office of State Planning and Budgeting (OSPB) for new construction 
or substantial renovations I to existing buildings. Consultation between OSA and OSPB may be 
necessary for such requests: 
a) Documentation of how requested projects conform with the State's High Performance 
Certification Program as outlined in OSA's Capital Construction Budget Instructions, 
Section I. 
b) Pursue onsite renewable energy generation or renewable clean energy purchases. 
1. 
Include documentation of renewable energy generation or the infeasibility of 
using renewable energy sources at the facility. 
11. 
Explore, as part of any renewable energy system procurement, whether the 
installation of energy storage systems is feasible and cost-effective. If found to 
be feasible and cost-effective, renewable energy systems shall include energy 
storage. 
m. 
Evaluation of cost-effectiveness shall include the value of resilience during 
power outages or natural disasters utilizing the value of lost load or a similar 
calculation and shall take into account the benefits of continuity of 
government services during power outages. 
c) Provide analysis of public-private partnership opportunities for financing projects, 
such as the use of energy performance contracts (EPC). Agencies shall make their 
best efforts to include EPC funding wherever possible and include projects with 
energy and water improvements. 
1 C.R.S. * 24-30-1305.5 8(c) "Substantial renovation" means any renovation with a cost that exceeds twenty-five 
percent of the value of the building. 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 5 of 8 
d) Join, as available, utility programs such as time-of-use billing and other demand 
management programs. 
e) Document Agency plans to meet requirements under§ 24-30-1305.5, C.R.S., for 
reporting building utility performance data to OSA through either a nationally 
recognized building certification program, a Colorado Energy Office (CEO)­
approved utility tracking database or software, or another OSA-accepted procedure; 
f) If parking is part of the proposed project and the site has electricity, provide 
documentation or plans that at least 28%, rounding up to the nearest whole space, of 
parking will be pre-wired ( electric panel capacity, conduit, wiring, and 
receptacle/junction box) for Level 2 charging or Direct Current Fast Charger, and that 
at least 2%, rounding up to the nearest whole space, will have Level 2 chargers or 
DCFC chargers installed to comply with the Colorado Model Electric Ready and 
Solar Ready Code. Existing parking that is left alone but serving a new building is 
exempt from this requirement. 
g) Provide documentation that the Agency reviewed options for the full electrification of 
building systems, including consideration of geothermal heating and cooling, and 
such review shall include a full life-cycle cost analysis of the impact of electrification, 
including the social cost of carbon and methane consistent with § 40-3.2-106( 4), 
C.R.S.; and 
h) For new construction, document that the building is built to net zero, near net zero 
greenhouse gas emissions standards, all electric, or meets the State's High 
Performance Certification Policy. 
E. 
State Fleet Management Directives to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions 
a) Agencies shall develop, in partnership with DPA and CEO, an annual fleet 
electrification plan to include the Agency's current and planned fleet, use cases of 
vehicles, and locations for needed EV charging stations and planned EV charging 
stations. 
b) Ensure accurate tracking and reporting of State fleet fuel use on the Colorado 
Automotive Reporting System (CARS), or successor system, as administered by State 
Fleet Management (SFM). The Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) shall 
use established reporting for all on-road and off-road vehicles that are not reported in 
CARS. On-road vehicles for CDOT shall be measured in miles per gallon and off­
road vehicles for CDOT shall be measured by gallons per hour. 
c) Purchase battery electric vehicles (BEVs) in cases where BEVs meet the Agency's 
operational needs and infrastructure is in place or is planned to be in place prior to 
vehicles arriving. Agencies may continue to purchase plug-in hybrid electric vehicles 
(PHEV) in cases where PHEV s better match the operational needs or charging 
infrastructure is planned for installation after arrival of the vehicles. 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 6 of 8 
d) Ensure that EVs (BEVs and PHEVs) are the default vehicle type for all light-duty 
vehicles for future vehicle purchases. If an electric model is available to the State that 
is the same vehicle type approved for replacement, DP A, with input from CEO, shall 
select the EV. In cases where an Agency believes that the selected EV will not meet 
the operational need, Agencies may request reconsideration of the selection by DP A. 
Agencies may appeal DP A's decision for final determination by the Governor. 
e) Work in partnership with DPA when DPA selects medium and heavy-duty Zero 
Emission Vehicles for Agency fleets where medium and heavy-duty Zero Emission 
Vehicles are cost-effective, meet operational needs, and are available in the 
marketplace. 
f) Ensure that any EV added to the fleet shall be used as an opportunity to provide 
education and outreach to the public and State employees. This can include vehicle 
wraps, bumper stickers, brochures, and other information within the vehicle, as well 
as training for vehicle users. 
g) Install charging stations at State facilities. Where additional infrastructure is needed, 
including at leased facilities, Agencies shall identify funding and pursue grants and 
rebates to install sufficient charging infrastructure. Explore, where cost-effective, the 
integration of bidirectional functionality for charging stations, including where 
vehicles can be utilized for vehicle-to-building services to reduce utility bills and 
utility demand charges. Where bidirectional functionality is considered, cost­
effectiveness shall include the value of resilience during power outages or natural 
disasters. 
h) Agencies shall report the installation of charging stations to the Office of 
Sustainability. 
i) Pursue planning and implementation of electric vehicle charging stations and 
expansion of alternative fuel vehicle technologies in partnership with agency facility 
management teams. 
j) DPA shall optimize take-home EVs by replacing take-home vehicles with EVs in 
each vehicle procurement cycle, with a goal of increasing the number of take-home 
EVs ordered in each successive round. 
k) DPA and COOT shall establish policies and procedures to promote cost-effective use 
of Alternative Fuel Vehicles (AFVs) and other fleet efficiency improvements 
wherever feasible. 
F. 
Lawn Equipment Electrification Directives to Reduce Greenhouse Gas Emissions 
As equipment reaches the end of its useful life, Agencies will purchase electric lawn 
equipment to meet the Air Quality Control Commissions' rule prohibiting gas powered 
equipment from June I to August 31, beginning 2025. 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 7 of 8 
a) Beginning June 1, 2025, Agencies cannot use gasoline-powered push and hand-held 
lawn and garden equipment with an internal combustion engine smaller than 19kw 
(25 horsepower) from June 1 to August 31 of the current year. Agencies should phase 
out gas-powered push and handheld lawn and garden equipment with internal 
combustion engines that are less than 25 horsepower to the maximum extent possible 
with current funds. Excluded equipment includes equipment used for abating or 
preventing damage during a declared disaster emergency, equipment used by first 
responders to provide emergency services, for the purpose of fire hazard reduction 
activities in or near wildland areas or wildland-urban interface, or riparian, forest or 
grassland management. 
b) Agencies procuring landscape services and contracts should include an evaluation 
factor to prefer vendors who utilize solely or primarily electrified lawn equipment. 
G. 
Water Efficient Practices to Reduce Water Usage 
a) Any new construction or substantial renovation projects at a State-owned facility that 
begins a design solicitation or where landscaping is being modified, shall incorporate 
regional climatic and ecosystem considerations as well as water efficient irrigation 
and follow the Statewide Water Efficient Landscape Policy. 
1. 
The water efficient criteria shall require the installation of sustainable, 
drought-tolerant, and ecoregion-appropriate landscaping, including drought­
tolerant lawn grasses and forbs, prioritizing native species and pollinators and 
excluding invasive-non-native plants. 
11. 
High-efficiency irrigation infrastructure shall be utilized in areas where high­
water turf grass is non-essential, such as medians, roundabouts, parking lot 
islands, slopes greater than 4: 1, and areas not used for water quality. 
b) Pursue water-efficient landscaping and water-efficient improvements for State real 
property to the extent funding is available to support the Colorado Water Plan. 
H. 
Office of Sustainability Directives 
I direct the Office of Sustainability to: 
a) Develop waste diversion goals and best practices for waste diversion and improved 
recycling and organics collection at Agencies. Agencies' facilities management units 
should prepare waste prevention and diversion plans annually. 
b) Develop, in consultation with the State Purchasing and Contracting Office, 
environmentally preferred purchasing goals and standards for office paper, office 
supplies, facility maintenance supplies, and single-use supplies. The Office of 

Executive Order D 2025 003 
April 22, 2025 
Page 8 of 8 
Sustainability may choose to create environmentally preferred purchasing goals and 
standards for any other State supplies in partnership with Agency procurement teams. 
c) Review and implement green commuting opportunities for all Agencies to decrease 
the environmental impact of State government operations. 
d) Provide regular reporting on all greening government goals and metrics to the 
Executive Director of DPA and the Governor's Office. 
111. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2023 018 is hereby amended and restated by this Executive Order. 
Executive Orders D 2022 016 and D 2019 016 are superseded and rescinded by this Executive 
Order. This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by a future 
Executive Order of the Governor. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twenty-second 
day of April, 2025. 
Jared Polis 
Governor 
•

title: D 2025 004
full_text:
Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 1 of 7 
D 2025 004 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Executive Orders D 2020 171, D 2023 015, D 2022 019, D 2022 024, D 2022 025, 
and D 2022 030, Pertaining to the Pine Gulch, Bent’s Fort, Fort Lyons, High Park, Ute 
Pass, and Menkhaven Fires to Ensure Sufficient and Continued Availability of Disaster 
Emergency Funds  
And 
Amending Executive Order D 2013 026 (and subsequent Executive Orders) Pertaining to 
the Historic 2013 Flooding in Colorado to Ensure Continued Availability of Disaster 
Emergency Funds 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending prior Executive 
Orders pertaining to the Pine Gulch, Bent’s Fort, Fort Lyons, High Park, Ute Pass, and 
Menkhaven Fires to ensure sufficient and continued availability of disaster emergency funds to 
respond to these disasters.  
In addition, I hereby issue this Executive Order amending Executive Order D 2013 026 
and subsequent Executive Orders pertaining to the historic 2013 flooding in Colorado to ensure 
continued availability of disaster emergency funds. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire, flood . . . .” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
Fires 
The Pine Gulch Fire started on July 31, 2020, on Bureau of Land Management land in 
Mesa County. On August 2, 2020, at approximately 9:10 PM, I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency for the Pine Gulch Fire in Mesa County and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. On August 22, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 171,  

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 2 of 7 
 
memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $8,000,000 from the 
Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, response, 
consequence management, and recovery efforts related to the Pine Gulch Fire, and making the 
funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date of my Executive Order. On August 22, 
2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 015, which extended the duration provision for funding 
in the original Executive Order for an additional twenty-four (24) months. This extension 
ensured the funds allocated in the original Executive Order would remain available to enable 
State agencies to coordinate ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation efforts related to the 
Pine Gulch Fire. 
The Bent’s Fort Fire started on April 12, 2022, in Otero County, on private land near La 
Junta, Colorado. Shortly thereafter, also on April 12, the Fort Lyons Fire started in Bent County, 
on private land near Las Animas, Colorado. On April 12, 2022, at approximately 7:21 PM, I 
verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Bent’s Fort and Fort Lyons Fires in Otero and 
Bent Counties and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On May 11, 2022, I issued 
Executive Order D 2022 019, memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, 
encumbering $611,000 from the DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, 
response, consequence management, and recovery efforts related to the Bent’s Fort and Fort 
Lyons Fires, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date of my 
Executive Order. 
The High Park Fire started on May 12, 2022, in Teller County on private land west of 
Cripple Creek, Colorado. On May 13, 2022, at approximately 8:38 PM, I verbally declared a 
disaster emergency for the High Park Fire in Teller County and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. On June 8, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 024, 
memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $1,835,000 from the 
DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, 
and recovery efforts related to the High Park Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six 
(36) months from the date of my Executive Order. 
The Ute Pass Fire started on May 13, 2022, in La Plata County on private land east of 
Durango, Colorado. On May 13, 2022, at approximately 8:31 PM, I verbally declared a disaster 
emergency for the Ute Pass Fire in La Plata County and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. On June 8, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 025, 
memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $115,000 from the 
DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, 
and recovery efforts related to the Ute Pass Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six 
(36) months from the date of my Executive Order. 
 

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 3 of 7 
 
The Menkhaven Fire started on May 28, 2022, in Conejos County west of Antonito, 
Colorado. On May 28, 2022, at approximately 5:48 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
for the Menkhaven Fire in Conejos County and activated the State of Colorado Emergency 
Operations Plan. On June 26, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 030, memorializing my 
verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $810,000 from the DEF for costs 
associated with the State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, and recovery 
efforts related to the Menkhaven Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months 
from the date of my Executive Order. 
2013 Flooding 
Beginning on September 11, 2013, heavy rainfall fell west of Interstate 25 from south of 
Colorado Springs to the Wyoming border. This rainfall led to historic flooding, resulting in loss 
of life, injuries, and widespread property and infrastructure damage across more than 20 counties 
in Colorado. On September 12, 2013, Governor Hickenlooper verbally declared a disaster 
emergency for the flooding in Boulder and Larimer Counties and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan. On September 13, 2013, Governor Hickenlooper issued Executive 
Order D 2013 026, memorializing his verbal declaration of a disaster emergency and 
encumbering funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) to pay for the flood response and 
recovery. This disaster declaration was later expanded by Governor Hickenlooper to cover 24 
counties in the State. The disaster declaration was subsequently extended and amended in 
Executive Orders D 2013 027, D 2013 028, D 2013 030, D 2013 031, D 2013 033, D 2014 001, 
D 2014 002, D 2014 006, D 2014 012, D 2016 005, and D 2019 001.  
In 2019, I amended these Executive Orders due to the need for additional resources for 
ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation efforts. In Executive Order D 2019 001, I transferred 
$12,500,000, as authorized by HB 18-1322, from the General Fund to the DEF to pay for 
response, recovery, and mitigation efforts connected with the 2013 Flooding. I also encumbered 
$7,000,000 in the DEF to pay for response, recovery, and mitigation efforts connected with the 
2013 Flooding. The total funds allocated to the 2013 Flooding were to remain available for sixty 
(60) months from December 9, 2019.  
This Executive Order 
This Executive Order amends Executive Orders D 2020 171, D 2023 015, D 2022 019, D 
2022 024, D 2022 025, and D 2022 030, pertaining to the Pine Gulch, Bent’s Fort, Fort Lyons, 
High Park, Ute Pass, and Menkhaven Fires to ensure sufficient and continued availability of 
disaster emergency funds to respond to these disasters. Specifically, this Executive Order extends 
availability of funds for each fire by another forty-eight (48) months, so that encumbered funds 
in the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) may continue to be used for response and recovery 

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 4 of 7 
efforts related to these fires, and so that State agencies are able to continue to coordinate and 
complete fire billing and reimbursement processes with affected local and federal agencies. 
In addition, this Executive Order allocates an additional $36,500 to the Bent’s Fort and 
Fort Lyons Fires; $242,100 to the High Park Fire; and $25,300 to the Ute Pass Fire. These funds 
are necessary for additional response, recovery, and suppression expenses for these fires beyond 
the initial estimates that were available at the time of the fires and the initial Executive Orders. 
The additional funding is transferred from the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund to the DEF, 
and then encumbered in the DEF.  
In sum, this Executive Order amends the duration provisions for funding for six (6) fires 
throughout Colorado in 2020 and 2022, and allocates additional funds for three (3) of these fires: 
•
Pine Gulch Fire in Mesa County (D 2020 171 and D 2023 015; $8,000,000
encumbered from the DEF)
•
Bent’s Fort Fire in Otero County and Fort Lyons Fire in Bent County (D 2022
019; $611,000 encumbered from the DEF)
o
Allocates an additional $36,500, for a total of $647,500
•
High Park Fire in Teller Count (D 2022 024; $1,835,000 encumbered from the
DEF)
o
Allocates an additional $242,100, for a total of $2,077,100
•
Ute Pass Fire in La Plata County (D 2022 025; $115,000 encumbered from the
DEF)
o
Allocates an additional $25,300, for a total of $140,300
•
Menkhaven Fire in Conejos County (D 2022 030; $810,000 encumbered from the
DEF)
This Executive Order also amends Executive Order D 2019 001 pertaining to 2013 
Flooding (which amended Executive Orders D 2013 026, D 2013 027, D 2013 028, D 2013 030, 
D 2013 031, D 2013 033, D 2014 001, D 2014 002, D 2014 006, D 2014 012, and D 2016 005). 
This amendment will ensure sufficient and continued availability of disaster emergency funds to 
respond to this historic event in Colorado. The extension is needed due to the State’s and the 
federal government’s timeline for the administrative closeout of projects and to ensure all local 
entities achieve the maximum reimbursement possible. Specifically, this Executive Order 
extends availability of funds for the 2013 Flooding by another thirty-six (36) months from 
December 9, 2024. 

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 5 of 7 
 
Fire Duration Amendments 
1. Executive Order D 2020 171 (Pine Gulch Fire), as amended by D 2023 015, is amended 
so that the duration clause indicates that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive 
Order D 2020 171 (and amended in Section IV.1 of Executive Order D 2023 015) shall 
remain available for the described purposes for an additional forty-eight (48) months, for 
a total of one hundred and eight (108) months from the date of Executive Order D 2020 
171. 
2. Executive Order D 2022 019 (Bent’s Fort and Fort Lyons Fires) is amended so that the 
duration clause indicates that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 
2022 019 shall remain available for the described purposes for an additional forty-eight 
(48) months, for a total of eighty-four (84) months from the date of Executive Order D 
2022 019. 
3. Executive Order D 2022 024 (High Park Fire) is amended so that the duration clause 
indicates that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 024 shall 
remain available for the described purposes for an additional forty-eight (48) months, for 
a total of eighty-four (84) months from the date of Executive Order D 2022 024. 
4. Executive Order D 2022 025 (Ute Pass Fire) is amended so that the duration clause 
indicates that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 025 shall 
remain available for the described purposes for an additional forty-eight (48) months, for 
a total of eighty-four (84) months from the date of Executive Order D 2022 025. 
5. Executive Order D 2022 030 (Menkhaven Fire) is amended so that the duration clause 
indicates that the funds described in Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 030 shall 
remain available for the described purposes for an additional forty-eight (48) months, for 
a total of eighty-four (84) months from the date of Executive Order D 2022 030. 
 
Fire Funding Amendments 
1. Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) are 
insufficient to pay for additional response, recovery, and suppression expenses for the 
Bent’s Fort and Fort Lyons Fires ($36,500), High Park Fire ($242,100), and Ute Pass Fire 
($25,300). Therefore, I order that three hundred and three thousand nine hundred dollars 
($303,900) be transferred from the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund to the DEF. 
 
2. Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 019 (Bent’s Fort and Fort Lyons Fires) is 
amended to encumber an additional thirty six thousand, five hundred dollars ($36,500) in 
the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional response, recovery, and suppression 

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 6 of 7 
expenses for these fires beyond the initial estimates that were available at the time of the 
fires and the initial Executive Order, for a total of $647,500 encumbered in the DEF. 
3. Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 024 (High Park Fire) is amended to encumber an 
additional two hundred forty two thousand, one hundred dollars ($242,100) in the 
Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional response, recovery, and suppression 
expenses for this fire beyond the initial estimates that were available at the time of the 
fire and the initial Executive Order, for a total of $2,077,100 encumbered in the DEF. 
4. Section II.C of Executive Order D 2022 025 (Ute Pass Fire) is amended to encumber an 
additional twenty five thousand, three hundred dollars ($25,300) in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional response, recovery, and suppression expenses for 
this fire beyond the initial estimates that were available at the time of the fire and the 
initial Executive Order, for a total of $140,300 encumbered in the DEF. 
 
2013 Flood Duration Amendment 
1. Executive Order D 2019 001 (which amends Executive Orders D 2013 026, D 2013 027, 
D 2013 028, D 2013 030, D 2013 031, D 2013 033, D 2014 001, D 2014 002, D 2014 
006, D 2014 012, and D 2016 005, pertaining to 2013 Flooding) is amended so that the 
duration clause indicates that the funds described in the Executive Orders shall remain 
available for the described purposes for an additional thirty-six (36) months from 
December 9, 2024. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2025 004 
May 9, 2025 
Page 7 of 7 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Orders D 2020 171, D 2023 015, D 2022 019, 
D 2022 024, D 2022 025, and D 2022 030 (pertaining to fires) and Executive Orders D 2013 
026, D 2013 027, D 2013 028, D 2013 030, D 2013 031, D 2013 033, D 2014 001, D 2014 002, 
D 2014 006, D 2014 012, D 2016 005, and D 2019 001 (pertaining to 2013 flooding) shall 
remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this ninth 
day of May, 2025. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2025 005
full_text:
Executive Order D 2025 005 
May 16, 2025 
Page 1 of 6 
D 2025 005 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Regarding Strategic Growth through Compliance with State Laws 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order regarding guidance to State 
agencies and local governments in order to properly execute strategic growth laws that were 
passed by the General Assembly. 
 
Background and Purpose 
This Executive Order provides clarifying guidance to State agencies and local 
governments regarding statutory compliance and enforcement of State laws pertaining to 
recently enacted strategic growth legislation. 
Since the beginning of my administration, one of the most critical needs of the State has 
been the availability of affordable, quality housing for all Coloradans. The concerns I stated in 
the 2023 Executive Order on Strategic Growth continue to escalate statewide; Coloradans are 
even more deeply concerned about the cost and lack of housing supply, not being able to live 
close to where they work, and the impact of increased congestion of our roads, greenhouse gas 
emissions, and the environment. For example, according to the State Demographer, 49% of 
Colorado renters are cost burdened, and housing starts continue to lag behind demand.  
Recognizing this critical need and acknowledging housing barriers as a matter of 
statewide concern, my administration published the Roadmap to Colorado’s Future: 2026, and 
set forth bold goals of investing more than $5 billion into the State transportation and transit 
system by 2031, reducing emissions by 50% from 2005 levels by 2030, and creating sustainable 
housing near job centers for the additional 1.72 million people projected to be added to the State 
population by 2050. 
In partnership with local governments and the General Assembly, we have taken steps to 
build more housing people can afford and to speed up approvals across the State. Since 2020, the 
Department of Local Affairs (DOLA) has awarded more than $768 million in housing 
development funding supporting almost 20,000 housing units, has awarded more than $152 
million for affordable housing supporting the construction of nearly 10,000 affordable housing 
units, and has provided Emergency Rental Assistance through three programs and supported  

Executive Order D 2025 005 
May 16, 2025 
Page 2 of 6 
 
nearly 65,000 households with a total investment of over $538 million. In that same time frame, 
the Office of Economic Development and International Trade (OEDIT) awarded $290 million to 
support the development of 8,650 housing units. OEDIT has also invested an additional $50 
million in Colorado’s offsite construction industry to increase innovation and lower construction 
costs. The State has further deployed various resources to increase the supply of housing through 
subsidization of costs and cost reduction. 
After extensive data analysis, research, testimony, and community engagement to create 
more housing across the State, the General Assembly passed, and I signed into law, the 
following strategic growth legislation in 2024 and 2025: 
 
HB24-1007, Prohibit Residential Occupancy Limits; 
HB24-1152, Accessory Dwelling Units; 
HB24-1304, Minimum Parking Requirements; 
HB24-1313, Housing in Transit-Oriented Communities; 
SB24-174, Sustainable Affordable Housing Assistance; 
HB25-1273, Residential Building Stair Modernization; and 
SB25-002, Regional Building Codes for Factory-Built Structures 
These laws unlock critical tools that offer flexibility and smarter growth for Colorado 
communities to meet our housing, climate, and transportation goals, and will allow housing 
supply to finally start catching up with demand. These laws also recognize that housing, transit, 
environment, and affordability are inextricably linked and that Colorado has a strong and 
legitimate statewide interest in addressing population and development growth across regions to 
support the general health and welfare of Coloradans. Housing insecurity jeopardizes family 
well-being and our economy, and job growth and need for workforce housing in one community 
impacts housing development, water, sewer, and transportation infrastructure in nearby 
jurisdictions. 
I thank all the local governments that have been working toward allowing more housing 
in many ways over many years and are following our State laws to help achieve that goal. I also 
thank those that are experiencing challenges and obstacles and yet still striving in good faith on 
our aligned housing goals and in compliance with State laws. Finally, I appreciate those local 
governments that are attending DOLA webinars, utilizing technical, programmatic, compliance 
tools and resources published by DOLA, and taking advantage of the technical expertise of 
DOLA staff. With feedback received during the 2024 legislative session, $58 million was 
appropriated by the General Assembly to assist local governments in their efforts, on top of 
additional funding of more than $224 million previously appropriated. DOLA also published 
guidance with example code amendments, standards, strategies, and other resources to begin 
implementation, with more resources to come. I encourage all local governments to continue to 
work toward compliance with the seven State laws cited above, both because they are State law  

Executive Order D 2025 005 
May 16, 2025 
Page 3 of 6 
 
and because they are important pieces in the strategic growth puzzle. I encourage them to utilize 
DOLA as a resource to meet our shared housing, climate, and transportation goals. 
I want to reemphasize that the cost of housing is a statewide issue and that, despite many 
communities pursuing local actions, the State is still short of more than 100,000 housing units. 
As such, these regional and statewide actions are critical and our success requires that all local 
governments comply with State law. And as the Governor of the State with vested executive 
authority under the Colorado Constitution to faithfully execute the State’s laws, it is imperative 
to ensure that State funds are appropriately used in the furtherance of State laws and toward the 
legislative goal to incentivize more housing and reduce costs for Coloradans. To ensure State 
funds are effectively used to support these laws and further the legislative goal of incentivizing 
more housing and reducing costs for Coloradans, this Executive Order outlines the scope of State 
funding that will be tied to compliance with strategic growth laws. 
 
Definitions 
 
The following definitions apply within this Executive Order: 
A. 
“Funding Opportunities” means competitive or discretionary grants, contracts, 
loans, incentive programs, and discretionary tax credits that provide support to
