Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this ninth 
day of December, 2025.

title: D 2026 001
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 001 
February 26, 2026 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2026 001 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza in Weld 
County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza in Weld County, enabling State 
agencies to better support our agriculture sector by coordinating for statewide monitoring and 
mitigation of disease spread, response, consequence management, and recovery efforts. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) was initially detected in the United States in 
the wild bird population in January 2022 in South Carolina and in the domestic poultry 
population in February 2022 in Indiana. HPAI has spread across the United States over the past 
four years. The disease spreads rapidly among wild and domestic poultry. It has a mortality rate 
above ninety percent in poultry.  
HPAI was detected in wild birds in Colorado on March 24, 2022, with ongoing detection 
in the State since that time. Cases in Colorado are occurring in wild, domestic, and commercial 
flocks, including occasional spillover into mammals. 
On January 29, 2026, the Colorado Department of Agriculture was notified of a high 
mortality event at a commercial table egg layer facility in Weld County; the Colorado State 
University Veterinary Diagnostic Laboratory reported presumptive positive results for HPAI that 
afternoon. The facility had approximately 1.3 million chickens on site at the time of the 
diagnosis.  
On January 29, 2026, at approximately 8:05 PM, the Lieutenant Governor, acting as 
Governor, verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza in  

Executive Order D 2026 001 
February 26, 2026 
Page 2 of 3 
 
Weld County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. This Executive Order 
memorializes that verbal declaration. 
On January 30, 2026, HPAI was confirmed in the Weld County facility by the United 
States Department of Agriculture National Veterinary Services Laboratory. 
Oversight of the response to the outbreak in livestock and implementation of statewide 
surveillance and protective measures requires the deployment and coordination of multiple State 
agencies, including the Colorado Department of Agriculture, Colorado Department of Public 
Health and Environment, the Colorado Division of Homeland Security and Emergency 
Management, and federal partners including the United States Department of Agriculture. 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
HPAI in Weld County and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency 
under C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. The Lieutenant Governor’s verbal order of 
January 29, 2026, declaring a disaster emergency is hereby memorialized by this 
Executive Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were 
contained within this Executive Order. 
B. 
The Lieutenant Governor’s verbal order of January 29, 2026, activated the State 
Emergency Operations Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to 
take whatever actions may be required and requested by the Director or their 
delegate of the Office of Emergency Management (OEM), pursuant to the State 
Emergency Operations Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and 
equipment as necessary. 
C. 
Personal Protective Equipment at the OEM’s Resource Asset Management site 
shall be made available to protect the health and safety of response workers and 
support personnel at the affected sites, and to minimize further spread of HPAI. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for statewide monitoring and mitigation of disease 
spread, response, consequence management, and recovery efforts related to HPAI. 
Therefore, I order that two hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) be transferred 
from the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber 
two hundred thousand dollars ($200,000) in the DEF for costs associated with the 
State’s statewide monitoring and mitigation of disease spread, response, 
consequence management, and recovery efforts related to the HPAI in Colorado. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 001 
February 26, 2026 
Page 3 of 3 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the OEM is authorized and 
directed to allocate the funding described in Section Il(D) to the appropriate 
government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute awards, purchase 
orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds described 
in Section II(D) above shall remain available for this purpose until seventy-two 
(72) months from the date of this Executive Order, and any unexpended funds 
shall remain in the DEF. 
F. 
The OEM, in coordination with the Colorado Department of Agriculture and the 
Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment, is authorized to 
coordinate application for any funds available related to this disaster emergency. 
Specifically, the Colorado Department of Agriculture is directed to seek 
reimbursement, including reimbursement from the United States Department of 
Agriculture for response activities under the 2026 Highly Pathogenic Avian 
Influenza Emergency Cooperative Agreement, and make payments as needed to 
agencies providing assistance on this incident. 
III. 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from January 29, 2026, unless 
extended further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall 
remain available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this 
Executive Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twenty-sixth 
day of February, 2026.

title: D 2026 002
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 002 
May 12, 2026 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2026 002 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Schwachheim Fire in Las Animas County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the Schwachheim Fire in Las Animas County, enabling State and federal 
agencies to coordinate fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and 
recovery efforts. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.”  C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On April 12, 2026, the Schwachheim Fire began near Trinidad in Las Animas County. 
The fire burned on the Lake Dorothey State Wildlife Area on State land. While limited structures 
were immediately threatened, fuels conditions and forecasted weather conditions indicated high 
potential for additional fire growth. Additionally, a steep, inaccessible canyon with hazardous 
terrain led to difficult access and caused the Schwachheim Fire to exceed the capacity of local 
agencies.  
On April 13, 2026, at approximately 3:25 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
due to the Schwachheim Fire in Las Animas County, and activated the State of Colorado 
Emergency Operations Plan and directed the Office of Emergency Management (OEM) to take 
all necessary and appropriate State actions to assist the affected jurisdictions with their fire 
suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts. The Lake 
Dorothey State Wildlife Area remained closed during the emergency response period and is now 
open. As of April 24, 2026, the Schwachheim Fire had burned 1,582 acres and was 100% 
contained. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 002 
May 12, 2026 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Schwachheim Fire and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency 
under C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of April 13, 2026, declaring a 
disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall 
have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive 
Order. 
B. 
My verbal order of April 13, 2026, activated the State Emergency Operations 
Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to take whatever actions 
may be required and requested by the OEM Director or their delegate pursuant to 
the State Emergency Operations Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and 
equipment, as necessary. 
C. 
I direct State agencies to pursue all available federal funding for response and 
recovery operations. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Schwachheim Fire. 
Therefore, I order that three million seven hundred fifty-nine thousand dollars 
($3,759,000) be transferred from the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund 
(SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber three million seven hundred fifty-nine thousand 
dollars ($3,759,000) in the DEF to pay for fire suppression, response, 
consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the 
Schwachheim Fire. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the OEM Director is authorized and 
directed to allocate the funding described in Section II(D) to the appropriate 
government agencies and nonprofit organizations and execute awards, purchase 
orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds described 
in Section II(D) shall remain available for this purpose until seventy-two (72) 
months from the date of this Executive Order, and any unexpended funds shall 
remain in the DEF. 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 002 
May 12, 2026 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from April 13, 2026, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive 
Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twelfth  
day of May, 2026. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2026 003
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 1 of 9 
D 2026 003 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Amending Previous Disaster Emergency Executive Orders to Ensure Sufficient and 
Continued Availability of Disaster Emergency Funds 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order amending previous disaster 
emergency Executive Orders to ensure sufficient and continued availability of disaster 
emergency funds.  
Specifically, this Executive Order amends prior Executive Orders pertaining to the 
Gageby Creek Fire, Grizzly Creek and Red Canyon Fire, Cameron Peak Fire, Mullen Fire, East 
Troublesome Fire, Calwood Fire, Marshall Fire, June 2023 Severe Weather and Flooding, and 
Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County, to ensure sufficient and continued availability of 
disaster emergency funds to respond to these disasters. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire, flood . . . .” C.R.S § 24-33.5-703(3). 
Fires 
The Gageby Creek Fire started on April 18, 2023, in Bent County. On April 20, 2023, at 
approximately 8:35 AM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Gageby Creek Fire in 
Bent County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On May 16, 2023, I issued 
Executive Order D 2023 010, memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, 
encumbering $340,000 from the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) for costs associated with the 
State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts 
related to the Gageby Creek Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from 
the date of my Executive Order.  
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 2 of 9 
 
The Grizzly Creek Fire started on August 10, 2020, in Garfield County. On August 12, 
2020, at approximately 3:00 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Grizzly Creek 
Fire in Garfield and Eagle Counties and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. The Red 
Canyon Fire started on August 19, 2020, in Garfield County. On September 9, 2020, I issued 
Executive Order D 2020 187 memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, 
encumbering $3,150,000 from the DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, 
response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Grizzly Creek 
Fire and Red Canyon Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the 
date of my Executive Order. On September 8, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 017, which 
extended the duration provision for funding in the original Executive Order for an additional 
thirty-six (36) months. This extension ensured the funds allocated in the original Executive Order 
would remain available to enable State agencies to coordinate ongoing response, recovery, and 
mitigation efforts related to the Grizzly Creek and Red Canyon Fire. 
The Cameron Peak Fire started on August 13, 2020, in Larimer County. On September 6, 
2020, at approximately 4:15 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Cameron Peak 
Fire in Larimer County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On October 5, 2020, 
I issued Executive Order D 2020 208, memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster 
emergency, encumbering $6,739,500 from the DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire 
suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the 
Cameron Peak Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date of 
my Executive Order. On September 8, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 017, which 
extended the duration provision for funding in the original Executive Order for an additional 
thirty-six (36) months. This extension ensured the funds allocated in the original Executive Order 
would remain available to enable State agencies to coordinate ongoing response, recovery, and 
mitigation efforts related to the Cameron Peak Fire. 
The Mullen Fire started on September 17, 2020, in Carbon County, Wyoming and burned 
into Jackson County, Colorado. On October 1, 2020, at approximately 5:45 PM, I verbally 
declared a disaster emergency for the Mullen Fire in Jackson County and activated the State 
Emergency Operations Plan. On October 30, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 233, 
memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $610,000 from the 
DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, 
mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Mullen Fire, and making the funds available for 
thirty-six (36) months from the date of my Executive Order. On September 8, 2023, I issued 
Executive Order D 2023 017, which extended the duration provision for funding in the original 
Executive Order for an additional thirty-six (36) months. This extension ensured the funds 
allocated in the original Executive Order would remain available to enable State agencies to 
coordinate ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation efforts related to the Mullen Fire. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 3 of 9 
 
The East Troublesome Fire started on October 14, 2020, in Grand County. On October 
17, 2020, at approximately 12:05 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the East 
Troublesome Fire in Grand County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On 
November 15, 2020, I issued Executive Order D 2020 252, memorializing my verbal declaration 
of a disaster emergency, encumbering $1,495,000 from the DEF for costs associated with the 
State’s fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts 
related to the East Troublesome Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months 
from the date of my Executive Order. On September 8, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 
017, which extended the duration provision for funding in the original Executive Order for an 
additional thirty-six (36) months. This extension ensured the funds allocated in the original 
Executive Order would remain available to enable State agencies to coordinate ongoing 
response, recovery, and mitigation efforts related to the East Troublesome Fire. 
The Calwood Fire started on October 17, 2020, in Boulder County. On October 17, 2020, 
at approximately 3:00 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Calwood Fire in 
Boulder County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On November 15, 2020, I 
issued Executive Order D 2020 253, memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster 
emergency, encumbering $3,648,000 from the DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire 
suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the 
Calwood Fire, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date of my 
Executive Order. On September 8, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 017, which extended 
the duration provision for funding in the original Executive Order for an additional thirty-six (36) 
months. This extension ensured the funds allocated in the original Executive Order would remain 
available to enable State agencies to coordinate ongoing response, recovery, and mitigation 
efforts related to the Calwood Fire. 
The Marshall Fire started on December 30, 2021, in Boulder County. On December 30, 
2021, at approximately 2:18 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency for the Marshall Fire 
in Boulder County and activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. On January 26, 2022, I 
issued Executive Order D 2022 004, memorializing my verbal declaration of a disaster 
emergency, encumbering $40,431,266 from the DEF for costs associated with the State’s fire 
suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the 
Marshall Fire and to employ two term-limited staff to advise on and coordinate wildfire recovery 
efforts, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date of my Executive 
Order.  
On January 28, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 005, which extended the disaster 
declaration for the Marshall Fire. On February 25, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 012, 
amending Executive Orders D 2022 004 and D 2022 005, to return ten million dollars 
($10,000,000) from the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) to the State Emergency Reserve Cash  

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 4 of 9 
 
Fund (SERCF). On May 11, 2022, I issued Executive Order D 2022 018, amending the prior 
Executive Orders to unencumber $19,168,322 in the DEF due to the increased Federal 
government share of funds to manage and respond to the Marshall Fire in Boulder County. On 
January 24, 2025, I issued Executive Order D 2025 001, which extended the duration provision 
for funding in the original Executive Order for an additional twenty-four (24) months. This 
extension ensured the funds allocated in the other Executive Orders would remain available for 
response and recovery efforts related to the Marshall Fire. 
June 2023 Severe Weather and Flooding 
Beginning June 8, 2023, and continuing through June 24, 2023, Colorado experienced a 
destructive and disruptive summer flood, flash flood, tornado, and severe weather season. On 
June 22, 2023, at approximately 4:45 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency due to the 
June 2023 Severe Weather and Flooding in El Paso, Elbert, Lincoln, and Washington Counties 
and activated the State of Colorado Emergency Operations Plan. On July 18, 2023, at 
approximately 3:00 PM, after further assessments with the Federal Emergency Management 
Agency (FEMA), I verbally extended the disaster emergency declaration and expanded the 
declaration to include Arapahoe, Cheyenne, Douglas, Kit Carson, and Logan Counties.  
On July 21, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 011, memorializing my verbal 
declaration of a disaster emergency, encumbering $50,000,000 from the DEF for response, 
consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the June 2023 Severe 
Weather and Flooding, and making the funds available for thirty-six (36) months from the date 
of my Executive Order. On September 22, 2023, I issued Executive Order D 2023 019, which 
declared a disaster emergency for the same weather event in Baca, Jefferson, Kiowa, Prowers, 
and Teller Counties, and made the already-encumbered $50,000,000 available to pay for 
response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts in these counties as well. 
Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County 
On or around August 20, 2025, inspectors of the Office of Funeral and Mortuary Science 
Services in the Colorado Department of Regulatory Agencies (DORA) arrived at Davis 
Mortuary, a funeral home in Pueblo, for a statutorily mandated annual inspection. Inspectors 
found several bodies in various stages of decomposition in a concealed room. On August 21, 
2025, the City of Pueblo, Pueblo County Sheriff’s Office, and the Board of County 
Commissioners of Pueblo County declared a local disaster in Pueblo County due to the situation 
at Davis Mortuary and the involvement of the elected Pueblo County Coroner.  
On August 22, 2025, I issued Executive Order D 2025 013, declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County, activating the State of 
Colorado Emergency Operations Plan, encumbering $300,000 from the DEF for response,  

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 5 of 9 
 
consequence management, and hazard mitigation efforts, and making the funds available for 
seventy-two (72) months from the date of my Executive Order.  
This Executive Order 
This Executive Order amends the Executive Orders outlined above pertaining to the 
following disaster emergencies: Gageby Creek Fire, Grizzly Creek and Red Canyon Fire, 
Cameron Peak Fire, Mullen Fire, East Troublesome Fire, Calwood Fire, Marshall Fire, June 
2023 Severe Weather and Flooding, and Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County. This 
Executive Order ensures sufficient and continued availability of disaster emergency funds to 
respond to these disasters.  
Specifically, this Executive Order extends availability of funds for each disaster 
emergency, with the exception of the Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County, by another 
sixty (60) months, so that encumbered funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) may 
continue to be used for response and recovery efforts related to these disasters, and so that State 
agencies are able to continue to coordinate and complete billing and reimbursement processes 
with affected local and federal agencies. 
In addition, this Executive Order allocates additional funding to certain disaster 
emergencies: $24,568 to the Gageby Creek Fire; $797,703 to the Mullen Fire; $1,114,579 to the 
East Troublesome Fire; $90,866 to the Calwood Fire; and $82,500 to the Hazardous Materials 
Incident in Pueblo County. These funds are necessary for the fires for additional response, 
recovery, and suppression expenses for these disaster emergencies beyond the initial estimates 
that were available at the time of the disaster emergencies and the initial Executive Orders. The 
additional funding for the Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County covers forensic 
services that exceeded initial estimates. These funds, totaling $2,110,216, are encumbered in the 
DEF. This same amount, $2,110,216, is being unencumbered for the June 2023 Severe Weather 
and Flooding in the DEF, so this Executive Order is budget neutral.  
In sum, this Executive Order amends the duration provisions for funding for eight (8) 
disaster emergencies throughout Colorado from 2020 to 2025 (all disaster emergencies in this 
Executive Order except the Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County), allocates additional 
funds for five (5) disaster emergencies (Gageby Creek Fire, Mullen Fire, East Troublesome Fire, 
Calwood Fire, and the Hazardous Materials Incident in Pueblo County), and unencumbers funds 
that are not needed for the June 2023 Severe Weather and Flooding, making this Executive Order 
budget neutral. The amendments in this Executive Order include the following: 
1. Gageby Creek Fire in Bent County (D 2023 010; $340,000 encumbered from the 
DEF) 
a. Extends duration provision by another sixty (60) months 

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 6 of 9 
 
b. Allocates an additional $24,568, for a total of $364,568 
2. Grizzly Creek and Red Canyon Fire in Garfield and Eagle Counties (D 2020 187 
and D 2023 017; $3,150,000 encumbered from the DEF)

$47,889,784

encumbered in the DEF)

Duration Amendments 
1. Executive Order D 2023 010 (Gageby Creek Fire) is amended so that the duration

(60) months, for a total of ninety-six (96) months from the date of Executive 
Order D 2023 010. Accordingly, the funds will be available until May 16, 2031 

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 7 of 9 
 
2. Executive Order D 2020 187 (Grizzly Creek and Red Canyon Fire), as amended 
by D 2023 017, is amended so that the duration clause indicates that the funds 
described in Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2020 187 (and amended in 
Section II of Executive Order D 2023 017) shall remain available for the 
described purposes for an addition sixty (60) months, for a total of one hundred

Accordingly, the funds will be available until September 9, 2031. 
3. Executive Order D 2020 208 (Cameron Peak Fire), as amended by D 2023 017, is

4. Executive Order D 2020 233 (Mullen Fire), as amended by D 2023 017, is

5. Executive Order D 2020 252 (East Troublesome Fire), as amended by D 2023

7. Executive Order D 2022 004 (Marshall Fire), as amended by D 2022 005, 2022

shall remain available for the described purposes for an additional sixty (60)

months, for a total of one hundred twenty (120) months from the date of

8. Executive Order D 2023 011 (June 2023 Severe Weather and Flooding), as

Accordingly, the funds will be available until July 21, 2031. 
 
Funding Amendments 
1. Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2023 010 (Gageby Creek Fire) is amended to

recovery efforts beyond the estimates that were available at the time of the fire 
and the initial Executive Order, for a total of $364,568 encumbered in the DEF. 
2. Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2020 233 (Mullen Fire), as amended by D 
2023 017, is amended to encumber an additional $797,703 in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, and recovery efforts beyond the estimates that were available at the 
time of the fire and the initial Executive Order, for a total of $1,407,703 
encumbered in the DEF. 
3. Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2020 252 (East Troublesome Fire), as 
amended by D 2023 017, is amended to encumber an additional $1,114,579 in the 
Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional fire suppression, response, 
consequence management, and recovery efforts beyond the estimates that were 
available at the time of the fire and the initial Executive Order, for a total of 
$2,609,579 encumbered in the DEF. 
4. Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2020 253 (Calwood Fire), as amended by D 
2023 017, is amended to encumber an additional $90,866 in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund (DEF) for additional fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, and recovery efforts beyond the estimates that were available at the 
time of the fire and the initial Executive Order, for a total of $3,738,866 
encumbered in the DEF. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 003 
May 12, 2026 
Page 9 of 9 
 
5. Section II(D) of Executive Order D 2023 011 (June 2023 Severe Weather and 
Flooding), as amended by D 2023 019, is amended to unencumber $2,110,216 in 
the Disaster Emergency Fund (DEF), for a total of $47,889,784 encumbered in 
the DEF. Given the widespread nature of this weather event and the number of 
counties involved, a large amount of funds were initially encumbered in the DEF 
to respond to this disaster emergency. The initial encumbrance was necessary due 
to uncertainty regarding reimbursement from the Federal Emergency 
Management Agency (FEMA). As time has passed the State has been able to 
secure additional reimbursements from FEMA. 
6. Section II(C) of Executive Order D 2025 013 (Hazardous Materials Incident in 
Pueblo County) is amended to encumber an additional $82,500 in the Disaster 
Emergency Fund (DEF) for needed forensic services beyond the estimates that 
were available at the time of the incident and the initial Executive Order, for a 
total of $382,500 encumbered in the DEF. 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order. In all other respects, Executive Orders D 2023 010, D 2020 187, D 2020 208, 
D 2020 233, D 2020 252, D 2020 253, D 2023 017, D 2022 004, D 2022 005, D 2022 012, D 
2022 018, and D 2025 001 (pertaining to fires), D 2023 011 and D 2023 019 (pertaining to June 
2023 Severe Weather and Flooding), and D 2025 013 (pertaining to Hazardous Materials 
Incident in Pueblo County), shall remain in full force and effect as originally promulgated. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twelfth  
day of May, 2026. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2026 004
full_text:
D 2026 004 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Enhancing Coloradans’ Access to Healthy Choices 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order enhancing Coloradans’ 
access to healthy choices. 
 
Background and Purpose 
Increasing access to healthy foods and activities is critical to improving Coloradans’ 
health and quality of life and reducing health care costs. Five of the top ten causes of death in 
Colorado—heart disease, cancer, stroke, diabetes, and chronic liver disease—are strongly 
influenced by diet. A healthier diet and regular physical activity can help prevent, delay, and 
manage chronic diseases, while also improving sleep, enhancing immunity, and contributing to 
mental health.    
According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), in 2021, 38.1% of 
Coloradans consumed fruit less than one time per day, and 18.6% of Coloradans consumed 
vegetables less than one time per day. Americans receive 55% of our daily caloric intake from 
ultra-processed foods, including sweetened beverages. According to 2023 CDC data, only 35.4% 
of adults in Colorado met federal physical activity guidelines in the past 30 days.                                  
Colorado has a wealth of opportunities and resources to improve these statistics. 
Colorado agricultural and food producers supply healthy, affordable foods. These resources 
include more than 50 farmers’ markets across the State and more than 200 farms, ranches, and 
roadside stands selling locally sourced ingredients directly to consumers listed in the Colorado 
Department of Agriculture’s Farm Fresh Directory. Our State is also known for outdoor 
recreation and active lifestyles, due to our geography and ethos. The Colorado outdoor recreation 
industry contributed $18.1 billion and 138,000 jobs to the State’s economy in 2024, which helps 
more Coloradans enjoy outdoor activities. State agencies can pursue and implement policies that 
encourage residents to take advantage of these resources and engage in healthy habits.   
With health care costs rising for individuals and families across Colorado, it is more 
critical than ever to focus on prevention. Healthier nutrition and behavior lead to lower health 
and dental insurance premiums for all of us. Through this Executive Order, the State is using a  
 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 2 of 9 
 
whole government approach to promote scientifically supported methods to improve health 
outcomes. 
 
Definitions  
A. 
“Agency” means a State agency, department, or office managed by the governor 
or governor-appointed executives. 
B. 
“Official function” means a meeting, conference, training, or other function that is 
hosted by an agency, attended by guests and/or State employees, held for official 
State business purposes and includes an expenditure of State funds. This 
definition aligns with State Controller policy, dated March 30, 2019. 
C. 
“Official State business purposes” means purposes that further the activities of the 
State and are for the benefit of the State and not personal for individual 
employees. This definition aligns with State Controller policy, dated March 30, 
2019. 
D. 
“Soft drinks” means nonalcoholic beverages that contain natural or artificial 
sweeteners. Soft drinks do not include beverages that contain milk or milk 
products, soy, rice, or similar milk substitutes, or greater than fifty percent of 
vegetable or fruit juice by volume. 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
All Agencies shall work collaboratively with vendors to ensure access to healthy 
options at State facilities, as allowed by existing contracts and resources. 
B. 
An Agency shall not use State funds to purchase alcoholic beverages or soft 
drinks for an official function. This Executive Order does not prohibit an agency’s 
use of State funds to purchase alcoholic beverages or soft drinks to fulfill its 
statutory purpose, including promotion of the State and our business environment 
(such as by the Colorado Wine Board), or a statutory obligation. 
C. 
Agencies may refer to the Food Service Guidelines and the modules and tools in 
the Food@Work platform in implementing the directives below, as relevant and 
appropriate. 
D. 
The Colorado Department of Agriculture (CDA) shall: 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 3 of 9 
 
 
1. Promote and expand its Colorado Proud School Meal Month program and 
increase student, school, and school district participation by 10% statewide 
from 2025 to 2026, furthering statewide access to local agricultural products 
in the month of October. 
 
2. Pursue efforts to lower prices for healthy foods in low-income and 
underserved areas of the State by providing technical assistance to small 
food retailers, farmers, and distributors to fully utilize the expanded 
Community Food Access tax credit and maximize its impact. 
E. 
The Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS) shall: 
1. Continue to advocate for federal approval of Colorado’s application for its 
pending Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) hot foods 
waiver that would allow SNAP participants to use their benefits to purchase 
hot foods at grocery stores, including nutritionally-dense options such as 
rotisserie chicken. 
2. Enhance the geographic diversity of SNAP incentives, such as Double Up 
Food Bucks and Electronic Healthy Incentives Program (eHIP), by engaging 
retailers in all regions of the State, subject to available appropriations. 
3. Improve the Colorado SNAP Bonus program, or Electronic Healthy 
Incentives Program (eHIP), subject to available funding. The program issues 
electronic incentives on SNAP EBT cards to subsidize fruits and vegetables 
purchased with SNAP EBT cards, incentivizing individuals to choose 
healthy foods. 
4. Conduct a robust analysis of the outcomes of Colorado’s SNAP Healthy 
Choice Waiver (Project), including across states with other similar waivers 
if that data is available, if it is federally approved and the State Board of 
Human Services adopts rules implementing the Project. This evaluation will 
include the following information to the extent feasible: 
a) Results of participant surveys regarding: 
(1) Meals and foods eaten outside the home or with foods not 
purchased at SNAP authorized retailers; 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 4 of 9 
(2) Purchase and/or consumption of less healthy or “unhealthy” food 
items not restricted by the Project; 
 
(3) Non-SNAP dollars spent to purchase food items restricted by the 
Project; 
 
(4) SNAP client’s ability and confidence in correctly identifying food 
items that can or cannot be purchased with SNAP benefits during 
the Project; 
(5) When and how the SNAP client became aware of the Project; and 
(6) Any impacts the Project potentially had on participants’ shopping 
routines. 
b) Baseline and monthly data collection for out of State transactions for 
all states that border Colorado. 
c) Baseline and total monthly amount of SNAP dollars spent out of state. 
d) Total monthly amount of SNAP client hearings filed related to the 
Project. 
e) Monthly total number of SNAP-authorized retailer Project 
compliments and complaints received and a qualitative summary. 
f) Monthly total number of SNAP households Project compliments and 
complaints received and a qualitative summary. 
g) Monthly total number of Project compliments and complaints from 
advocacy, community, or retailer association groups and a qualitative 
summary. 
h) Whenever possible, partner with retailers to collect baseline and 
monthly retailer data on SNAP shopping cart transaction data. 
5. Implement a "Healthy Hydration" program across Veterans Community 
Living Centers by integrating accessible, nutrient-infused hydration stations 
and communicating the importance of hydration to residents and visitors. 
These stations provide alternatives to sugary beverages, utilizing fresh fruits 
and herbs to encourage voluntary water consumption. 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 5 of 9 
 
 
6. Report on the rates of obesity, underweight, and healthy weight among 
youth in the custody of the Division of Youth Services to the general public 
on a quarterly basis. 
F. 
The Behavioral Health Administration shall develop a plan to support CDHS, 
CDA, Colorado Department of Education (CDE), and Colorado Department of 
Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) in their statewide outreach to promote 
education on nutrient-dense diets and their impacts on both emotional resilience 
and physical health by July 1, 2026. 
G. 
The Colorado Department of Labor and Employment (CDLE) shall partner with 
CDE to expand the reach of apprenticeship and pre-apprenticeship programs 
related to healthy food occupations with a goal of increasing apprenticeship 
placements by 35% by January 31, 2027, which the Chef Ann Foundation and 
Healthy School Food Pathway programs will support. 
H. 
The Department of Health Care Policy and Financing (HCPF) shall: 
1. Collaborate with the Office of eHealth Innovation to design, fund and 
implement the next generation of the Colorado Social Health Information 
Exchange (CoSHIE) to help Regional Accountable Entities (RAEs) and 
Medicaid providers connect members to community nutrition resources, 
support, and programs, with a target implementation date of 2028. 
2. Provide education and training for Managed Care Entities and RAEs on 
health-related social services twice annually, beginning in July 2026. 
I. 
The Department of Natural Resources (DNR), shall encourage and work in 
collaboration with Colorado Parks and Wildlife (CPW) to: 
1. Promote the health benefits of outdoor activities like hunting, fishing, 
hiking, wildlife photography, cycling and many other forms of outdoor 
recreation and message that hunting and fishing provides a more sustainable 
option for fresh, lean, organic, and healthy sources of protein by sharing fish 
and game recipes across a variety of CPW outlets, including in the Colorado 
Outdoors magazine’s September and October 2026 issues, statewide social 
media accounts, and related hunting and fishing focused eNewsletters, 
beginning in June 2026. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 6 of 9 
 
2. Promote healthy activities like hiking, biking, rafting and other outdoor 
activities that promote physical fitness, a connection to nature, and mental 
well-being through focused and targeted health-related benefits messaging 
in five activity-specific eNewsletter editions beginning in June 2026, 
reaching approximately 450,000 opted-in subscribers and related social 
media posts to over 650,000 followers of CPW accounts. 
J. 
The Department of Personnel & Administration (DPA) shall: 
1. Expand its Bike to Work Day program from one day to a week-long 
challenge to encourage employees to bike or walk to work more frequently 
and coordinate with other State agencies to support the program through 
targeted communications, identifying event volunteers, and conducting 
activities throughout the week. 
2. Improve State employee wellness by expanding the personal exercise 
training support program to include a personal health coach and extend the 
length of personal exercise training support for participating employees 
from three to six months, starting in June 2026. 
3. Provide periodic health and wellbeing coaching groups through the 
Colorado State Employee Assistance Program (CSEAP) to offer connection, 
discussion, information, and support to participants setting and meeting their 
overall health and wellbeing goals. 
K. 
The Colorado Department of Public Health & Environment (CDPHE) shall: 
1. Build on the National Diabetes Prevention Program (DPP) by providing 
technical assistance to maintain the existing DPP network, supporting the 
Community Health Worker (CHW) infrastructure to increase referrals to the 
DPP, and increasing the number of adults in Colorado who enrolled in the 
DPP from 26,362 in 2024 to 31,000 by July 2029 and the number of 
community health workers in the CHW registry from 273 in 2024 to 450 by 
July 2029. 
2. In collaboration with the Colorado Department of Transportation, increase 
interventions for active travel, including walking, biking, scootering, and 
skateboarding, to schools and youth-serving destinations through education, 
policy, practice, and environmental changes, increasing the percentage of 
students with active travel options from 52% in 2023 to 54% in 2029. 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 7 of 9 
 
L. 
The Department of Regulatory Agencies (DORA), Division of Insurance, shall 
evaluate the annual insurance premium savings from healthy lifestyle changes 
such as smoking cessation and communicate their findings by June 30, 2026. 
M. 
The Department of Revenue (DOR) shall implement a Colorado Proud lottery 
scratch ticket celebrating Colorado grown delicious and healthy Palisade peaches, 
Rocky Ford cantaloupe, Olathe sweet corn, and Pueblo chiles with a target launch 
date of September 2026. 
N. 
The Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) shall: 
1. Identify opportunities to expand investments in bike lanes and safe 
pedestrian access. 
2. Prioritize future investments that close gaps in the active transportation 
network and complete an inventory of active transportation projects on 
state-owned facilities throughout Colorado, as part of the Active 
Transportation Plan, by July 31, 2026. 
O. 
The Office of Economic Development and International Trade (OEDIT) shall, in 
alignment with Colorado’s Outdoors Strategy, create a plan for emphasizing 
healthy habits and activities within their Rural Technical Assistance Program 
(RTAP) and Colorado State Outdoor Recreation Grant Program (COSORG), as 
well as the broader activities of the Outdoor Recreation Office, by July 1, 2026. 
This plan should: assess existing metrics about the effectiveness of these 
programs in promoting healthy activities and lifestyles; propose new actions that 
OEDIT can take; and identify any additional metrics which should be used to 
measure success. 
P. 
The Colorado Department of Corrections (CDOC) shall evaluate the daily 
nutritional intake of all inmates to ensure a nourishing and well-balanced diet, 
including healthier alternative options, and add diet and nutrition curriculum to 
courses offered to inmates by July 1, 2026, and report on the rates of obesity, 
underweight, and healthy weight among inmates to the general public on a 
quarterly basis. 
Q. 
The Colorado Department of Early Childhood (CDEC) shall disseminate to 
childcare centers quarterly (every 3 months) recommendations from CDPHE’s 
Colorado Food Program regarding seasonal healthy foods and nutrition, beginning 
on July 1, 2026. 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 8 of 9 
 
R. 
The Colorado Department of Education (CDE) is strongly encouraged to: 
1. Support healthy habits and student well-being by expanding access to 
nutritious school breakfasts and the use of Colorado-grown foods in the 
School Breakfast Program, National School Lunch Program, and Healthy 
School Meals for All Program, beginning in the 2026-2027 school year, 
with the goal of increasing the number of schools offering alternative 
breakfast service models by 10% and the number of school districts that 
spend more than 20% of their total food service budget on local foods and 
feature local products on menus at least once a month. 
2. Support targeted outreach and marketing efforts for healthy foods, including 
building a diverse library of images that represent school meal programs 
across the State, promoting district success stories, marketing school 
nutrition professional development opportunities, elevating partnerships 
with Colorado producers, highlighting the use of local foods and increasing 
awareness of innovative meal service models, beginning in the 2026-2027 
school year. 
3. Through the expansion of the Local Food Program through Healthy School 
Meals for All and the passing of Propositions LL and MM, provide school 
districts access to up to $18 million annually to spend on local foods for 
their school meal programs, support local agriculture, and strengthen student 
voices, beginning in July 2026. 
S. 
The Colorado Department of Higher Education (CDHE) shall coordinate with 
public colleges and universities across the State to increase participation and 
further programs supporting healthy lifestyles, including: 
1. Building a broad, talented, and engaged agricultural and food workforce 
through the national Together We Grow consortium, which works with 
Coloradans, companies, non-governmental organizations, universities, and 
government agencies committed to building a modern food and agricultural 
workforce that reflects the communities in which we live and work, based at 
the CSU Spur campus in Denver; 
2. Increasing enrollment in the Food Studies degree program at Adams State 
University; 
 

Executive Order D 2026 004 
May 21, 2026 
Page 9 of9 
3. Providing plant-based food options, including plant-powered protein 
options, at the University of Colorado Boulder Campus and exploring 
expanding those options to other campuses; 
4. Implementing the Culture of Wellness in Head Start preschools in Denver 
County through the Rocky Mountain Prevention Research Center, Colorado 
School of Public Health, and University of Colorado Anschutz Medical 
Campus; 
5. Increasing awareness of nutrition, gardening, and food preservation classes 
available to Coloradans statewide through Colorado State Extension; 
6. Strengthening statewide food sustainability efforts through beekeeping 
education at Fort Lewis College; and 
7. Reducing food insecurity and furthering mental health initiatives through 
the Wellness Den at the Community College of Aurora. 
IV. 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twenty-first 
day of May, 2026. 
Jared Poli 
Governor

title: D 2026 005
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 005 
May 22, 2026 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2026 005 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Dianne 
Primavera, Lieutenant Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a 
state of disaster emergency due to the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County, enabling State 
and federal agencies to coordinate fire suppression, response, consequence management, 
mitigation. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On April 24, 2026, the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County began near 
Villegreen. The Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County exceeded the capacity of local 
agencies due to elevated fire indices including hot, dry, and windy conditions paired with rugged 
terrain and inaccessible canyons with numerous safety risks including rolling rocks, erratic 
winds, dense vegetation, and active fire behavior with short range spotting. The Poitrey Canyon 
Fire in Las Animas County burned 676 acres on private and state land, threatening 30 residences.  
On April 25, 2026, at approximately 1:08 PM, Governor Polis verbally declared a 
disaster emergency due to the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County, and activated the 
State of Colorado Emergency Operations Plan and directed the Office of Emergency 
Management (OEM) to take all necessary and appropriate State actions to assist the affected 
jurisdictions with fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery 
efforts. 
On May 3, 2026, the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County had burned 2,113 acres 
and was 100% contained. 
 

Executive Order D 2026 005 
May 22, 2026 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County and associated impacts constitute 
a disaster emergency under C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. Governor Polis’s verbal 
order of April 25, 2026, declaring a disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized 
by this Executive Order and shall have the full force and effect of law as if it were 
contained within this Executive Order. 
B. 
Governor Polis’s verbal order of April 25, 2026, activated the State Emergency 
Operations Plan. All State departments and agencies are authorized to take 
whatever actions may be required and requested by the Director or their delegate 
of the OEM, pursuant to the State Emergency Operations Plan, including 
provision of appropriate staff and equipment, as necessary. 
C. 
I direct State agencies to pursue all available federal funding for response and 
recovery operations. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Poitrey Canyon Fire 
in Las Animas County. Therefore, I order that two million seven hundred seventy-
nine thousand dollars ($2,779,000) be transferred from the State Emergency 
Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I encumber two million seven hundred 
seventy-nine thousand dollars ($2,779,000) in the DEF to pay for fire suppression, 
response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to 
the Poitrey Canyon Fire in Las Animas County. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the Director of the OEM is authorized and 
directed to allocate the funding described in Section II(D) to the appropriate 
government agencies and non-profit organizations and execute awards, purchase 
orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds described 
in Section II(D) shall remain available for this purpose until seventy-two (72) 
months from the date of this Executive Order, and any unexpended funds shall 
remain in the DEF. 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 005 
May 22, 2026 
Page 3 of 3 
 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from April 25, 2026, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive 
Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this twenty-second  
day of May, 2026.  
Dianne Primavera 
Lt. Governor, acting on behalf of the State 
while Governor Jared Polis is absent from 
the State.

title: D 2026 006
full_text:
D 2026 006 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
No State Funds Shall be Used for Conversion Therapy 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2 of the Colorado Constitution, I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order directing that no State 
funds shall be used for conversion therapy.  
 
Background and Purpose 
My administration believes in a Colorado for All, where every person - no matter who 
they love or how they identify - can thrive, belong, and be free. Conversion therapy, also referred 
to as “sexual orientation and gender identity change efforts” or “reparative therapy,” is contrary 
to this ethos and threatens the well-being of LGBTQ+ Coloradans, particularly LGBTQ+ youth. 
The State has a vested interest in saving taxpayer money and in protecting LGBTQ+ individuals 
- and state taxpayers - from the promotion and funding of this inhumane treatment.  
Conversion therapy refers to a range of discredited practices with the intended goal of 
changing or discouraging an individual’s sexual orientation or gender identity. According to the 
American Psychiatric Association, these practices are based on the outdated assumption that 
“diverse sexual orientations and gender identities are mental illnesses” and being LGBTQ+ is 
something to be “cured.”  
Research points to the inefficacy and often long-lasting harms associated with conversion 
therapy, particularly among youth, including increased risks of depression, anxiety, and 
suicidality. In Colorado, LGBTQ+ youth who were subjected to or threatened with conversion 
therapy were more than twice as likely to have attempted suicide than their counterparts, 
according to a 2024 survey from the Trevor Project, a non-profit suicide prevention 
organization.  
Medical associations and other professional groups have resoundingly opposed 
conversion therapy. These include the American Academy of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, 
the American Counseling Association, the American Psychiatric Association, the American 
Psychological Association, the National Association of School Psychologists, the American 
Academy of Family Physicians, the American Academy of Nursing, the American Academy of 
Pediatrics, and the American Medical Association, among several others.  
JARED Pous 
GOV1!RNOR 
I. 
136 STATE CAPITOL 
DBNVBR, COLORADO 80203 
TBL 303-866-2471 
FAx 303-866-2003 

Executive Order D 2026 006 
June 1, 2026 
Page 2 of 3 
 
It is critical that Coloradans know their tax dollars are not going to services that are a 
scam and can even be harmful. Through this Executive Order, I am ensuring that funding 
allocated by our State Agencies is spent wisely and used only for evidence-based treatment.  
 
Definitions 
A. “Agency” means a state agency, department, or office managed by the governor 
or governor-appointed executives. 
B. “Sexual orientation and gender identity change efforts” means any practice by a 
licensed mental health professional that seeks to direct a patient toward a 
predetermined sexual orientation or gender identity outcome, or to eliminate or 
reduce sexual or romantic attractions or feelings toward individuals of a particular 
sex or gender, regardless of the sexual orientation or gender identity the patient is 
directed toward.1  
C. “Sexual orientation and gender identity change efforts” does not include: 
1. Counseling or therapy that is neutral with respect to sexual orientation and 
gender identity and that does not seek to direct the patient toward a 
predetermined sexual orientation or gender identity outcome; or 
2. Counseling or therapy related to a patient’s sexual behaviors, practices, or 
relationships, provided that the counseling or therapy does not seek to 
direct the patient toward a predetermined sexual orientation or gender 
identity. 
 
Directives 
A. Each Agency shall take appropriate steps to ensure no State funds are allocated or 
spent by their respective Agencies for sexual orientation and gender identity 
change efforts.  
B. Each Agency shall update any applicable policies, procedures, standards, 
manuals, treatment guidance, and other relevant materials related to health care 
services and coverage, child welfare, and juvenile justice services to implement 
Directive A. 
 
 
 
1 HB26-1322. 
II. 
III. 

IV. 
Duration 
Executive Order D 2026 006 
June 1, 2026 
Page 3 of 3 
This Executive Order shall remain in effect unless modified or rescinded by future 
Executive Order of the Governor. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this 1st 
day of June 2026.

title: D 2026 007
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 007 
June 15, 2026 
Page 1 of 3 
D 2026 007 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Declaring a Disaster Emergency Due to the Sharpe Fire in Baca County 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 2, of the Colorado Constitution and the relevant 
portions of the Colorado Disaster Emergency Act, C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq., I, Jared Polis, 
Governor of the State of Colorado, issue this Executive Order declaring a state of disaster 
emergency due to the Sharpe Fire in Baca County, enabling State and federal agencies to 
coordinate fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery 
efforts. 
 
Background and Purpose 
The Governor is responsible for meeting the dangers to the State and people presented by 
disasters. C.R.S. § 24-33.5-704(1). The Colorado Disaster Emergency Act defines a disaster as 
“the occurrence or imminent threat of widespread or severe damage, injury, or loss of life or 
property resulting from any natural cause or cause of human origin, including but not limited to 
fire.” C.R.S. § 24-33.5-703(3). 
On May 15, 2026, the Sharpe Fire began northwest of Boise City, Oklahoma in Cimarron 
County. The Sharpe Fire burned approximately 10,074 acres for two days before crossing into 
Baca County, Colorado on May 17. Evacuation orders were issued for the Town of Campo as 
well as rural residences in Baca County. The Sharpe Fire burned on two sides of the Town of 
Campo and came within a few hundred yards of the Town itself.  
On May 17, 2026, at approximately 5:06 PM, I verbally declared a disaster emergency 
due to the Sharpe Fire in Baca County, and activated the State of Colorado Emergency 
Operations Plan and directed the Office of Emergency Management (OEM) to take all necessary 
and appropriate State actions to assist the affected jurisdictions with their fire suppression, 
response, consequence management, mitigation, and recovery efforts. 
As of May 21, 2026, the Sharpe Fire burned 29,209 acres in Baca County and was 100% 
contained. 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 007 
June 15, 2026 
Page 2 of 3 
 
 
Declarations and Directives 
A. 
The Sharpe Fire and associated impacts constitute a disaster emergency under 
C.R.S. § 24-33.5-701, et seq. My verbal order of May 17, 2026, declaring a 
disaster emergency, is hereby memorialized by this Executive Order and shall 
have the full force and effect of law as if it were contained within this Executive 
Order. 
B. 
My verbal order of May 17, 2026. activated the State Emergency Operations Plan. 
All State departments and agencies are authorized to take whatever actions may 
be required and requested by the OEM Director or their delegate pursuant to the 
State Emergency Operations Plan, including provision of appropriate staff and 
equipment, as necessary. 
C. 
I direct State agencies to pursue all available federal funding for response and 
recovery operations. 
D. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-706(4)(b), funds in the Disaster Emergency Fund 
(DEF) are insufficient to pay for fire suppression, response, consequence 
management, mitigation, and recovery efforts related to the Sharpe Fire. 
Therefore, I order that two hundred fifty-nine thousand dollars ($259,000) be 
transferred from the State Emergency Reserve Cash Fund (SERCF) to the DEF. I 
encumber two hundred fifty-nine thousand dollars ($259,000) in the DEF to pay 
for fire suppression, response, consequence management, mitigation, and 
recovery efforts related to the Sharpe Fire. 
E. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 24-33.5-705.4(5), the OEM Director is authorized and 
directed to allocate the funding described in Section II(D) to the appropriate 
government agencies and nonprofit organizations and execute awards, purchase 
orders, or other mechanisms to effect the allocation of funds. The funds described 
in Section II(D) above shall remain available for this purpose until seventy-two 
(72) months from the date of this Executive Order, and any unexpended funds 
shall remain in the DEF. 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 007 
June 15, 2026 
Page 3 of 3 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from May 17, 2026, unless extended 
further by Executive Order, except that the funds described in Section II(D) above shall remain 
available for the described purposes for seventy-two (72) months from the date of this Executive 
Order. 
 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this fifteenth  
day of June, 2026. 
Jared Polis 
Governor

title: D 2026 008
full_text:
Executive Order D 2026 008 
June 16, 2026 
Page 1 of 2 
D 2026 008 
EXECUTIVE ORDER 
Activating the Colorado National Guard to Assist with  
Election Cybersecurity Defense Efforts 
Pursuant to the authority vested in the Governor of the State of Colorado and, in 
particular, pursuant to Article IV, Section 5 of the Colorado Constitution and C.R.S. § 28-3-104, 
et seq., I, Jared Polis, Governor of the State of Colorado, hereby issue this Executive Order 
activating no less than four (4) and up to six (6) unarmed members of the Colorado National 
Guard Task Force Cyber to assist with election cybersecurity defense efforts during the 2026 
Primary Election. 
 
Background and Purpose 
Like many states, Colorado uses online voter registration systems that allow eligible 
citizens to access and update their voter registration information. These systems are protected by 
various security features that maintain confidentiality and prevent data tampering or interference 
with data. 
Even with such security features, online voter registration systems could provide an 
avenue for cyber actors to gain unlawful access to voter registration databases. Outside of 
Colorado, voter registration databases have been compromised by cyber actors hacking into 
various systems. While cyber actors are unable to modify voter records, breaches could result in 
the release of voters’ personally identifiable information. The exposure of voters’ personally 
identifiable information does not threaten the integrity of our State elections, but it could 
undermine public confidence in the system and suppress voter registration. The Colorado 
National Guard Task Force Cyber has unique cyber skills and capabilities to assist in protecting 
critical election infrastructure. 
The Colorado Secretary of State has requested that the Colorado National Guard Task 
Force Cyber provide election cybersecurity support on June 16, 2026, and extending for up to 
thirty (30) days, for the 2026 Primary Election. By activating no less than four (4) and up to six 
(6) unarmed members of the Colorado National Guard Task Force Cyber, the National Guard 
will support the Secretary of State with election cybersecurity defense efforts up to and including 
the 2026 Primary Election on June 30, 2026. 
 
 

Executive Order D 2026 008 
June 16, 2026 
Page 2 of 2 
 
 
Declarations and Directives 
On June 9, 2026, the Colorado Secretary of State requested support from the Colorado 
National Guard Task Force Cyber for the 2026 Primary Election occurring on June 30, 2026. 
Pursuant to C.R.S. § 28-3-104, the Governor may employ the National Guard for the defense of 
the State. I hereby activate no less than four (4) and up to six (6) unarmed members of the 
Colorado National Guard Task Force Cyber to participate in training activities and assist with 
election cybersecurity defense efforts on June 16, 2026, and extending for up to thirty (30) days, 
for the 2026 Primary Election. 
 
Duration 
This Executive Order shall expire thirty (30) days from June 16, 2026, unless extended 
further by Executive Order. 
GIVEN under my hand and the 
Executive Seal of the State of 
Colorado this sixteenth  
day of June, 2026. 
Jared Polis 
Governor
